docs.title: Exploration Synthesis
docs.text:
The one document the analysis agent reads first.

Date: 2026-04-05 Exploration tasks: T01-T26 complete (23 reports, 4 gate memos) Input data: data/unified.parquet (99 columns, 1,395,790 rows)

docs.title: RQ1: SWE labor demand restructuring
docs.text: Dimension Quality Notes Junior share trend Good (with caveats) 5,019 arshkon + 24,095 scraped SWE. Entry-level trend direction is robust using seniority_native (arshkon-only 2024 baseline: 22.3% to 14.0%). CRITICAL CAVEAT: direction may reverse with seniority_3level in the overlap panel (T16 surprise). seniority_llm (planned) will resolve. AI requirements Strong Binary keyword detection on full description is robust across all sensitivity checks. Calibration ratio 5-17x (T08). Not aspirational (T22). Scope inflation Moderate (corrected) Management indicator corrected from +31pp to +4-10pp (T22). Must use strict/validated pattern set. Text quality asymmetry: 2024 has LLM-cleaned text, 2026 does not -- inflates apparent change. Domain recomposition Strong T09 clustering is method-robust (ARI >= 0.996). ML/AI archetype 4% to 27% is the largest structural change. Within-firm vs compositional Strong T16 shift-share decomposition: 57% compositional, 43% within-firm. 451-company overlap panel.

docs.title: RQ2: Technology ecosystem evolution
docs.text: Dimension Quality Notes Technology mention rates Strong 146-tech regex dictionary on full description. Within-2024 calibration validates AI signals (5-17x noise). Co-occurrence networks Strong Phi-coefficient networks with Louvain clustering. 2024 and 2026 networks both have >460 edges. Stack diversity Strong Mean techs/posting 6.2 to 8.3. Robust to aggregator exclusion and company capping. AI additive pattern Strong AI-mentioning postings: 11.4 techs vs 7.3 non-AI. Clear and large.

docs.title: RQ3: Employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence
docs.text: Dimension Quality Notes Posting-side AI rates Strong Well-measured at ~41% (2026). Usage-side benchmarks Moderate External surveys (StackOverflow, JetBrains, GitHub) with different sampling frames. 2026 estimate (75%) is extrapolated. Divergence computation Moderate Direction is clear (posting lags usage). Exact gap depends on benchmark choice. Aspiration assessment Strong T22 hedge-fraction method validated. AI is less hedged than traditional requirements.

docs.title: RQ4: Mechanisms (qualitative)
docs.text: Dimension Quality Notes Interview artifacts Ready T25 produced 6 artifacts with corrected findings. Three interview themes prioritized. Stimuli grounded in data Strong JD pairs, company panel, and visualizations all derived from the parquet data.

docs.title: Primary SWE sample
docs.text:
SELECT * FROM read_parquet('data/unified.parquet')
WHERE source_platform = 'linkedin'
  AND is_english = true
  AND date_flag = 'ok'
  AND is_swe = true
-- Yields: ~52,327 rows (arshkon 5,019 + asaniczka 23,213 + scraped 24,095)

docs.title: Entry-level analysis (arshkon-only 2024 baseline)
docs.text:
-- 2024 entry: arshkon ONLY (asaniczka has zero native entry labels)
SELECT * FROM <primary_sample>
WHERE source = 'kaggle_arshkon' AND seniority_native = 'entry'
-- Yields: ~769 rows (seniority_native) or ~830 rows (seniority_final)

-- 2026 entry:
SELECT * FROM <primary_sample>
WHERE source = 'scraped' AND seniority_native = 'entry'
-- Yields: ~2,789 rows (seniority_native) or ~3,255 rows (seniority_final)

docs.title: Cross-occupation DiD sample
docs.text:
SELECT * FROM read_parquet('data/unified.parquet')
WHERE source_platform = 'linkedin'
  AND is_english = true
  AND date_flag = 'ok'
  AND (is_swe = true OR is_swe_adjacent = true OR is_control = true)
-- Yields: ~214K rows (SWE ~52K + adjacent ~20K + control ~142K)

docs.title: Company overlap panel
docs.text:
-- Companies with >= 3 SWE in both eras (per T16 methodology)
-- Pooling 2024-01 + 2024-04 as "2024"; 2026-03 as "2026"
-- Yields: ~451 companies, ~22,929 postings (44% of all SWE)

docs.title: Text analysis recommendations
docs.text: Use case Column Coverage Notes Binary keyword presence description (full text) 100% all sources Best recall; includes boilerplate Density/frequency metrics description_core_llm > description_core LLM: 99% arshkon, 81% asaniczka, 0% scraped; Core: 100% all LLM-cleaned preferred but creates asymmetry Uniform cross-period text description_core 100% all sources Lower quality (~44% boilerplate accuracy) but consistent Embeddings (pre-computed) all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim Available for SWE sample Used in T09, T15

docs.title: Ablation framework (in priority order)
docs.text: Priority Column When to use Coverage Notes 1 seniority_llm After Stage 10 budget allocated Currently 0% (all null) Explicit-signal-only LLM classification. Will be the canonical column. 2 seniority_native Trend estimation (primary) arshkon 69%, asaniczka 100%*, scraped 83% Cleanest platform labels. *Asaniczka has only mid-senior/associate. 3 seniority_final Cross-sectional analysis (best coverage) arshkon 81%, asaniczka 100%, scraped 93% Merges imputed + native backfill. 4 seniority_3level Coarse analyses Same as seniority_final junior/mid/senior/unknown. Associate standalone unusable (9% accuracy). 5 seniority_imputed Never as primary arshkon 44%, asaniczka 69%, scraped 54% Severe mid-senior bias. Only 21-42% entry-level accuracy.

docs.title: Critical rule: Exclude asaniczka from ALL seniority-stratified analyses
docs.text: Asaniczka has zero native entry-level labels. Its seniority_final entry rate (0.6%) is a title-keyword imputation artifact. Including asaniczka in seniority trends creates false precision and potentially reverses the direction of entry-level findings.

docs.title: The operationalization discrepancy
docs.text:
The direction of the entry-level trend depends on column choice: - seniority_native (arshkon-only 2024): 22.3% to 14.0% = DECLINE of 8.3pp - seniority_final (arshkon 2024): 20.4% to 14.5% = DECLINE of 5.9pp - seniority_3level in T16 overlap panel: appeared to show INCREASE (3.4% to 13.5%)

The T16 increase is likely driven by different operationalization and panel composition (pooled 2024 includes asaniczka). Resolution requires seniority_llm.

Recommendation for analysis: Use seniority_native as primary for entry-level trends (arshkon-only baseline). Report seniority_final as robustness. Flag the discrepancy explicitly and note that seniority_llm will resolve it.

docs.title: Severity: HIGH
docs.text: Confounder Description Affected analyses Mitigation Description length growth 57-67% median growth from 2024 to 2026. Cohen's d = 0.77 -- the single largest effect. All text-based metrics Length-normalize (per 1K chars). Use binary presence for keyword analyses. Asaniczka seniority gap Zero native entry-level labels. 0.6% entry rate is imputation artifact. Any seniority-stratified analysis including asaniczka Exclude asaniczka from seniority analyses. Use arshkon-only as 2024 entry baseline. Company composition 57% of aggregate change is compositional (T16). Only 18% company overlap between periods. All aggregate cross-period comparisons Use within-company panel design (451 companies) as primary. Report aggregate as robustness. Text quality asymmetry 2024 Kaggle data has LLM-cleaned text (80-99% SWE coverage). 2026 scraped has 0%. Rule-based cleaning retains more boilerplate in 2026. All text-based indicators, especially management/scope patterns Use description_core uniformly for cross-period comparisons. When LLM-cleaned scraped becomes available, re-run.

docs.title: Severity: MODERATE
docs.text: Confounder Description Affected analyses Mitigation Field-wide posting evolution Management, leadership, soft skills expansion is NOT SWE-specific (T18 DiD ~0). Scope inflation claims Use cross-occupation DiD to isolate SWE-specific effects. Only claim SWE-specificity for metrics with large positive DiD. Aggregator contamination Aggregators are 12-27% of SWE postings by source. Different seniority profiles (higher entry rates). Seniority distribution, text patterns Run all analyses with and without aggregators. Report both. Measurement error in keyword indicators T22 showed "leading" was 99.4% adjective usage, not management. Broad pattern sets capture boilerplate language. Any regex-based indicator, especially management/scope Use validated/strict pattern sets only. Document pattern validation. Seniority operationalization Entry-level trend direction depends on column choice (see Section 3). RQ1 entry-level findings Use seniority_native as primary; seniority_final as robustness. Await seniority_llm.

docs.title: Severity: LOW
docs.text: Confounder Description Affected analyses Mitigation SWE classification noise 4-6% false positive rate; QA/test engineer boundary unstable. SWE sample definition Report sensitivity with regex-tier-only (highest precision). Geographic sampling design Scraped data uses 26-metro targeted search; Kaggle is national. Geographic comparisons Use metro-level fixed effects. All 26 metros show same direction (T17). Scraper first-day backlog 1.6x normal volume on first scrape day (Mar 20). Within-March temporal analyses Mild; daily metric CVs < 10% (T19).

docs.title: Confirmed (with caveats)
docs.text: Finding Evidence Key caveat AI requirements surged, SWE-specific 8% to 33% (broad) or 3.9% to 27.5% (entry). DiD vs control: +24.4pp. Validated as genuine, not aspirational (T22). Binary keyword detection; specific-tool rates lower (LLM 10%, Copilot 4%, Claude 4%) Junior share declined, SWE-specific seniority_native: 22.3% to 14.0% (-8.3pp). DiD vs control: -24.9pp. Control junior share INCREASED. Direction depends on seniority operationalization. Thin arshkon-only 2024 baseline (769 native entry). Domain recomposition ML/AI archetype 4% to 27% (+22pp). Frontend/Web 41% to 24% (-17pp). Domain NMI 10x seniority NMI. Text source sensitivity (LLM vs rule-based): cosine similarity 0.88, not perfect. AI is additive to stacks AI-mentioning postings: 11.4 techs vs 7.3. Stack diversity increased 6.2 to 8.3. New 25-tech AI community emerged (T14). Aggregators slightly inflate non-AI counts; pattern robust to exclusion. YOE slot purification 5+ YOE entry-level: 22.8% to 2.4%. Median entry YOE: 3.0 to 2.0. Confirmed across 5 independent decompositions (T08). Not yet tested in cross-occupation DiD. GenAI accelerated 8.3x Within-2024 rate: +1.2pp/yr. Cross-period rate: +10.2pp/yr. Consistent with model release cascade. 3-point estimation is fragile; acceleration ratio depends on within-2024 baseline quality.

docs.title: Corrected
docs.text: Finding Original claim Corrected to Source Management indicator +31pp at entry level (T11) +4-10pp using validated patterns (T22) "Leading" was 99.4% adjective; "cross-functional" was 84% collaboration; "leadership" was 77% boilerplate Soft skills expansion +16pp at entry level (T11) SWE grew LESS than control (DiD = -5.1pp, T18) Field-wide trend, not SWE-specific

docs.title: Contradicted
docs.text: Hypothesis Evidence against Implication Management migrated from senior to entry Expanded at ALL levels (T21). Entry +31pp, mid-senior +27pp, director +7pp (narrow metric). Director density actually fell -23%. Universal template expansion, not seniority-specific migration. Junior-senior semantic convergence Within-2024 calibration shift exceeds cross-period change (T15). Base-rate-adjusted NN analysis shows only +6.3pp excess. Convergence claim not robust. Use specific requirement-level signals (T11) instead. Requirements outpace usage Requirements LAG usage: ~41% posting vs ~75% developer usage (T23). Gap narrowed from -45pp to -34pp. Inverts RQ3 direction. Employers are catching up, not inflating beyond reality.

docs.title: New findings (not in original design)
docs.text: Finding Evidence Significance AI-entry orthogonality Firm-level r = -0.07 (p=0.138, T16). Metro-level r = -0.04 (p=0.850, T17). The paper CANNOT claim AI caused junior elimination at the firm level. These are parallel market trends. 57% of aggregate change is compositional T16 shift-share decomposition. New entrants have 24.3% AI (vs 2.5% for exits). More than half of what we observe reflects different companies posting, not same companies changing. Associate collapsing toward entry Relative position in feature space: 0.30 to 0.16 (T20). Feature profiles nearly identical. De facto 3-tier seniority system emerging (junior, mid, senior). Director/mid-senior boundary blurred AUC dropped 0.75 to 0.64 (T20). Management density gap: 0.62 to 0.09. Director increasingly indistinguishable from mid-senior on structured features. AI requirements genuine, not aspirational Hedge fraction reversed: AI was more aspirational in 2024 (40% vs 34%), less aspirational in 2026 (20% vs 30%). (T22) AI listing requirements represent real hiring bars, strengthening AI surge finding.

docs.title: 6. Posting Archetype Summary (T09)
docs.text:
14 BERTopic archetypes reduced from 92 fine-grained topics. Four macro-archetypes account for 81% of non-noise postings:

Archetype 2024 share 2026 share Change Character Frontend/Web 41% 24% -17pp JavaScript, React, web applications Embedded/Systems 26% 19% -7pp Test, systems, hardware-adjacent Data/Analytics 22% 21% -1pp (stable) Cloud, AWS, Azure, data pipelines ML/AI Engineering 4% 27% +22pp AI, building, ML, models

Seniority NMI = 0.018 (weakest signal). Domain NMI = 0.175 (strongest). A senior data engineer posting is more similar to a junior data engineer posting than to a senior frontend posting.

Implication for analysis: All seniority-stratified analyses should also be domain-stratified. Pooled junior-vs-senior comparisons mask domain-specific dynamics.

docs.title: 7. Technology Evolution Summary (T14)
docs.text:
• 61 technologies rising, 12 declining, 73 stable (FDR-corrected).
  • Top risers: CI/CD +16.3pp, Python +15.4pp, LLM +12.8pp, agent frameworks +10.3pp, generative AI +8.8pp, ML +8.3pp.
  • Top decliners: Agile -4.8pp, SQL -3.8pp, HTML/CSS -3.6pp, Linux -2.1pp, .NET -1.8pp.
  • Python became majority: 34.6% to 50.1%, the largest absolute gain among traditional technologies.
  • New AI ecosystem: LangChain, RAG, vector DBs, agent frameworks emerged from near-zero.
  • AI is additive: AI-mentioning postings require 11.4 techs vs 7.3 for non-AI.
  • Stack diversity: 6.2 to 8.3 mean techs/posting (robust to all sensitivity checks).

docs.title: 8. Geographic Summary (T17)
docs.text:
• All 26 metros show positive changes on all five metrics (entry share, AI prevalence, org scope, description length, tech diversity). No counter-trend metro.
  • Tech hubs vs non-hubs: No significant differences (all p > 0.08). Non-hubs change as much as hubs. The transformation is nationally uniform.
  • AI-entry correlation at metro level: r = -0.039, p = 0.850 (null). Reinforces firm-level orthogonality finding.
  • CV of changes: Description length most uniform (CV=0.24); entry share most variable (CV=0.40) but all positive.
  • Remote work: 22.6% of 2026 scraped SWE postings are remote-tagged but invisible to metro analysis (no metro assignment). Cross-period remote comparison impossible (2024 = 0% by data artifact).

docs.title: Key finding: Management expanded everywhere; orchestration is the real senior shift
docs.text:
Management language did NOT migrate from senior to entry. It expanded at ALL levels: - Entry: 9.5% to 40.8% (+31pp narrow metric; corrected to +4-10pp with validated patterns) - Mid-senior: 23.1% to 49.9% (+27pp) - Director: 47.8% to 54.5% (+7pp binary; but DENSITY fell -23%)

The distinctive senior change is technical orchestration surging: +16% mid-senior, +46% director. AI-mentioning senior postings have HIGHER orchestration and LOWER management density than non-AI senior postings. The gap widened from 2024 to 2026.

docs.title: Senior sub-archetypes (k=4 clustering)
docs.text: Cluster Share AI rate Key trait Low-touch Generalist 58% (stable) 12% Standard senior SWE People Manager 22% (+2.5pp) 11% Team management, mentoring, hiring Technical Orchestrator 6% (+1.3pp) 18% Code review, system design, AI workflows (highest AI rate) Strategic Leader 14% (-3.3pp) 13% Stakeholder, roadmap, budget (declining)

docs.title: Management indicator: Measurement error corrected
docs.text:
The T11 headline (+31pp management at entry) was inflated by three problematic patterns: - \bleading\b: 33% of 2026 entry postings matched, but 99.4% were adjective usage ("a leading company") - \bcross-functional\b: 19% matched, 84% were collaboration not management - \bleadership\b: 12% matched, 77% were company boilerplate

Corrected rates (strict validated patterns): 24.6-30.1% (2024) to 34.3% (2026) = +4-10pp.

docs.title: AI requirements: Genuine, not aspirational
docs.text:
Period AI hedge fraction Non-AI hedge fraction 2024-01 40.2% 34.2% 2024-04 30.9% 21.9% 2026-03 20.0% 30.0%

AI requirements REVERSED from more-aspirational-than-average (2024) to less-aspirational-than-average (2026). This is strong evidence that AI listing requirements are real hiring bars.

docs.title: Text quality asymmetry warning
docs.text: LLM-cleaned text reduces 2024 management indicator rates by ~15pp compared to rule-based text. 2026 scraped data has no LLM-cleaned text. This asymmetry systematically inflates the apparent 2024-to-2026 change for ANY boilerplate-sensitive indicator. When LLM-cleaned 2026 text becomes available, the 2026 management rate will likely drop further.

docs.title: 11. New Hypotheses from T24
docs.text:
Eight new hypotheses generated, of which the most testable with existing data:

  1. H1: Domain recomposition drives junior decline. ML/AI has lower entry share AND grew from 4% to 27%. The compositional shift alone could account for much of the aggregate decline. Test: within-domain entry share decomposition.

  2. H4: Two-speed market. AI-native firms (new entrants) vs legacy transformation firms. Different companies drive the compositional (57%) and behavioral (43%) components.

  3. H7: GenAI as domain-boundary dissolving force. AI-mentioning postings may show higher cross-domain similarity, blurring traditional archetype boundaries.

The orthogonality puzzle (AI and entry changes parallel but uncorrelated within firms) is the most important open question for both analysis and interviews.

docs.title: Recommended statistical methods by RQ
docs.text: RQ Primary method Secondary / robustness RQ1 (restructuring) Cross-occupation DiD (SWE vs control, by metric) Within-firm panel FE; shift-share decomposition; domain-stratified entry share RQ1b (within vs compositional) Shift-share decomposition (T16 method) Company-level regression with FE RQ2 (technology) Technology co-occurrence network analysis; stack diversity regression NMF topic modeling; within-2024 calibration for all tech trends RQ3 (divergence) Benchmark comparison with confidence intervals Aspiration-ratio within postings; specificity gradient (generic vs tool-specific) RQ4 (mechanisms) Reflexive thematic analysis on semi-structured interviews JD-stimulated recall using T25 artifacts

docs.title: Recommended sensitivity framework
docs.text: Every finding should be reported with the following checks: 1. (a) Aggregator exclusion -- removes 12-27% of sample by source 2. (b) Company capping -- max 10-20 per company to prevent template dominance 3. (c) Seniority operationalization -- seniority_native vs seniority_final vs seniority_3level 4. (f) Within-2024 calibration -- arshkon vs asaniczka as noise floor 5. (g) SWE classification tier -- regex-only vs full sample

docs.title: Analysis sequencing
docs.text:
1. First: Run the domain-stratified entry share decomposition (H1). This may reframe the entire junior decline story.
  2. Second: Reproduce T18 DiD with corrected management indicators. Confirm SWE-specificity of AI and entry findings with the strict pattern set.
  3. Third: Formal robustness tables for each core finding (AI surge, junior decline, domain recomposition, AI additive, slot purification).
  4. Fourth: Within-firm panel analysis with company FE for the 451 overlap companies.
  5. Fifth: Geographic heterogeneity analysis with metro FE.

docs.title: Findings requiring formal robustness checks
docs.text: Finding Required sensitivities Notes Junior share decline (a)(b)(c)(f)(g) + domain stratification The most specification-sensitive finding. Must show robustness across seniority operationalizations. AI requirements surge (a)(b)(f)(g) Already well-validated; formal tables needed for paper. Domain recomposition Text source (LLM vs rule-based), method (BERTopic vs NMF), sample (full vs random) T09 showed 0.88 cosine similarity between LLM and rule-based cluster distributions. Management indicator (corrected) Pattern set specification (strict vs moderate), text source (description vs description_core) Must use T22 validated patterns ONLY. AI-entry orthogonality Panel size threshold (min 3 vs 5 vs 10), seniority operationalization, with/without aggregators A negative result -- must show it doesn't appear under any specification. 57% compositional Panel definition (overlap threshold), era pooling (separate 2024-01/2024-04 vs pooled) Critical for framing; should be robust to reasonable alternatives.

docs.title: seniority_llm (Stage 10 budget allocation)
docs.text:
What it provides: Explicit-signal-only seniority classification for all SWE rows. Will NOT infer seniority from responsibilities (which would circularly contaminate the analysis of how responsibilities differ by seniority).

What changes when available: - Resolves the seniority operationalization discrepancy (Section 3) - Provides a consistent classification across all three sources (including asaniczka) - Enables asaniczka to participate in seniority-stratified analyses for the first time - Expected to settle the entry-level trend direction definitively

Priority: HIGHEST. This is the binding constraint on RQ1.

docs.title: description_core_llm for scraped data (Stage 9 budget allocation)
docs.text:
What it provides: LLM-quality boilerplate removal for 2026 scraped data (currently 0% coverage).

What changes when available: - Eliminates the text quality asymmetry between 2024 and 2026 - The 2026 management indicator rate will likely DROP further (T22 showed 15pp difference between LLM and rule-based text for 2024) - All text density metrics become comparable across periods - Enables reliable description_core_llm-based analyses on scraped data

Priority: HIGH. Second after seniority_llm.

docs.title: Priority 1: The orthogonality puzzle
docs.text:
Prompt: "AI adoption surged and junior hiring declined simultaneously at the market level, but these trends are completely uncorrelated within individual companies. Companies that adopted AI the most did NOT cut junior roles. What explains this?"

Why this matters: This finding challenges the dominant "AI replaces junior developers" narrative. Practitioner explanations will help distinguish between: (a) separate mechanisms operating in parallel, (b) market-level equilibrium effects, (c) time-lag causation our snapshots cannot capture, or (d) the trends being genuinely independent.

docs.title: Priority 2: Are AI requirements real hiring bars or signaling?
docs.text:
Prompt: "41% of SWE postings now mention AI requirements, and they're phrased more firmly than traditional requirements. But 75% of developers already use AI. Is listing 'LLM experience' on a posting a real filter, or is it signaling organizational modernity?"

Why this matters: T22 showed AI requirements are less hedged than traditional ones. But whether they function as actual hiring screens vs recruitment signaling is a behavioral question the data cannot answer.

docs.title: Priority 3: The domain recomposition experience
docs.text:
Prompt: "ML/AI engineering grew from 4% to 27% of SWE postings in two years. Frontend/Web contracted from 41% to 24%. How are you experiencing this shift? Are existing teams retraining? Are new hires arriving with different skills? Is the shift in posting labels matching a shift in actual work?"

Why this matters: The domain recomposition is the largest structural change in the data. Whether it represents genuine new work or cosmetic relabeling is a critical interpretation question.

docs.title: 16. The Paper's Strongest Story
docs.text:
Based on 23 exploration tasks and 4 gate reviews, the evidence supports this narrative:

The SWE labor market restructured between 2024 and 2026 through three SWE-specific mechanisms operating in parallel:

  1. AI competency requirements surged (+24pp DiD vs control), validated as genuine hiring requirements (not aspirational). Requirements still lag developer usage but are converging rapidly.

  2. Entry-level SWE posting share declined while non-SWE junior share increased (DiD = -25pp). But AI adoption and entry-level changes are orthogonal at the firm level (r ~ 0) -- these are parallel market trends, not causally linked within organizations. Over half (57%) of aggregate change is compositional.

  3. The SWE domain landscape recomposed: ML/AI engineering grew from 4% to 27% of postings while frontend/web contracted. Domain is 10x more structurally important than seniority in determining posting content.

What the paper should NOT claim: - That AI caused junior elimination within firms - That management scope inflation is dramatic (+4-10pp, not +31pp) or SWE-specific (field-wide) - That junior and senior postings are semantically converging (fails calibration) - That posting requirements outpace developer usage (they lag) - That soft skills expansion is SWE-specific (SWE grew LESS than control)

What requires caveats: - Entry-level trend direction depends on seniority operationalization (planned LLM labels will resolve) - Text quality asymmetry between 2024 (LLM-cleaned) and 2026 (rule-based) inflates text-based indicators - 57% of aggregate change is compositional -- the "restructuring" is partly about which companies post, not just how they post - The corrected management indicator (+4-10pp) still uses rule-based text for 2026; will likely drop further with LLM cleaning

docs.title: Appendix: Task Completion Summary
docs.text: Task Wave Key finding T01 1 28 cols >50% null; LLM cols null; core analysis columns viable T02 1 Asaniczka associate NOT junior proxy; 830 arshkon entry-level baseline T03 1 Junior decline 5.9-8.3pp robust across 4 operationalizations T04 1 SWE classification 4-6% FP, <0.5% FN T05 1 Most cross-dataset diffs are artifacts; seniority is the exception T06 1 Within-company entry decline -11.8pp (stronger than aggregate) T07 1 Well-powered; r>0.97 geographic representativeness T08 2 YOE slot purification (5+ YOE entry: 22.8% to 2.4%); AI tech 5-17x noise T09 2 Domain > seniority (10x); ML/AI 4% to 27% T10 2 AI titles 4x; Staff tripled; titles stable in meaning T11 2 Scope inflation real but management CORRECTED by T22 to +4-10pp T12 2 AI dominant text signal; most 2026-distinctive terms are boilerplate T13 2 93.9% of length growth is core content, not boilerplate T14 2 61 rising techs; AI additive; new AI community; stack diversity +2.1 T15 2 Convergence fails calibration; 7/9 findings representation-robust T16 3 57% compositional; AI-entry null (r=-0.07); 4 company clusters T17 3 All metros same direction; hub/non-hub null; AI-entry null at metro T18 3 SWE-SPECIFIC CONFIRMED: AI DiD +24pp, junior DiD -25pp; mgmt/soft field-wide T19 3 GenAI accelerated 8.3x; within-March stable (CV<10%) T20 3 Boundaries asymmetric: entry/assoc sharp, dir/midsen blurred; associate collapsing toward entry T21 3 Management migration REJECTED; expanded everywhere; orchestration +46% at director T22 3 Management indicator CORRECTED +4-10pp; AI NOT aspirational (20% vs 30% hedge) T23 3 Requirements LAG usage (~41% vs ~75%); gap narrowing; AI-as-domain may overshoot T24 4 8 new hypotheses; H1 (domain drives junior decline) most testable T25 4 6 interview artifacts; 3 priority themes T26 4 This synthesis

docs.title: T01: Column Coverage Audit
docs.text: Data source: data/unified.parquet (Stage 11 final output, 99 columns, 1,395,790 rows) Generated: 2026-04-05 Filters: SWE subset = source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Of the 99 columns in data/unified.parquet, 28 columns are >50% null in at least one SWE source. Most are expected source-specific metadata gaps, but analysis-critical gaps include company_industry (0% for asaniczka), company_size (0% for asaniczka and scraped), skills_raw (0% for scraped), and description_core_llm (0% for scraped). All Stage 10 LLM classification columns are entirely null across all sources (no budget allocated). Core rule-based analysis columns have adequate coverage for cross-period work, but text analyses must use description_core (rule-based, ~44% accuracy) rather than the higher-quality description_core_llm for any analysis involving scraped data.

docs.text: Source Platform Total Rows SWE Filtered kaggle_arshkon linkedin 118,100 5,019 kaggle_asaniczka linkedin 1,058,779 23,213 scraped linkedin 145,583 24,095 scraped indeed 73,328 (excluded from SWE filter) Total 1,395,790 52,327

docs.title: 2. Coverage Heatmap

See exploration/figures/T01/coverage_heatmap_all.png (all LinkedIn rows) and coverage_heatmap_swe.png (SWE filtered).

The heatmaps show non-null percentage for every column by source, sorted by mean coverage (lowest on top). Key visual patterns:

  • Top band (low coverage): LLM classification columns, source-specific metadata, and company_size form a red band across the top.
  • Middle band (partial coverage): company_industry, description_core_llm, date_posted, metro_area, and yoe_extracted show source-dependent gaps.
  • Bottom band (full coverage): Core identity, pipeline-derived, and quality-flag columns are green (100%) across all sources.

docs.text:
Source Coverage Status Non-empty Empty string Null Total arshkon labeled 4,981 11 23 5,015 arshkon skipped_short 0 4 0 4 asaniczka labeled 18,834 46 15 18,895 asaniczka deferred 0 0 4,316 4,316 asaniczka skipped_short 0 2 0 2 scraped deferred 0 0 24,095 24,095

Key finding: LLM-cleaned text is available for 99.2% of arshkon SWE and 81.1% of asaniczka SWE, but 0% of scraped SWE. The 4,316 deferred asaniczka rows represent budget exhaustion during Stage 9. All scraped rows were deferred (no extraction budget allocated for scraped data).

llm_classification_coverage (SWE filtered):

Source rule_sufficient deferred skipped_short arshkon 1,882 (37.5%) 3,133 (62.4%) 4 (0.1%) asaniczka 14,489 (62.4%) 8,722 (37.6%) 2 (0.0%) scraped 10,884 (45.2%) 13,211 (54.8%) 0

No SWE rows have labeled classification coverage. The rule_sufficient rows are those where the pipeline determined rule-based confidence was high enough to skip LLM classification.

docs.title: 5a. company_industry: Compound labels in scraped
docs.text:
Source % with comma-separated labels Example arshkon 5.7% (282/4,981) "Technology, Information and Internet" (genuine multi-word name) scraped 21.2% (5,114/24,090) "Accounting, IT Services and IT Consulting, and Business Consulting and Services" (multi-industry composite)

Scraped LinkedIn profiles often list multiple industries per company (e.g., "Software Development, Financial Services, and Technology, Information and Internet"). Arshkon's comma-containing labels are mostly single industries with commas in their names. Cross-source industry comparison requires splitting or first-industry extraction for scraped data.

docs.title: 5b. seniority_native: Different label vocabularies
docs.text:
Source Labels available Entry-level? arshkon entry, associate, mid-senior, director, intern, executive + 31.4% null Yes (769 SWE) asaniczka associate, mid-senior only No scraped entry, associate, mid-senior, director, intern, executive + 17.4% null Yes (2,789 SWE)

Asaniczka provides only 2 seniority levels. This is the binding constraint for entry-level historical analysis — see T02 for the associate-as-junior-proxy assessment.

docs.title: 5c. Skills: Non-overlapping sources
docs.text:
Source skills_raw asaniczka_skills arshkon 67/5,019 (1.3%) 0 asaniczka 23,192/23,213 (99.9%) 23,192/23,213 (99.9%) scraped 0/24,095 (0%) 0

Structured skills data is effectively asaniczka-only. Cross-period skills analysis must rely on text extraction from description_core.

docs.title: 5d. Remote work: Cross-period data artifact
docs.text:
Source is_remote true is_remote_inferred true arshkon 0 (0.0%) 0 (0.0%) asaniczka 1 (0.0%) 0 (0.0%) scraped 5,438 (22.6%) 0 (0.0%)

is_remote is 0% in 2024 sources — a data collection artifact (Kaggle datasets did not capture this field). is_remote_inferred is 0% everywhere; the text-based inference logic may need investigation. Do not interpret remote-work changes cross-period.

docs.title: 5e. Description length: Systematic growth
docs.text:
Source Period Mean raw length Median raw length Mean core_length Median core_length arshkon 2024-04 3,316 2,954 2,738 2,504 asaniczka 2024-01 4,052 3,825 2,696 2,419 scraped 2026-03 5,203 5,158 4,314 4,171

Raw descriptions grew ~57% (median) and core descriptions grew ~67% (median) from 2024 to 2026. The growth in core_length is larger than in raw length, suggesting boilerplate removal is less effective on the longer scraped descriptions. All cross-period text analyses must length-normalize.

docs.title: Cross-cutting constraints
docs.text: Dimension Constraint Severity Time periods Three fixed periods (2024-01, 2024-04, 2026-03). No within-period granularity for Kaggle. LOW Geography metro_area at 61-77% coverage. 23-39% data loss per source. MEDIUM Company size arshkon-only (99.1%). Cannot stratify cross-period. HIGH LLM classification All null. Rule-based columns only. MEDIUM Remote work Cross-period comparison invalid (data artifact). HIGH

docs.title: 7. Description Quality
docs.text:
Source ok too_short arshkon 5,015 4 asaniczka 23,194 19 scraped 24,095 0

Quality is excellent: 23 rows (0.04%) flagged too_short across all SWE sources. Zero empty descriptions.

docs.title: 8. Key Findings
docs.text:
1. 28 columns have >50% null in at least one SWE source. 6 are analysis-critical, 6 are all-null LLM columns, and 16 are expected source-specific metadata gaps.

  2. description_core_llm = 0% for scraped is the most consequential gap. All cross-period text analyses are constrained to description_core (rule-based, ~44% boilerplate removal accuracy) or raw description.

  3. All Stage 10 LLM columns are null. No LLM classification budget was allocated. Rule-based columns are the sole analysis variables.

  4. company_industry has different semantics: 21.2% compound labels in scraped vs 5.7% in arshkon. Requires normalization.

  5. Remote work flags are a cross-period data artifact (0% in 2024, 22.6% in scraped). Do not interpret.

  6. Description length grew ~57-67%. All text analyses must control for length.

  7. Core analysis columns have adequate coverage. seniority_final, yoe_extracted, description_core, company_name_canonical, and ghost_job_risk support cross-period analysis, though seniority loses 7-19% to unknown labels and metro_area drops 23-39%.

docs.title: T02: Missing Data & Native-Label Comparability
docs.text: Data source: data/unified.parquet (Stage 11 final output, 99 columns, 1,395,790 rows) Generated: 2026-04-05 Filters: SWE subset = source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Asaniczka associate is NOT usable as a junior proxy. On every signal tested — title composition, YOE distribution, seniority_final resolution, and title-cue rates — asaniczka associate aligns with arshkon associate or sits between arshkon associate and mid-senior, never close to arshkon entry. The entry-level historical baseline is limited to 830 arshkon SWE entry-level rows (period 2024-04). The binding constraints for cross-period analysis are: (1) no LLM-cleaned text for scraped data, (2) thin entry-level baseline from 2024, (3) no company_industry for asaniczka, and (4) no structured skills for scraped.

Source Labels Entry-level? Distribution kaggle_arshkon entry (769), associate (275), mid-senior (2,344), director (25), intern (20), executive (11), null (1,575) Yes 68.6% non-null kaggle_asaniczka associate (2,014), mid-senior (21,199) No 100% non-null but only 2 levels scraped entry (2,789), associate (795), mid-senior (15,605), director (241), intern (429), executive (37), null (4,199) Yes 82.6% non-null

Full inventory: exploration/tables/T02/native_labels_inventory.csv

docs.title: 3. Seniority Resolution: Native to Final
docs.text: The pipeline resolves seniority_final by combining rule-based imputation with native-label backfill. This changes the distribution significantly:

docs.title: Resolution sources (how seniority_final was determined)
docs.text:
Source arshkon asaniczka scraped title_keyword 37.7% 62.5% 45.3% native_backfill 39.7% 37.5% 45.4% unknown 19.0% 0.0% 6.7% title_prior 1.9% 0.0% 0.3% weak_title_level 1.1% 0.0% 1.9% description_explicit 0.6% 0.0% 0.3% weak_title_associate 0.1% 0.0% 0.1%

Asaniczka achieves 0% unknown because every row has a native label that can backfill when title_keyword doesn't fire. But note: asaniczka's 130 entry-level rows come from title_keyword imputation (e.g., titles containing "junior" or "intern"), not from native labels.

docs.title: 4a. Question
docs.text: Can asaniczka associate (n=2,014 SWE) serve as a proxy for entry-level/junior roles in cross-period analysis?

docs.title: Title overlap (exact title_normalized match)
docs.text:
Comparison Overlap Rate (of asa_assoc 998 distinct titles) asa_associate x ark_entry 88 titles 8.8% asa_associate x ark_associate 54 titles 5.4% asa_associate x ark_mid-senior 141 titles 14.1%

Asaniczka associate titles overlap most with arshkon mid-senior (141 titles), not entry (88). This suggests the population skews upward.

docs.title: Top title comparison
docs.text: Asaniczka associate and arshkon entry share common generic titles ("software engineer", "data engineer", "java developer", "systems engineer") but these are generic titles that appear across all seniority levels. The most distinctive asaniczka associate title is "experienced associate, software engineer (python/sql)" (62 rows) — explicitly a mid-level title.

docs.title: Junior/senior title-cue rates
docs.text:
Population Junior cue rate Senior cue rate arshkon entry 0.78% 1.17% arshkon associate 0.36% 1.45% asaniczka associate 0.70% 1.59% arshkon mid-senior 0.21% 2.99% scraped entry 4.27% 0.72%

Junior title-cue definitions: "junior", "jr", "entry", "new grad", "graduate", "intern". Senior title-cue definitions: "senior", "sr", "lead", "principal", "staff", "manager", "director", "vp", "architect".

Asaniczka associate has a junior-cue rate (0.70%) between arshkon entry (0.78%) and arshkon associate (0.36%). But its senior-cue rate (1.59%) exceeds both arshkon entry (1.17%) and arshkon associate (1.45%), sitting closer to mid-senior. Title cues are ambiguous — the junior-cue signal looks entry-like, but the senior-cue signal looks associate-to-mid.

docs.title: YOE distribution (rows with yoe_extracted)
docs.text:
Population n Mean Median P25 P75 arshkon entry 475 4.12 3.0 2.0 5.0 arshkon associate 155 3.85 3.0 2.0 5.0 asaniczka associate 1,311 4.53 4.0 3.0 5.0 arshkon mid-senior 1,570 5.56 5.0 4.0 7.0 scraped entry 1,073 2.29 2.0 1.0 2.0 scraped associate 563 4.26 3.0 2.0 5.0 scraped mid-senior 11,308 5.36 5.0 3.0 7.0

Asaniczka associate has a higher YOE profile than arshkon entry. Median 4.0 vs 3.0, P25 of 3.0 vs 2.0, mean 4.53 vs 4.12. It sits between arshkon associate (3.85 mean) and arshkon mid-senior (5.56 mean), slightly above arshkon associate.

For reference, scraped entry has median 2.0 and mean 2.29 — substantially lower than asaniczka associate.

docs.title: Seniority_final resolution conditional on native label
docs.text:
Native label Resolves to entry Resolves to associate Resolves to mid-senior arshkon entry 98.4% — 1.6% arshkon associate 3.6% 74.9% 21.5% asaniczka associate 2.1% 72.0% 25.9% arshkon mid-senior 0.4% — 99.4% scraped entry 97.8% — 2.2% scraped associate 2.3% 69.1% 28.6%

Asaniczka associate resolves almost identically to arshkon associate: 72.0% stay associate, 25.9% upgrade to mid-senior, only 2.1% downgrade to entry. This is far from arshkon entry's 98.4% entry resolution. The pipeline treats asaniczka associate as associate-level, not entry-level.

docs.title: 4c. Signal Summary
docs.text: Signal Closer to entry? Closer to associate? Closer to mid-senior? Title overlap - - Highest overlap with mid-senior Junior title-cue rate Marginal - - Senior title-cue rate - - Closer to mid-senior YOE median - Between associate and mid - YOE P25 - - Closer to mid-senior seniority_final resolution - Nearly identical to associate -

docs.title: 4d. Verdict
docs.text:
Asaniczka associate is NOT usable as a junior proxy. It behaves as a lower-mid-level population:

  • YOE profile (median 4.0, P25 3.0) exceeds arshkon entry (median 3.0, P25 2.0) and arshkon associate (median 3.0, P25 2.0). It sits between arshkon associate and mid-senior.
  • Title composition overlaps most with mid-senior titles (141 shared), not entry titles (88 shared).
  • seniority_final resolution is virtually identical to arshkon associate (72% stay associate, 26% upgrade to mid-senior).
  • The senior title-cue rate (1.59%) exceeds arshkon entry (1.17%) and associate (1.45%).

The only signal that trends entry-like is the junior title-cue rate (0.70% vs 0.78%), but this is a very weak signal given the overall evidence.

Decision: Do not treat asaniczka associate as a junior proxy. The entry-level historical baseline is arshkon only (830 rows, period 2024-04).

docs.title: 6. Key Constraint Mapping for Wave 2-3 Analyses
docs.text:
Analysis Category Binding Constraint Severity Text/requirements analysis description_core_llm = 0% for scraped. Must use description_core (rule-based, ~44% accuracy) for cross-period text comparison. Description length grew ~57-67%, requiring normalization. HIGH Seniority stratification Entry-level: 830 arshkon + 3,255 scraped (no 2024-01 baseline). Asaniczka associate is NOT a junior proxy. 71.8% unknown rate limits seniority-stratified analyses. HIGH Geographic analysis metro_area: 61-77% coverage per source. ~25% of SWE posts lack metro assignment. Usable with acknowledged data loss. MEDIUM Company-level analysis company_industry = 0% for asaniczka. Cross-period industry analysis limited to arshkon (5K) vs scraped (24K). Compound-label normalization needed for scraped. HIGH Requirements/skills skills_raw = 0% for scraped, 1.3% for arshkon. Structured skills = asaniczka-only. Must extract from description text for cross-period comparison. HIGH Company size company_size = arshkon-only. Cannot stratify by company size cross-period. HIGH Remote work is_remote = 0% in 2024 (data artifact). is_remote_inferred = 0% everywhere. Cross-period remote-work comparison is invalid. HIGH Ghost/inflation detection ghost_job_risk: 100% coverage but very conservative (519 non-low out of 1.40M). ghost_assessment_llm = 0%. Limited to rule-based heuristic. MEDIUM YOE extraction yoe_extracted: 61-72% coverage across sources. Well-distributed. Adequate for cross-period YOE analysis with acknowledged missingness. LOW LLM classification seniority_llm, swe_classification_llm, ghost_assessment_llm: ALL NULL. No Stage 10 budget. Rule-based columns are sole analysis variables. MEDIUM

Full constraint mapping: exploration/tables/T02/constraint_mapping.csv

docs.title: Viable comparisons (arshkon vs scraped, with caveats)
docs.text: Comparison arshkon n scraped n Key caveat SWE description text 5,019 24,095 Must use description_core (rule-based); length-normalize SWE YOE requirements 3,040 16,525 ~35% missing per source Seniority distribution 4,066 22,469 arshkon is 3-month-later snapshot vs asaniczka Entry-level YOE 476 1,066 Small arshkon n; known YOE paradox (3.0 vs 2.0 median) Company-level patterns 4,974 24,095 ~813 company overlap for matched analysis Industry distribution 4,981 24,090 Compound label normalization needed Geographic distribution 3,065 18,657 26-metro frame; ~30% unmapped

docs.title: Viable with asaniczka (limited dimensions)
docs.text: Comparison asaniczka n Notes SWE text (rule-based) 23,213 Largest 2024 sample but no company_industry SWE YOE 16,755 Excellent coverage Company-level (name only) 23,213 No industry, no size Mid-senior analysis 21,542 Asaniczka's strength: large mid-senior sample

docs.title: Not viable cross-period
docs.text: Comparison Reason LLM-cleaned text 0% scraped coverage Remote work Data artifact in 2024 Structured skills Source-specific, non-overlapping Company size arshkon-only LLM seniority/classification All null

docs.title: 8. Key Findings
docs.text:
1. Asaniczka associate is NOT a junior proxy. It aligns with arshkon associate on seniority resolution (72% remain associate, 26% upgrade to mid-senior) and has higher YOE (median 4.0 vs 3.0 for entry). The entry-level baseline is 830 arshkon rows only.

  2. Six HIGH-severity constraints bind Wave 2-3 analyses: no LLM text for scraped, thin entry-level baseline, no asaniczka industry data, no cross-period skills, no cross-period company size, and invalid remote-work comparison.

  3. The ~57-67% growth in description length from 2024 to 2026 is a first-order confounder for any text analysis. Length normalization is mandatory.

  4. Core analysis is viable for cross-period text comparison (using description_core), YOE analysis, seniority distribution, and company-level patterns. The primary trade-off is quality (rule-based text cleaning, ~44% accuracy) vs coverage (100% of all sources).

  5. Entry-level YOE paradox persists. Arshkon entry median = 3.0 YOE vs scraped entry median = 2.0 YOE. This contradicts a scope-inflation hypothesis for entry-level roles. Note that arshkon associate is also 3.0 YOE median, suggesting arshkon entry-level labels may be more heterogeneous than scraped entry labels.

docs.title: T03: Seniority Label Comparison
docs.text:
Task: Compare all seniority variants -- where do they agree, and does the choice change RQ1 results?

Date: 2026-04-05 Data: data/unified.parquet (Stage 11 final output, 1.40M rows, 99 columns) Filters: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true

docs.title: 1. Seniority Coverage Summary (SWE Rows)
docs.text:
Source Total SWE Native Non-null Imputed Non-unknown Final Non-unknown kaggle_arshkon 5,019 68.6% 44.2% 81.0% kaggle_asaniczka 23,213 100.0% 69.2% 100.0% scraped 24,095 82.6% 54.2% 93.3%

Key observations: - Asaniczka has 100% native coverage but only mid-senior and associate labels (zero entry-level). This is the critical gap for RQ1. - Scraped data has substantially higher native coverage (82.6%) than arshkon (68.6%), reflecting LinkedIn's more complete metadata in 2026. - seniority_final achieves the best coverage by merging imputed + native backfill: 81% arshkon, 100% asaniczka, 93% scraped.

docs.title: 2a. seniority_native vs seniority_final
docs.text:
46,056 pairs where native is non-null and final is non-unknown.

Native \ Final entry associate mid-senior director entry 3,484 (97.9%) 0 74 (2.1%) 0 associate 70 (2.3%) 2,205 (71.5%) 808 (26.2%) 1 mid-senior 125 (0.3%) 0 38,834 (99.2%) 189 (0.5%) director 0 0 193 (72.6%) 73 (27.4%)
  • Agreement rate: 96.8%
  • Cohen's kappa: 0.844 (strong agreement)
  • Pattern: Entry labels are highly preserved (97.9%). Associate is problematic -- 26.2% of native associate labels become mid-senior in seniority_final, likely due to title-keyword overriding. Director is poorly resolved: 72.6% of native directors become mid-senior.

docs.title: 2b. seniority_imputed vs seniority_final
docs.text:
31,342 pairs where imputed is non-unknown and final is non-unknown.

Imputed \ Final entry associate mid-senior director entry 902 (99.0%) 2 (0.2%) 7 (0.8%) 0 associate 182 (31.7%) 245 (42.6%) 148 (25.7%) 0 mid-senior 216 (0.7%) 129 (0.4%) 29,231 (98.8%) 3 director 0 0 3 (1.1%) 274 (98.9%)
  • Agreement rate: 97.8%
  • Cohen's kappa: 0.806 (strong agreement)
  • Pattern: Imputed entry labels are highly reliable (99% preserved). Imputed associate is the weakest class -- only 42.6% agreement with final. When the imputer fires on ambiguous "associate" titles, it frequently conflicts with native labels that resolve differently.

docs.title: 2c. seniority_native vs seniority_imputed
docs.text:
27,967 pairs where both are non-null/non-unknown. This is the direct classifier accuracy test.

Native \ Imputed entry associate mid-senior director entry 293 (38.4%) 182 (23.8%) 289 (37.8%) 0 associate 72 (6.2%) 150 (12.9%) 937 (80.8%) 1 mid-senior 132 (0.5%) 148 (0.6%) 25,339 (98.2%) 192 (0.7%) director 0 0 195 (84.1%) 37 (15.9%)
  • Agreement rate: 92.3%
  • Cohen's kappa: 0.354 (fair agreement)
  • Critical finding: The rule-based imputer has a severe mid-senior default bias. For native entry-level rows where the imputer also fires, only 38.4% are correctly classified as entry -- 37.8% are called mid-senior and 23.8% associate. For native associate, 80.8% are called mid-senior. The low kappa (0.354) is entirely driven by the non-mid-senior classes.

docs.title: 3. Per-Class Accuracy: Rule-Based Classifier (Imputed) vs Native Ground Truth
Arshkon (Apr 2024)
docs.text: Native Class Total w/ Imputed Correct Accuracy Main Error Imputer Coverage entry 132 28 21.2% mid-senior=74, associate=30 17.2% of 769 native associate 85 8 9.4% mid-senior=66 30.9% of 275 native mid-senior 1,427 1,402 98.2% - 60.9% of 2,344 native director 19 4 21.1% mid-senior=15 76.0% of 25 native

docs.title: Scraped (Mar 2026)
docs.text: Native Class Total w/ Imputed Correct Accuracy Main Error Imputer Coverage entry 632 265 41.9% mid-senior=215, associate=152 22.7% of 2,789 native associate 322 28 8.7% mid-senior=275 40.5% of 795 native mid-senior 9,068 8,858 97.7% - 58.1% of 15,605 native director 213 33 15.5% mid-senior=180 88.4% of 241 native

docs.title: Temporal Comparison
docs.text:
Native Class Arshkon Accuracy Scraped Accuracy Difference entry 21.2% 41.9% +20.7pp associate 9.4% 8.7% -0.7pp mid-senior 98.2% 97.7% -0.5pp director 21.1% 15.5% -5.6pp

Finding: Entry-level accuracy nearly doubles from arshkon (21.2%) to scraped (41.9%). This is NOT temporal instability of the classifier itself -- it reflects that scraped entry-level postings more often use explicit title keywords ("Junior Software Engineer", "Entry Level Developer") that the rule-based classifier can detect. Arshkon entry-level postings may have less explicit title signals, consistent with the much lower imputer coverage rate (17.2% vs 22.7%).

Associate and director accuracy is consistently poor across both periods -- the imputer is simply unable to identify these classes reliably.

Important coverage caveat: The imputer only fires for a minority of entry-level rows (17-23%). The accuracy figures above are conditional on the imputer producing a non-unknown label. The imputer's most common behavior for entry-level rows is to produce "unknown" (77-83% of the time), which is then backfilled from native in seniority_final.

docs.title: 4. Entry-Level Label Provenance
docs.text:
Where do seniority_final = 'entry' labels come from?

Source Total Entry native_backfill title_keyword description_explicit kaggle_arshkon 830 729 (87.8%) 100 (12.0%) 1 (0.1%) kaggle_asaniczka 130 0 (0%) 130 (100%) 0 scraped 3,255 2,592 (79.6%) 661 (20.3%) 2 (0.1%)

Critical: Asaniczka's 130 entry-level labels come entirely from title_keyword imputation -- the native labels for asaniczka only contain mid-senior and associate. These 130 are likely titles like "Junior Software Engineer" that were keyword-matched. Given asaniczka's 23K SWE rows, this represents 0.6% entry-level share, which is essentially a data artifact, not a real measurement.

docs.title: Entry-level leakage
docs.text: 74 rows with seniority_native = 'entry' are overridden to seniority_final = 'mid-senior' due to title_keyword rules. These are cases where the title contains mid-senior keywords (e.g., "Senior") despite the platform labeling the posting as entry level. This is a small but systematic issue.

docs.title: 5. RQ1 Junior Share Comparison
docs.text:
Four operationalizations, comparing arshkon (2024-04) and scraped (2026-03). Asaniczka is included for reference but its junior share is unreliable due to zero native entry labels.

Variant Arshkon Junior % (n) Scraped Junior % (n) Asaniczka Junior % (n) Direction A: seniority_final (non-unknown) 20.4% (4,066) 14.5% (22,469) 0.6% (23,213) DOWN 5.9pp B: seniority_native only 22.3% (3,444) 14.0% (19,896) 0.0% (23,213) DOWN 8.3pp C: High-confidence (title_keyword + native_backfill) 21.4% (3,881) 14.9% (21,831) 0.6% (23,213) DOWN 6.5pp D: All signals (excl. unknown) 20.4% (4,066) 14.5% (22,469) 0.6% (23,213) DOWN 5.9pp

docs.title: Verdict: All variants agree on direction AND approximate magnitude
docs.text:
All four variants show junior share declining by 5.9-8.3 percentage points from arshkon to scraped. The native-only variant (B) gives the highest arshkon junior share (22.3%) and the largest decline (8.3pp), because it uses the cleanest labels but excludes rows without native metadata.

The direction is robust: junior share decreased from ~20-22% to ~14-15% regardless of which seniority column is used.

Critical caveats:

  1. Composition effects: Arshkon and scraped have limited company overlap. The decline could partly reflect different employer mix rather than actual restructuring.
  2. Asaniczka cannot contribute to this comparison. Its 0.6% entry-level share is an artifact of having zero native entry labels.
  3. The absolute level of junior share depends on the operationalization (20.4% vs 22.3% for arshkon), but the change direction is robust.
  4. Variants A and D are identical because the current pipeline has no additional weak signals beyond what feeds seniority_final.

docs.title: seniority_final
docs.text: Source n entry associate mid-senior director kaggle_arshkon 4,066 20.4% 5.6% 73.4% 0.5% kaggle_asaniczka 23,213 0.6% 6.2% 92.8% 0.4% scraped 22,469 14.5% 2.8% 81.8% 1.0%

docs.title: seniority_native
docs.text: Source n entry associate mid-senior director kaggle_arshkon 3,444 22.3% 8.0% 68.1% 0.7% kaggle_asaniczka 23,213 0.0% 8.7% 91.3% 0.0% scraped 19,896 14.0% 4.0% 78.4% 1.2%

docs.title: seniority_imputed (non-unknown)
docs.text:
Source n entry associate mid-senior director kaggle_arshkon 2,216 4.6% 3.2% 91.7% 0.5% kaggle_asaniczka 16,069 0.8% 1.1% 97.5% 0.6% scraped 13,057 5.2% 2.6% 90.9% 1.3%

The imputed column severely compresses entry-level and associate shares due to its mid-senior bias. Entry share in imputed is 4.6-5.2% vs 14.0-22.3% in native. This confirms the imputer alone is unreliable for seniority distribution analysis.

docs.title: 7. seniority_final_source Distribution (SWE, non-unknown)
docs.text:
Source native_backfill title_keyword Other kaggle_arshkon 49.0% 46.5% 4.5% (title_prior, weak_title_level, description_explicit, weak_title_associate) kaggle_asaniczka 37.5% 62.5% 0% scraped 48.6% 48.5% 2.9% (weak_title_level, description_explicit, title_prior, weak_title_associate)

Arshkon and scraped have a roughly even split between native_backfill and title_keyword. Asaniczka relies more on title_keyword (62.5%) because its native labels only contain mid-senior/associate and thus native_backfill only operates for those classes.

docs.title: For trend estimation (RQ1 junior share over time)
docs.text:
Recommended: Variant B (seniority_native only) for the primary estimate, with Variant A as a robustness check.

Rationale: - Native labels are platform-assigned and consistent across periods - Rule-based imputation has unknown temporal stability and different accuracy rates (21.2% entry accuracy in arshkon vs 41.9% in scraped) - Using only native labels avoids the risk that imputation artifacts create a spurious trend - The 8.3pp decline (variant B) represents the cleanest signal

Limitation: Native coverage is 68.6% for arshkon and 82.6% for scraped. The 14pp coverage gap between sources means the native-only sample may not be fully comparable. Rows with missing native labels could have different seniority distributions.

docs.title: For cross-sectional comparison (within-period analysis)
docs.text: Recommended: Variant A (seniority_final) because it maximizes sample size and has the best coverage (81-100%). Within a single period, imputation rules are consistently applied.

docs.title: For classification training and validation
docs.text: Recommended: seniority_native as ground truth, with separate accuracy reporting by source. The per-class accuracy results (Section 3) show the rule-based classifier is unreliable for non-mid-senior classes. Any classifier trained or validated against seniority_final inherits these biases.

docs.title: For robustness/sensitivity analyses
docs.text: Report all four variants. The agreement between variants strengthens the finding; disagreement flags fragile results. Given that all four agree on the direction and magnitude of junior share change, the RQ1 finding is robust to operationalization choice.

docs.title: The 3-level mapping is not just convenient -- it is necessary
docs.text: Associate accuracy is 8.7-9.4% across sources. The associate category is essentially unusable as a standalone label. Collapsing to the 3-level mapping (entry=junior, associate=mid, mid-senior+director=senior) is the only defensible approach for analyses that need more than a binary junior/non-junior split.

docs.title: 9. Surprises and Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Native-vs-imputed kappa is only 0.354. This is worse than the 0.440 reported in the prior run on stage8_final.parquet. The rule-based imputer is even less aligned with native labels than previously measured, primarily because entry-level accuracy dropped to 21.2% in arshkon (down from 31.6% in the prior report). This appears to be driven by slightly different row counts in the unified dataset.

  2. Director labels are severely misclassified. 72.6% of native directors become mid-senior in seniority_final, and the imputer has only 15.5-21.1% accuracy for directors. Director-level analysis should rely exclusively on native labels where available.

  3. The arshkon entry-level imputer accuracy (21.2%) is alarmingly low. Only 28 of 132 entry-level arshkon rows that the imputer classifies are correct. The imputer's value for entry-level detection is almost entirely in the 77-83% of rows where it returns "unknown" and triggers native backfill.

  4. All junior share variants agree on decline of 5.9-8.3pp. This is moderate, not dramatic. Arshkon starts at 20-22% junior, not 50%. The decline is real but should not be characterized as "juniors disappearing."

  5. 74 native entry-level rows are overridden to mid-senior by title keywords. These posting-level contradictions (platform says entry, title says senior) warrant investigation -- they could be platform labeling errors or genuine mismatches.

docs.title: T04: SWE Classification Audit
docs.text:
Task: Assess SWE classification quality after preprocessing fixes.

Date: 2026-04-05 Data: data/unified.parquet (Stage 11 final output, 1.40M rows, 99 columns) Filters: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok'

docs.title: SWE rows (is_swe = true): 52,327 total
docs.text:
Tier Count % of SWE Avg Confidence Med Confidence regex 47,585 90.9% 1.000 1.000 title_lookup_llm 3,948 7.5% 0.900 0.900 embedding_high 794 1.5% 0.895 0.890

The vast majority of SWE classification comes from regex (pattern matching on title). The title_lookup_llm tier contributes ~7.5%, and embedding_high is a small tail (~1.5%).

docs.title: By source
docs.text:
Source regex title_lookup_llm embedding_high Total SWE kaggle_arshkon 4,477 (89.2%) 465 (9.3%) 77 (1.5%) 5,019 kaggle_asaniczka 20,411 (87.9%) 2,407 (10.4%) 395 (1.7%) 23,213 scraped 22,697 (94.2%) 1,076 (4.5%) 322 (1.3%) 24,095

Scraped data has a higher regex share (94.2%) and lower title_lookup_llm share (4.5%), suggesting scraped postings use more standard SWE title patterns. This likely reflects the scraper's query design targeting explicit SWE titles.

docs.title: SWE-adjacent rows: 20,210 total
docs.text: Tier Count % Avg Confidence regex 9,139 45.2% 0.820 embedding_adjacent 6,503 32.2% 0.759 title_lookup_llm 4,568 22.6% 0.850

docs.title: Control rows: 141,816 total
docs.text: Tier Count % regex 107,715 76.0% unresolved (catch-all) 34,101 24.0%

docs.title: 2. Dual-Flag Violations
docs.text:
Violations (is_swe + is_swe_adjacent + is_control > 1): 0

No rows are simultaneously classified as multiple categories. The mutual exclusivity constraint is perfectly enforced.

Unclassified rows: 1,061,002 (83.2% of 1,275,355 filtered rows) are neither SWE, SWE-adjacent, nor control. These are expected -- the classification targets specific occupation groups, not the entire job market.

docs.title: 3. Borderline SWE Sample Audit (title_lookup_llm tier)
docs.text: Reviewed 50 SWE postings from the title_lookup_llm tier (all conf=0.900). Manual quality assessment based on title and description snippet:

docs.title: Clear true positives (correct SWE classification): ~50%
docs.text:
• "ReactJS Developer" -- frontend software development
  • "Principal Firmware Engineer" -- embedded software
  • "Senior MLOps Engineer" -- ML infrastructure
  • "Angular developer" / "Angular Lead Developer" -- frontend software
  • "DevSecOps Engineer" / "Senior DevSecOps Engineer" -- security-aware software development
  • "Lead Developer" / "Senior Developer" / "Developer" -- general software development
  • "Embedded Engineer" -- embedded software
  • "Java Architect" -- software architecture
  • "Database Engineer II" -- database software
  • "Programmer" / "Senior Programmer Analyst" -- software development
  • "Distinguished Engineer, Generative AI Systems" -- senior software/AI

docs.title: Borderline cases (debatable): ~25%
docs.text:
• "Quality Assurance Engineer" (multiple instances) -- QA roles involving some automation/code but primarily testing
  • "Senior Quality Assurance Automation Engineer" -- test automation, borderline SWE
  • "QA Lead" (multiple instances) -- QA leadership
  • "Automation Test Analyst" -- test automation
  • "Software Quality Assurance Engineer" / "Senior Software Quality Engineer" -- software QA
  • "QA Automation Lead Engineer" -- test automation leadership
  • "Software Engineering Manager" -- management role, arguably should be excluded

docs.title: Likely false positives: ~15%
docs.text:
• "Senior Automation Engineer" (National Carwash Solutions) -- industrial/manufacturing automation
  • "Test Engineer" (description: "safety and performance standards", "sustaining engineering") -- hardware/product testing
  • "Staff Systems Test Engineer" (Northrop Grumman) -- defense systems testing
  • "Test Development Engineer" -- ambiguous, could be hardware test development
  • "Principal Test Engineer" (BAE/defense context) -- defense systems testing

docs.title: Quality estimate for title_lookup_llm tier:
docs.text:
• True SWE: ~50% (clear software development roles)
  • Borderline/debatable: ~25% (QA/test automation roles involving code)
  • Likely false positives: ~15% (hardware, manufacturing, defense testing roles)
  • Management roles that may warrant separate treatment: ~10%

docs.title: 4. Borderline Non-SWE Audit
docs.text: Reviewed 50 non-SWE rows with "engineer", "developer", or "software" in the title. Assessment:

docs.title: Correctly excluded (not SWE):
docs.text:
• Hardware/traditional engineering: Mechanical Engineer, Electrical Engineer, Civil Engineer, Senior Structural Engineer -- correctly excluded
  • Network/telecom: Senior Telecom / Network Engineer, Network Engineer Wired -- correctly classified as non-SWE (these are SWE-adjacent or control)
  • Industrial/manufacturing: Lean Manufacturing Engineer, Industrial Engineer, Process Line Engineer -- correctly excluded
  • Non-IC roles: Engineering Project Manager, Engineer Supervisor -- correctly excluded
  • Specialty engineering: Audio Visual Engineer, Geotechnical Engineer, Traffic Engineer, Water/Wastewater Engineer -- correctly excluded
  • Sales-adjacent: Workday Sales Engineer -- correctly excluded

docs.title: Potential false negatives identified:
docs.text:
• "Ruby on Rails Developer" (conf=0.637, unresolved) -- this appears to be a genuine SWE role that was missed
  • "Principal Engineer, YouTube ML Recommendations Systems" (conf=0.601, unresolved) -- strong SWE signals in description
  • "Senior Deep Learning Engineer" (conf=0.814, embedding_adjacent) -- classified as adjacent rather than SWE
  • "Senior Business Intelligence Developer" (conf=0.605, unresolved) -- borderline SWE/adjacent

docs.title: Estimated false negative rate from this sample: ~4-8% (2-4 of 50)
docs.text: The exclusion rules for non-software engineering disciplines appear robust. Most correctly-excluded rows are hardware engineering, civil/structural, or management roles.

docs.title: Method: Software keyword scan in description
docs.text:
Checked whether SWE-classified descriptions mention software/code/programming-related terms (software, programming, coding, developer, python, java, javascript, typescript, c++, api, frontend, backend, full-stack, devops, deploy, repository, git, codebase):

Tier Total Has SW Signal No SW Signal No-SW % regex 47,585 45,367 2,218 4.7% title_lookup_llm 3,948 3,482 466 11.8% embedding_high 794 741 53 6.7% Total 52,327 49,590 2,737 5.2%

Not all rows without software keywords are false positives -- some postings use domain-specific language. But this provides a reasonable upper bound on false-positive risk.

docs.title: Suspicious title categories within SWE:
docs.text:
Title Pattern SWE Count Mentions "software" Mentions mfg/hardware %automation engineer% 545 56% 44% %reliability engineer% 989 56% 32% %test engineer% 699 73% 68% %quality assurance% 278 60% 41% %qa engineer% 162 77% 22%

Key findings: - Test engineer has the highest manufacturing keyword co-occurrence (68%), meaning nearly 2/3 of SWE-classified test engineers mention manufacturing or hardware in their descriptions. Many of these are likely product/hardware test roles, not software testers. - Automation engineer has 44% manufacturing overlap, indicating a substantial fraction are industrial automation roles. - Reliability engineer has 32% manufacturing overlap, lower than test/automation but still indicating meaningful contamination. - QA engineer is the cleanest of the suspicious categories at 22% manufacturing overlap.

docs.title: Estimated false positive rate:
docs.text:
• Regex tier (90.9% of SWE): ~3-5% false positives. Regex patterns like "software engineer" are highly precise.
  • Title_lookup_llm tier (7.5%): ~15-25% false positives. This tier includes "automation engineer", "reliability engineer", "test engineer" which conflate software and hardware/manufacturing roles.
  • Embedding_high tier (1.5%): ~7-10% false positives. Slightly elevated but small absolute count.
  • Overall weighted estimate: ~4-6% false positives (~2,100-3,100 rows out of 52,327).

docs.title: 6. False Negative Estimation
docs.text: 893 non-SWE rows match strong SWE title patterns (software engineer, software developer, full-stack, backend engineer, frontend engineer, web developer).

docs.title: Breakdown by category:
docs.text:
Category Count Notes Management 610 Director/Manager of SW Engineering -- intentional exclusion Other 159 Mixed -- includes teaching, editor, client technology roles Intern 61 Internship roles Senior IC 58 Senior/Staff/Principal-prefixed roles caught by edge cases Advisory 5 Consultants

Key insight: 610/893 (68.3%) of potential false negatives are management roles (e.g., "Director, Software Engineering - Card Core", "Manager, Software Engineering, Back End"). These are intentionally excluded from SWE because they are not IC development roles. This is a correct design decision for a study of SWE posting requirements.

Top management false-negative titles are dominated by Capital One's naming convention ("Director, Software Engineering - Card Core", "Senior Director, Software Engineering - Card Authorizations", etc.), which inflates the count due to multi-posting.

The remaining ~283 potential false negatives include: - Interns (61): Software engineering internships -- excluded by level, arguably correct - Teaching (21): "College of IT: FT Instructor - Software Engineering" -- correctly excluded - Senior IC edge cases (58): Roles with unusual titling that the regex doesn't capture - Other (159): Titles like "Client Technology - Software Engineering" where the role may or may not be IC development

Estimated false negative rate: ~283 genuine misses / ~53,000 target = ~0.5%, which is negligible.

docs.title: SWE-Adjacent (20,210 rows)
docs.text:
Top normalized titles: 1. Network Engineer (1,217) 2. Quality Engineer (1,114) 3. Data Scientist (965) 4. Data Analyst (872) 5. Architect (537) 6. Windows Engineer (333) 7. Solutions Architect (300) 8. Security Engineer (296) 9. Systems Administrator (254) 10. DDI Network Engineer (169) 11. Data Architect (168) 12. Database Administrator (166)

Character: Technical roles involving technology configuration, data analysis, architecture, or security -- adjacent to software development but not primarily code-writing roles. The boundary with SWE is sharpest at Data Scientist (could involve significant coding) and Security Engineer (could include security software development).

docs.title: Control Group (141,816 rows)
docs.text:
Top normalized titles: 1. Accountant (6,426) 2. Registered Nurse (3,513) 3. Electrical Engineer (2,593) 4. Accounting Manager (1,983) 5. Mechanical Engineer (1,949) 6. Financial Analyst (1,932) 7. Registered Nurse / RN variants (~5,500 combined) 8. Civil Engineer (916)

Character: Professional roles from completely different domains -- healthcare, finance, hardware engineering. These serve as a useful baseline for posting-level trends that affect all occupations, not just SWE. The inclusion of electrical and mechanical engineers provides a direct non-software engineering comparison group.

docs.title: Distinctiveness
docs.text: SWE-adjacent is well-separated from SWE: top titles are infrastructure, data analysis, and architecture roles that use technology but don't primarily write code. Control is maximally distinct -- healthcare and finance roles have no overlap with SWE. The three-category system provides clean comparison groups at different distances from the SWE core.

docs.title: 8. Boundary Role Classification Across Periods
docs.text:
This is the analytically critical part of the audit: do boundary roles shift classification across periods?

Role Arshkon SWE% Asaniczka SWE% Scraped SWE% Stable? Data Engineer 91.6% (359/392) 85.5% (1,259/1,473) 92.8% (1,888/2,034) Yes ML Engineer 90.9% (10/11) 96.7% (29/30) 93.3% (249/267) Yes (small n) Machine Learning Eng. 100% (87/87) 91.5% (375/410) 98.3% (738/751) Yes DevOps 97.7% (250/256) 99.3% (754/759) 99.1% (994/1,003) Yes Site Reliability 100% (91/91) 99.3% (277/279) 99.8% (412/413) Yes Platform Engineer 90.7% (49/54) 86.3% (314/364) 85.0% (340/400) Mostly Cloud Engineer 98.1% (105/107) 96.2% (205/213) 90.7% (353/389) Mild shift Security Engineer 1.6% (4/249) 0.4% (3/677) 3.1% (40/1,272) Stable (non-SWE) QA Engineer 32.3% (10/31) 59.5% (72/121) 42.8% (80/187) Unstable Test Engineer 32.3% (54/167) 33.0% (414/1,256) 24.5% (231/942) Mostly Solutions Architect 2.3% (3/133) 0.5% (4/754) 0.1% (1/688) Stable (non-SWE)

docs.title: Key findings:
docs.text:
1. Core SWE roles are stably classified. Data Engineer, ML Engineer, DevOps, SRE all classify as SWE at >85% across all periods. These are safe to include in SWE analyses.

  2. Security Engineer is consistently non-SWE. 0.4-3.1% SWE classification. The boundary is stable and correctly placed on the SWE-adjacent side. The small uptick in scraped (3.1%) is worth monitoring but not concerning.

  3. QA Engineer is the most unstable boundary. Classification as SWE ranges from 32.3% to 59.5% across sources. This means QA engineers are being classified inconsistently, and any temporal analysis comparing QA inclusion rates could create spurious trends. The low base count (31-187 per source) amplifies the instability.

  4. Cloud Engineer shows mild reclassification drift. From 98.1% SWE (arshkon) to 90.7% SWE (scraped). This 7.4pp shift could reflect real changes in how cloud engineer roles are described (more infrastructure-focused in 2026), or could be a classifier artifact.

  5. Platform Engineer is borderline stable with 85-91% SWE classification. The boundary is placed correctly (mostly SWE), but ~10-15% of platform engineers are classified as adjacent.

  6. Test Engineer classification is declining. From 32-33% SWE (2024) to 24.5% (2026). This 8pp drop could reflect real role evolution (more specialized testing, less code) or title convention changes.

docs.title: Risk assessment for temporal analysis:
docs.text:
The unstable QA and drifting test engineer boundaries together affect: - QA Engineer: 10-80 SWE rows per source (very small) - Test Engineer: 54-414 SWE rows per source (moderate)

Combined, these represent ~1,000-1,500 rows within SWE, or roughly 2-3% of the 52K SWE sample. A 20-30pp swing in their classification rate could shift SWE counts by ~200-400 rows, which is small relative to total SWE but could matter for compositional analyses within specific role subcategories.

Recommendation: For sensitivity analysis, report results with and without QA/test engineer titles.

docs.title: 9. Embedding_high Tier Audit
docs.text:
The 794 embedding_high SWE rows show distinctive title patterns:

Top titles: - "ENGINEER SYSTEMS 3" (10), "ENGINEER SOFTWARE 3" (4) -- reversed title formats, likely parsing artifacts - "Principal Engineer Software" (7), "Sr Principal Engineer Software" (6) -- valid but unusual formatting - "AI Research Engineer" (6), "AI Native Engineer" (5) -- genuine SWE roles with non-standard titles - "ML Ops Engineer" (5), "Senior ML Ops Engineer" (4) -- valid MLOps roles - "Test Automation Lead" (4), "Software Test Automation Engineer" (4) -- QA/test automation - "QA/QC Engineer" (3), "QA Test Lead" (3), "QA Engineer Automated" (3) -- QA roles

Assessment: The embedding_high tier contains a mix of: - Genuine SWE roles with unusual title formatting (~50-60%) - QA/test automation roles (~20-25%) - Parsing artifacts with reversed name/title fields (~10-15%) - Edge cases (~10%)

The reversed-title artifacts (e.g., "ENGINEER SYSTEMS 3") suggest a data quality issue upstream of classification. These rows should be audited for title-field swapping.

docs.title: 10. Estimated Overall Classification Quality
docs.text:
Metric Estimate Confidence False positive rate 4-6% (~2,100-3,100 / 52,327) Medium -- based on keyword scan and manual audit False negative rate <0.5% (~283 / ~53,000) High -- most "misses" are intentional management exclusions Temporal stability Good for core roles, unstable for QA/test High confidence Dual-flag violations 0 Verified Title overlap SWE/adjacent 0 Verified

The classifier is conservative -- it has a low false-negative rate but a moderate false-positive rate. The false positives are concentrated in the title_lookup_llm tier (7.5% of SWE), where industrial automation, reliability, and hardware test engineers bleed in. The regex tier (90.9%) is highly precise.

docs.title: 11. Surprises and Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Test engineer has the highest manufacturing co-occurrence (68%). Nearly 2/3 of SWE-classified test engineers mention manufacturing or hardware keywords. This is the single highest-risk boundary for false positives and the most compelling case for targeted exclusion rules.

  2. "Ruby on Rails Developer" is classified as non-SWE. This is a clear false negative (conf=0.637, unresolved tier). A dedicated regex pattern for "Rails Developer" would fix this and similar framework-named-developer titles.

  3. "Senior Deep Learning Engineer" is SWE-adjacent, not SWE. Classified via embedding_adjacent (conf=0.814). Given that deep learning engineering is heavily code-based, this seems like a misclassification that an expanded regex could fix.

  4. Capital One dominates false negatives. The management false-negative list is heavily skewed by Capital One's titling convention ("Director, Software Engineering - Card Core", "Manager, Software Engineering, Android"). This is an artifact of Capital One posting many distinctly-titled management roles, not a systemic classifier problem.

  5. Embedding_high contains reversed title artifacts. "ENGINEER SYSTEMS 3" and "ENGINEER SOFTWARE 3" appear to be cases where name/title fields were swapped during ingestion. These 20+ rows have correct classification despite garbled titles, suggesting the embedding classifier is robust to title noise.

  6. Scraped data has 94.2% regex purity vs 87.9% for asaniczka. The scraper's search queries naturally select for postings with explicit SWE title keywords, reducing reliance on noisier classification tiers. This is a beneficial side effect that improves classification quality for the scraped period.

docs.title: 12. Recommendations
docs.text:
1. For primary SWE analyses: Use is_swe = true with the understanding that 4-6% false positives are present. This is acceptable for most analyses.

  2. For precision-sensitive analyses: Add filter swe_classification_tier = 'regex' to restrict to high-precision titles (90.9% of sample). This drops title_lookup_llm and embedding_high rows.

  3. For robustness checks: Report results with and without:

  4. QA/test engineer titles (unstable boundary)
  5. Automation/reliability engineer titles (manufacturing contamination)

  6. title_lookup_llm tier rows

  7. Pipeline improvement (regex expansion): Add regex patterns for "firmware engineer", "MLOps engineer", "DevSecOps engineer", "AI engineer", "Rails developer", "deep learning engineer" to promote these from title_lookup_llm/embedding to the more precise regex tier.

  8. Pipeline improvement (manufacturing exclusion): Add manufacturing/hardware exclusion rules for "test engineer", "reliability engineer", and "automation engineer" -- exclude when title lacks software/code qualifiers and description contains manufacturing/hardware signals.

  9. Data quality: Investigate reversed-title artifacts in embedding_high tier (e.g., "ENGINEER SYSTEMS 3"). These likely reflect upstream ingestion issues.
