docs.title: T05: Cross-Dataset Comparability
docs.text: Source: data/unified.parquet (Stage 11, 99 columns, ~1.40M rows) Date: 2026-04-05 Sample: SWE postings, LinkedIn, English, date_flag=ok Counts: Arshkon 5,019 | Asaniczka 23,213 | Scraped 24,095

docs.title: Summary of Findings
docs.text:
All three datasets show statistically significant differences on every metric tested. However, the within-2024 calibration (arshkon vs asaniczka) establishes that much of this variability is attributable to data collection method artifacts rather than labor market changes. The critical artifact-vs-signal decomposition identifies three categories:

  1. Clear artifacts: Description length differences (scraper captures more text), asaniczka's missing entry-level labels (source only exports mid-senior/associate native labels)
  2. Mixed artifact + signal: Company composition (different sampling frames), geographic distribution (scraper uses metro-targeted queries)
  3. Likely real signal: Entry-level share increase in scraped vs arshkon (survives within-company analysis and company capping in T06), industry distribution shifts

docs.title: KS Test Results
docs.text: Pair KS stat (description_length) p-value KS stat (core_length) p-value arshkon vs asaniczka 0.1841 6.05e-123 0.0500 2.02e-09 arshkon vs scraped 0.3348 < 1e-300 0.3340 < 1e-300 asaniczka vs scraped 0.2082 < 1e-300 0.3777 < 1e-300

docs.title: Summary Statistics
docs.text: Source Column Mean Median Arshkon description_length 3,316 2,954 Asaniczka description_length 4,052 3,825 Scraped description_length 5,203 5,158 Arshkon core_length 2,738 2,504 Asaniczka core_length 2,696 2,419 Scraped core_length 4,314 4,171

docs.title: Artifact Assessment
docs.text:
Verdict: Primarily artifact. The scraped data captures systematically longer descriptions (+57% median vs arshkon, +35% vs asaniczka). This likely reflects differences in how the scraper extracts full HTML content versus Kaggle datasets which may have been truncated or stripped. The core_length difference is even more dramatic (scraped median 4,171 vs arshkon 2,504), suggesting the boilerplate removal pipeline also behaves differently on longer input text. The within-2024 calibration shows arshkon vs asaniczka already differ significantly (KS=0.18), establishing a baseline of collection-method variability.

Implication for analysis: Do not compare raw text length across datasets. Normalize any text-based metrics by document length within each dataset.

docs.text: Pair Set A size Set B size Intersection Jaccard arshkon vs asaniczka 2,298 4,946 741 0.1139 arshkon vs scraped 2,298 5,100 813 0.1235 asaniczka vs scraped 4,946 5,100 1,167 0.1314

docs.title: Top-50 Company Overlap
docs.text: Pair Companies in common (of 50) arshkon vs asaniczka 12 arshkon vs scraped 17 asaniczka vs scraped 13

docs.title: Top-20 Companies by Source
docs.text:
Arshkon (Apr 2024): DataAnnotation (163), Capital One (115), Apex Systems (52), Motion Recruitment (42), TCS (39), Amazon (32), Open Systems Technologies (31), Fidelity (29), Experis (26), Compunnel (26), ICE (26), Insight Global (25), Akkodis (25), CodeMonk (25), TEKsystems (24), Actalent (23), Deloitte (22), myGwork (22), Discover (21), Robert Half (20)

Scraped (Mar 2026): Jobs via Dice (903), Google (520), JPMorgan Chase (479), Deloitte (345), PwC (235), Leidos (226), Meta (205), Amazon (205), AWS (204), Booz Allen Hamilton (198), Affirm (192), YO IT Consulting (191), Capital One (176), Scribd (174), Epic (163), Wells Fargo (162), General Motors (155), Anduril (155), Microsoft (138), Actalent (128)

docs.title: Artifact Assessment
docs.text:
Verdict: Mixed artifact + real churn. All three pairwise Jaccard similarities are low (~0.11-0.13), and notably the within-2024 comparison (arshkon vs asaniczka = 0.1139) is essentially the same as the cross-period comparison (arshkon vs scraped = 0.1235). This means most of the company set difference is due to sampling frame differences (different search queries, date windows, geographic targets), not labor market churn between 2024 and 2026.

Interestingly, arshkon vs scraped top-50 overlap (17/50) is actually higher than arshkon vs asaniczka (12/50), suggesting the largest SWE employers are stable over time despite the broader company set churning. The appearance of FAANG companies (Google, Meta, Microsoft) and defense/consulting firms (Leidos, Booz Allen, Deloitte) in the scraped top-20 -- while arshkon's top-20 is dominated by staffing firms -- reflects the scraped data's metro-targeted design picking up more corporate employers.

docs.title: Chi-Squared on State Shares (Top 20 States)
docs.text: Pair Chi-squared p-value dof arshkon vs asaniczka 277.68 7.33e-48 19 arshkon vs scraped 279.78 2.74e-48 19 asaniczka vs scraped 1,809.40 < 1e-300 19

docs.title: Artifact Assessment
docs.text:
Verdict: Primarily artifact. The scraped dataset uses a 26-metro targeted search design, meaning it oversamples specific metro areas relative to a national random sample. The within-2024 calibration shows arshkon vs asaniczka already differ significantly (chi2=277.68), establishing that geographic composition varies even within the same year across data sources.

The asaniczka vs scraped difference is an order of magnitude larger (chi2=1,809) than the arshkon vs asaniczka baseline (chi2=278), likely reflecting the scraped data's metro-targeted design combined with asaniczka's broad national sampling. The arshkon vs scraped difference (chi2=280) is similar to the within-2024 baseline, suggesting arshkon and scraped have more similar geographic composition despite the 2-year gap.

Implication: Geographic comparisons across datasets should use metro-level fixed effects or restrict to common metros. Do not interpret geographic share shifts as labor market migration without controlling for sampling design.

docs.title: Chi-Squared Pairwise Tests
docs.text: Pair Chi-squared p-value arshkon vs asaniczka 4,023.56 < 1e-300 arshkon vs scraped 202.28 1.36e-43 asaniczka vs scraped 3,511.59 < 1e-300

docs.title: Entry-Level Label Provenance
docs.text:
Source native_backfill title_keyword description_explicit Total Arshkon 729 100 1 830 Scraped 2,592 661 2 3,255

Both datasets derive most entry-level labels from the same mechanism (LinkedIn's native seniority label backfill), making the comparison methodologically sound.

docs.title: Artifact Assessment
docs.text:
Verdict: Asaniczka seniority is a confirmed labeling artifact. The arshkon-scraped difference contains real signal.

Asaniczka: The Kaggle export only contains two native seniority labels: mid-senior (21,199) and associate (2,014). The 130 entry-level labels come entirely from title_keyword imputation. This is a confirmed artifact of the data source, not the 2024 labor market. Asaniczka must be excluded from all seniority-stratified analyses.

Arshkon vs Scraped: Entry share drops from 20.4% to 14.5%, a 5.9 percentage point decline. Both datasets use the same label generation mechanism (native_backfill as primary source), and both have native seniority coverage in the 67-69% range. The label provenance is comparable, making this a meaningful comparison.

However, this decline could reflect company composition effects (different companies in each sample) rather than a market-wide trend. T06's within-company analysis provides a cleaner test and finds the direction reverses: within overlap companies, entry share is higher in arshkon (25.0%) than scraped (13.2%).

docs.title: Jaccard Similarity of title_normalized Sets
docs.text: Pair Set A Set B Intersection Jaccard Unique to A Unique to B arshkon vs asaniczka 2,161 7,557 449 0.0484 1,712 7,108 arshkon vs scraped 2,161 9,894 420 0.0361 1,741 9,474 asaniczka vs scraped 7,557 9,894 695 0.0415 6,862 9,199

docs.title: Artifact Assessment
docs.text:
Verdict: Primarily artifact (vocabulary fragmentation). Title Jaccard is very low across all pairs (~0.04-0.05), and the within-2024 calibration (arshkon vs asaniczka = 0.0484) is actually higher than cross-period comparisons. Job titles are highly fragmented because companies use unique phrasing (e.g., "senior software engineer - platform" vs "sr. swe, platform team"), making set-based overlap a poor measure of structural change. Title normalization reduces but does not eliminate this fragmentation.

The low Jaccard across all pairs (including within-2024) means title vocabulary overlap is not informative about labor market evolution. Frequency-weighted or embedding-based comparisons would be more appropriate.

Titles unique to each source are saved to exploration/tables/T05/titles_unique_to_*.csv.

docs.title: 6. Industry Distribution (Arshkon vs Scraped Only)
docs.text: Asaniczka has no company_industry data (0% coverage). Only arshkon (99.3% via companion join) and scraped (100% via LinkedIn) are compared.

docs.title: Chi-Squared (Top 15 Industries)
docs.text: Chi-squared = 1,517.58, p < 1e-300, dof = 14

docs.title: Artifact Assessment
docs.text:
Verdict: Mixed. Industry labels come from different pipelines (arshkon: Kaggle companion data join; scraped: direct LinkedIn company industry field), so label alignment is imperfect. However, both sources use LinkedIn's industry taxonomy and achieve >99% coverage. Observed shifts in industry composition likely reflect a combination of real structural change (e.g., defense/government-tech growth in 2026) and sampling composition differences (metro-targeted scraping favoring different industries).

The full industry distribution is saved to exploration/tables/T05/industry_distribution.csv.

docs.title: 7. Artifact Diagnostic Summary
docs.text:
Metric Within-2024 Baseline Cross-Period (arshkon vs scraped) Excess Over Baseline Assessment Description length (KS) 0.1841 0.3348 +0.1507 Artifact -- scraper captures more text Core length (KS) 0.0500 0.3340 +0.2840 Artifact -- boilerplate removal scales with input length Company Jaccard 0.1139 0.1235 +0.0096 Artifact -- sampling frame difference, not employer churn Geographic chi-squared 277.68 279.78 +2.10 Artifact -- metro-targeted vs national sampling Seniority chi-squared 4,023.56* 202.28 N/A Signal -- asaniczka baseline invalid due to label gap Title Jaccard 0.0484 0.0361 -0.0123 Artifact -- title fragmentation is inherently high Remote flag 0.0% (both 2024) 22.6% (scraped) N/A Artifact -- Kaggle sources lack remote data

*Asaniczka baseline is not a valid calibration for seniority due to missing entry-level labels.

docs.title: Five Key Artifacts Identified
docs.text:
1. Description length: The scraped data captures systematically longer descriptions (+57% median vs arshkon). This is a scraping method artifact. All text-based analyses must normalize within-source.

  2. Asaniczka seniority labeling: Asaniczka's export only contains mid-senior and associate native labels. Its 130 entry-level SWE labels come from title keyword imputation only. Asaniczka cannot serve as a seniority baseline.

  3. Company composition: Low Jaccard (~0.12) between all pairs including within-2024 means the company sets are largely non-overlapping due to sampling design. Cross-dataset comparisons must use within-company designs or cap company contributions.

  4. Geographic sampling: The scraped data uses 26-metro targeted searches, creating geographic composition differences by design. Metro-level fixed effects are needed for cross-dataset geographic comparisons.

  5. Remote flag coverage: Kaggle sources have zero remote flag data, while scraped shows 22.6% remote. Remote/hybrid trend analysis is impossible across periods without imputation from text.

docs.title: 8. Within-2024 Calibration Table
docs.text:
This table compares arshkon vs asaniczka (both 2024, different collectors) to establish baseline cross-source variability, then tests whether arshkon vs scraped (cross-period) exceeds that baseline.

Metric Arshkon vs Asaniczka (within-2024) Arshkon vs Scraped (cross-period) Exceeds Baseline? description_length KS 0.1841 (p < 1e-122) 0.3348 (p < 1e-300) Yes -- collection artifact core_length KS 0.0500 (p = 2e-9) 0.3340 (p < 1e-300) Yes -- collection artifact Company Jaccard 0.1139 0.1235 No -- within noise Geographic chi-sq 277.68 (p = 7e-48) 279.78 (p = 3e-48) No -- within noise Seniority chi-sq 4,023.56* 202.28 N/A -- baseline invalid Title Jaccard 0.0484 0.0361 No -- within noise

docs.title: Calibration Verdict
docs.text:
The arshkon-asaniczka baseline reveals that most observable differences across datasets are attributable to data collection method, not temporal change. The two metrics where cross-period effects exceed the within-2024 baseline (description length, core length) are clearly scraping artifacts. Company overlap, geographic composition, and title vocabulary are all at or below baseline levels.

The one domain where the within-2024 calibration cannot speak is seniority, because asaniczka's label deficit makes it an invalid calibration partner. For seniority, the arshkon-to-scraped comparison stands on its own, supported by comparable label provenance mechanisms in both datasets. The entry share difference (20.4% vs 14.5%) is the most promising candidate for real labor market signal, though it must be further tested through within-company designs (see T06).

docs.title: T06: Company Concentration
docs.text: Source: data/unified.parquet (Stage 11, 99 columns, ~1.40M rows) Date: 2026-04-05 Sample: SWE postings, LinkedIn, English, date_flag=ok Counts: Arshkon 5,019 | Asaniczka 23,213 | Scraped 24,095

docs.title: Summary of Findings
docs.text:
Company posting concentration varies significantly across datasets. Asaniczka is the most concentrated (Gini=0.71, top-5 share=21.9%), driven by staffing aggregators like Recruiting from Scratch (7.3%) and Jobs for Humanity. Arshkon is the least concentrated (Gini=0.47, top-5=8.3%). Scraped falls in between (Gini=0.69, top-5=10.3%).

Key findings: 1. Aggregators are the main concentration driver. Asaniczka has 27.3% aggregator share vs 11.8% arshkon and 14.6% scraped. Aggregator postings have systematically different properties (shorter descriptions, higher entry-level rates). 2. Within-company analysis shows entry share declining from 2024 to 2026 in overlap companies (25.0% arshkon to 13.2% scraped), the opposite direction from the raw cross-dataset comparison. 3. Company-capping has minimal effect on seniority distributions, confirming that concentration does not drive the observed seniority differences. 4. 70% of 2026 companies are new relative to both 2024 datasets, accounting for 43% of postings.

docs.title: 1. Concentration Metrics
docs.text:
Metric Arshkon (Apr 2024) Asaniczka (Jan 2024) Scraped (Mar 2026) Number of companies 2,298 4,946 5,100 Total postings 4,971* 23,213 24,095 HHI (x10,000) 29.25 139.92 41.84 Top-1 share (%) 3.28 7.32 3.75 Top-5 share (%) 8.26 21.86 10.30 Top-10 share (%) 11.64 28.42 16.37 Top-20 share (%) 15.84 31.43 21.39 Gini coefficient 0.4662 0.7096 0.6892

*Arshkon total excludes 48 rows with null company_name_canonical.

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
All three datasets show moderate concentration (no HHI exceeds 140/10,000), well below what would indicate monopolistic posting behavior. However, asaniczka's concentration is driven almost entirely by staffing aggregators: Recruiting from Scratch alone accounts for 7.3% of all SWE postings. After excluding aggregators, concentration would drop substantially.

The scraped data's Gini (0.69) is close to asaniczka's (0.71) despite lower top-k shares, indicating a long tail of small companies alongside moderately large employers.

docs.title: 2. Companies with >3% SWE Posting Share
docs.text: Source Company Postings Share (%) Arshkon DataAnnotation 163 3.28 Asaniczka Recruiting from Scratch 1,699 7.32 Asaniczka Capital One 1,679 7.23 Asaniczka ClearanceJobs 920 3.96 Scraped Jobs via Dice 903 3.75

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• Only one company exceeds 3% in any single dataset (aside from aggregators). In asaniczka, Capital One (7.2%) is the sole non-aggregator above 3%, inflated by its high job posting volume.
  • In arshkon, DataAnnotation (3.3%) is a crowdsourcing/data-labeling platform, essentially an aggregator.
  • In scraped, Jobs via Dice (3.8%) is the only dominant entity, clearly an aggregator.
  • No organic employer exceeds 3% in either arshkon or scraped. This is reassuring for generalizability.

docs.title: 3. Within-Company Seniority Comparison (Overlap Set)
docs.text: Restricted to the 110 companies with at least 5 SWE postings in both arshkon and scraped.

docs.title: Aggregate Seniority in Overlap Companies
docs.text: Seniority Arshkon n Arshkon % Scraped n Scraped % entry 316 25.0 657 13.2 associate 50 4.0 134 2.7 mid-senior 892 70.6 4,124 83.0 director 6 0.5 55 1.1

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
This is the cleanest available test of seniority change. By restricting to the same companies across periods, we control for company composition effects. The results show:

  • Entry share declines from 25.0% to 13.2% in overlap companies, a 11.8 percentage point drop. This is larger than the raw cross-dataset decline (20.4% to 14.5%) and strongly suggests a real decrease in entry-level SWE postings within established companies.
  • Mid-senior share increases from 70.6% to 83.0%, absorbing the entry-level decline.
  • Associate and director shares are small and relatively stable.

Caveats: The overlap set (110 companies, 1,264 arshkon postings, 4,970 scraped postings) is small relative to the full datasets. Overlap companies are biased toward larger, more established firms that maintained hiring across both periods. This design cannot capture new-entrant effects.

The full within-company seniority table is saved to exploration/tables/T06/within_company_seniority.csv.

docs.title: 4. Company-Capped Sensitivity Analysis
docs.text: Seniority distribution after capping each company at 10 postings, to test whether a few high-volume companies drive the observed distributions.

docs.title: Results (Excluding Unknown Seniority)
docs.text: Source Method Entry % Associate % Mid-Senior % Director % Total Arshkon Uncapped 20.48 5.55 73.43 0.54 4,039 Arshkon Capped (10) 19.05 6.12 74.25 0.57 3,511 Asaniczka Uncapped 0.56 6.25 92.80 0.39 23,213 Asaniczka Capped (10) 0.68 8.07 91.05 0.19 11,939 Scraped Uncapped 14.49 2.77 81.76 0.99 22,469 Scraped Capped (10) 13.77 3.12 82.19 0.92 13,075

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
Company capping has minimal effect on seniority distributions. The entry share changes are small: - Arshkon: 20.48% to 19.05% (-1.4pp) - Scraped: 14.49% to 13.77% (-0.7pp)

The arshkon-to-scraped entry share gap narrows slightly (5.9pp uncapped to 5.3pp capped) but remains substantial and significant. This confirms that the seniority difference is not driven by a few high-volume companies posting disproportionately at one level.

docs.title: Aggregator Share by Source
docs.text: Source Aggregator Postings Total Aggregator Share Arshkon 591 5,019 11.8% Asaniczka 6,340 23,213 27.3% Scraped 3,517 24,095 14.6%

docs.title: Top Aggregators by Source
docs.text:
Arshkon: DataAnnotation (163), Dice (62), Apex Systems (52), Motion Recruitment (42), TCS (39)

Asaniczka: Recruiting from Scratch (2,084), Jobs for Humanity (1,839), ClearanceJobs (930), ClickJobs.io (483), Dice (286)

Scraped: Jobs via Dice (1,966), Lensa (337), Actalent (128), Motion Recruitment (100), Jobot (97)

docs.title: Aggregator vs Non-Aggregator Profile
docs.text: Source Type n Mean Desc Length Entry % (of known) Arshkon Aggregator 591 2,439 42.8% Arshkon Non-aggregator 4,428 3,433 17.6% Asaniczka Aggregator 6,340 4,411 0.2% Asaniczka Non-aggregator 16,873 3,917 0.7% Scraped Aggregator 3,517 4,135 26.7% Scraped Non-aggregator 20,578 5,386 12.3%

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
1. Aggregator postings are systematically different. They have shorter descriptions (by ~1,000 chars in arshkon, ~1,250 in scraped) and much higher entry-level rates (42.8% vs 17.6% in arshkon, 26.7% vs 12.3% in scraped).

  2. Aggregators inflate entry-level counts. In arshkon, aggregators account for 11.8% of postings but produce 42.8% entry-level (among their known-seniority rows). This means aggregator handling is critical for accurate seniority analysis.

  3. Asaniczka aggregator anomaly: Asaniczka has 27.3% aggregator share -- more than double the other sources. Its top aggregators (Recruiting from Scratch, Jobs for Humanity) are not prominent in arshkon or scraped. This likely reflects asaniczka's broader search terms capturing more staffing-firm postings.

  4. The aggregator composition difference between sources is itself a potential confounder. Since aggregators skew entry-level, the different aggregator shares (11.8% arshkon vs 14.6% scraped) could partially explain entry-level rate differences. Seniority analyses should be run both with and without aggregator postings.

docs.title: Company Overlap Between 2026 and 2024
docs.text: Metric Value Scraped unique companies 5,100 New vs arshkon only 4,287 (84.1%) New vs all 2024 (arshkon + asaniczka) 3,561 (69.8%) New entrant postings (vs arshkon) 13,545 (56.2% of scraped) New entrant postings (vs all 2024) 10,329 (42.9% of scraped)

docs.title: New Entrant Seniority Profile (vs Arshkon)
docs.text: Seniority n Share mid-senior 10,539 80.5% entry 2,084 15.9% associate 346 2.6% director 129 1.0%

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
The majority of companies in 2026 are "new" relative to 2024 data, but this reflects sampling frame differences (see T05, Section 2) more than genuine market entry. With company Jaccard of only 0.12 between arshkon and scraped, most of the apparent "new entrants" are simply companies that existed in 2024 but were not captured by the arshkon Kaggle scrape.

New entrant seniority (15.9% entry among known) is comparable to the overall scraped entry rate (14.5%), indicating no systematic seniority skew among companies not appearing in 2024 data. The 43% posting share from new-vs-all-2024 companies means nearly half the scraped sample comes from companies we have no historical comparison for, limiting the power of within-company designs.

docs.title: Overall Assessment
docs.text:
Company concentration is moderate and does not threaten analysis validity, provided standard precautions are taken:

  1. No single organic employer dominates. The highest non-aggregator share is Capital One at 7.2% in asaniczka, which is exceptional. In arshkon and scraped, no organic employer exceeds 3%.

  2. Aggregators are the primary concentration risk. They have systematically different characteristics (shorter text, higher entry rates). Analyses should be run with and without aggregators as a robustness check.

  3. Company-capping has minimal effect. The 10-posting cap barely changes seniority distributions, confirming broad-based patterns.

  4. Within-company analysis is the gold standard. The 110-company overlap set shows a clear entry-level decline (25% to 13%), controlling for company composition. This is the most credible estimate of seniority shift.

docs.title: Recommended Robustness Checks for Downstream Analysis
docs.text:
• Run all seniority analyses with and without aggregator postings
  • Report within-company results alongside cross-dataset comparisons
  • Cap company contributions at 10-20 postings for sensitivity testing
  • Flag that asaniczka cannot participate in seniority-based analyses

  • new_entrants.csv -- new company counts and shares

docs.title: T07: External Benchmarks & Power Analysis
docs.text: Generated: 2026-04-05 Data source: preprocessing/intermediate/stage8_final.parquet (Stage 8) Default filters: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok'

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
Our LinkedIn SWE posting samples show strong geographic representativeness (Pearson r = 0.97 for arshkon, r = 0.98 for scraped vs BLS OES state-level employment). Industry distributions over-represent Professional/Technical Services relative to BLS OES, consistent with LinkedIn's user base skew. JOLTS information sector openings were substantially lower during our 2024 collection periods than peak 2022 levels, and had partially recovered by early 2026.

Power analysis verdict: The primary comparisons (entry-level arshkon vs scraped, senior, and all SWE) are well-powered for detecting small-to-moderate effects (d = 0.04-0.11). Metro-level analyses are feasible for 8-18 metros depending on the minimum threshold. Company-panel analysis is well-powered in aggregate (202 companies, 8,948 postings) but underpowered at the individual-company level. The arshkon-asaniczka entry-level comparison (n=830 vs n=130) and arshkon-scraped mid-level comparison (n=228 vs n=622) are adequate but not generous.

docs.title: 1.1 State-Level Correlation with BLS OES
docs.text:
We compared our state-level SWE posting counts against BLS OES May 2023 employment estimates for SOC 15-1252 (Software Developers).

Dataset N states Pearson r Spearman rho Log-scale Pearson r Arshkon (2024-04) 47 0.969 0.949 0.961 Scraped (2026-03) 31 0.983 0.896 0.796

Interpretation: - Arshkon covers 47 of 51 states/DC (missing HI, SD, VT, WY), representing 99.5% of BLS SWE employment. Its geographic distribution is an excellent match to BLS OES at both raw and log scale. - Scraped covers only 31 states because the scraper targets 26 metros, not all states. Among covered states, raw correlation is slightly higher (r=0.983) but log-scale correlation is lower (0.796) because the scraper's metro-targeted design produces sharper concentration in tech-hub states. The 20 missing states represent 7.6% of BLS SWE employment. - The scraped data's lower log-scale r reflects its metro-targeted sampling design: it oversamples high-employment states (CA, TX, WA, NY) relative to small states. This is by design, not a defect, but it means scraped cannot support state-level analyses for smaller states.

Target met: Both datasets exceed the r > 0.80 geographic benchmark by substantial margins.

docs.title: 1.2 State Coverage Details
docs.text:
Top 10 states by BLS OES SWE employment and our coverage:

State BLS OES Arshkon Scraped Arshkon share Scraped share CA 293,760 620 5,128 0.21% 1.75% TX 161,230 475 2,689 0.29% 1.67% WA 108,040 193 1,742 0.18% 1.61% NY 89,050 258 1,753 0.29% 1.97% VA 81,140 292 1,167 0.36% 1.44% MA 71,590 127 1,164 0.18% 1.63% FL 68,560 142 541 0.21% 0.79% IL 63,470 177 878 0.28% 1.38% GA 59,330 160 868 0.27% 1.46% NC 52,910 145 1,157 0.27% 2.19%

The scraped data captures a substantially higher share of BLS employment per state than arshkon, consistent with the scraper being designed for higher per-metro volume. The "share" column shows our sample represents ~0.2-0.4% (arshkon) and ~0.8-2.2% (scraped) of BLS employment by state — reasonable for a posting-level sample.

Figure: exploration/figures/T07/geographic_correlation.png — log-scale scatter plots of our counts vs BLS OES by state.

docs.title: 2.1 Comparison with BLS OES Industry Distribution
docs.text:
BLS OES reports SWE employment by NAICS sector. LinkedIn uses its own industry taxonomy, so the comparison requires approximate mapping. The mapping below is rough but directional:

Sector (BLS NAICS-equivalent) BLS OES (%) Arshkon (%) Scraped (%) Professional/Technical Services 37.2 46.1 44.3 Finance & Insurance 11.8 12.5 9.7 Manufacturing 10.5 3.7 4.4 Information 9.8 4.8 3.1 Healthcare 2.8 1.6 1.3 Retail/Wholesale 3.2 1.5 0.8 Staffing (no BLS equiv.) — 5.5 2.5 Other 20.6 24.3 33.8

Key observations: - Our data over-represents Professional/Technical Services by ~7-9 percentage points relative to BLS OES. This is expected: LinkedIn's user base skews toward tech, consulting, and professional services. - Manufacturing and Information sectors are under-represented in our data (~3-5% vs ~10% in BLS OES), likely because many manufacturing-sector SWE roles are posted on different platforms or through internal channels. - Finance & Insurance representation is close to BLS OES. - Staffing/recruiting postings (5.5% arshkon, 2.5% scraped) have no BLS OES equivalent — these are aggregator postings that BLS would count under the actual employer's industry. - The "Other" category is large in scraped (33.8%) partly because LinkedIn's industry labels are finer-grained and many don't map cleanly to a single NAICS sector.

Implication: Our sample is most representative of Professional/Technical Services SWE. Findings about manufacturing or information-sector SWE should be treated with caution due to underrepresentation.

Figure: exploration/figures/T07/industry_comparison.png

docs.title: 3.1 JOLTS Trends and Our Data Periods
docs.text:
We retrieved JOLTS seasonally adjusted information sector job openings (Series JTS510000000000000JOL) from the BLS public API for 2022-2026.

Period JOLTS Info Openings (000s) Our data 2022 peak (Apr) 274 — 2022 trough (Dec) 80 — 2024-01 (asaniczka) 160 23,213 SWE 2024-04 (arshkon) 87 5,019 SWE 2025 avg 120 — 2026-01 100 — 2026-02 91 — 2026-03 (scraped) ~95 (est.) 24,095 SWE

Key observations: - The information sector experienced a massive contraction from 274K openings (Apr 2022) to 80K (Dec 2022), consistent with the widely reported tech hiring freeze. - Our asaniczka period (Jan 2024, 160K) captures a partial recovery; the arshkon period (Apr 2024, 87K) captures a secondary dip. - By early 2026, JOLTS information sector openings appear to have partially stabilized around 90-100K, still well below the 2022 peak. - Our scraped data collection (March 2026) occurred during a period of subdued but stable information-sector hiring. This means our 2026 sample is not from a hiring boom, reducing the risk that composition shifts are driven by cyclical expansion.

docs.title: 3.2 JOLTS vs Our Daily Counts
docs.text:
The scraped data has limited date_posted coverage for LinkedIn (only 2.8% of rows), so direct daily comparison with JOLTS is not feasible. However, scrape_date shows our daily SWE yield of 865-3,047 postings across 12 scrape days in March 2026, consistent with stable scraping activity rather than variation in underlying demand.

Figure: exploration/figures/T07/jolts_information_sector.png

docs.title: 4.1 What Population Does Our Sample Represent?
docs.text: Dimension Coverage Representativeness Platform LinkedIn only (primary), Indeed (sensitivity) Represents LinkedIn-posted SWE roles. Excludes company career pages, Greenhouse/Lever ATS-only postings, and non-LinkedIn platforms. Geography 47 states (arshkon), 31 states (scraped), 26-metro frame Excellent correlation with BLS OES state distribution. Scraped concentrated in major metros by design. Industry Professional/Technical Services over-represented LinkedIn skews toward tech-adjacent employers. Manufacturing and information-sector SWE underrepresented. Company size Large employers over-represented in scraped (Google, JPMorgan, Deloitte dominate) LinkedIn favors established companies with active recruiting presence. Seniority 81% known (arshkon), ~100% known (asaniczka, scraped) for SWE Good coverage, but asaniczka has zero entry-level labels. Entry-level baseline relies solely on arshkon. Time Snapshots at Jan 2024, Apr 2024, Mar 2026 Not continuous panel. Cross-sectional comparisons only. Cyclical effects not separable from structural shifts.

docs.title: 4.2 What We Can and Cannot Generalize
docs.text:
Can generalize to: - LinkedIn-posted SWE roles in major US metros - Posting content and requirement trends among established employers - Relative geographic distribution of SWE demand - Cross-period changes in posting composition at the aggregate level

Cannot generalize to: - All SWE jobs (many are never posted on LinkedIn) - Small companies and startups (underrepresented) - Manufacturing-sector or government SWE roles - International markets - Actual employment (postings measure labor demand signals, not hires) - Individual-company trajectories (panel is too sparse for most companies)

docs.title: 4.3 Key Confounds for Cross-Period Comparison
docs.text:
1. Description length grew ~56% from 2024 to 2026 (median 3,316 to 5,181 chars for SWE). Any text-based measure MUST be normalized by description length.
  2. Company overlap is only ~18% between arshkon and scraped. Composition effects from different company mixes could drive observed differences.
  3. JOLTS context: The arshkon period (Apr 2024, 87K openings) was a relative trough; the scraped period (Mar 2026, ~95K) was slightly higher but still subdued. Cyclical position differs modestly.
  4. Seniority label provenance differs: arshkon entry-level labels are 88% native_backfill vs 80% in scraped. Label construction should be checked for artifacts.

docs.title: 5.1 Method
docs.text: Minimum detectable effect sizes (MDE) computed for two-sample comparisons at 80% power, alpha = 0.05 (two-tailed). - Binary outcomes (e.g., presence of a keyword): MDE for difference in proportions, using normal approximation. - Continuous outcomes (e.g., embedding similarity, YOE): MDE expressed as Cohen's d.

docs.title: 5.2 Cross-Period Comparisons
docs.text:
Comparison n1 n2 MDE (d) MDE (prop, base=50%) MDE (prop, base=10%) Verdict Entry-level: arshkon vs scraped 830 3,255 0.109 5.4 pp 3.3 pp well-powered Senior: arshkon vs scraped 3,008 18,592 0.055 2.8 pp 1.7 pp well-powered All SWE: arshkon vs scraped 5,019 24,095 0.044 2.2 pp 1.3 pp well-powered Entry: arshkon vs asaniczka 830 130 0.264 13.2 pp 7.9 pp adequate Mid-level: arshkon vs scraped 228 622 0.217 10.8 pp 6.5 pp adequate Senior: pooled 2024 vs scraped 24,641 18,592 0.027 1.4 pp 0.8 pp well-powered All SWE: pooled 2024 vs scraped 28,232 24,095 0.025 1.2 pp 0.7 pp well-powered Entry (native_backfill only) 729 2,592 0.118 5.7 pp 3.5 pp well-powered

Key takeaways: - The primary entry-level comparison (arshkon vs scraped) can detect effects as small as d=0.11, or a 3.3 pp shift in a 10%-prevalence keyword. This is sufficient for the hypothesized scope-inflation effects. - Pooling asaniczka + arshkon for 2024 dramatically improves power for senior and all-SWE comparisons (d = 0.025-0.027), but pooling is only valid if source composition effects are controlled for (since asaniczka and arshkon differ in company mix, seniority labels, and description length). - The arshkon-asaniczka entry-level comparison (n=130 asaniczka) is adequate but only for large effects. Asaniczka entry-level labels are rule-imputed (130 rows) and should be treated as a cross-check, not a primary analysis. - The mid-level comparison (n=228 arshkon mid) is the weakest cross-period comparison, with MDE of d=0.22. Only substantial effects will be detectable.

docs.title: 5.3 Interpretation of Effect Sizes
docs.text:
For context, what do these MDE values mean in practical terms?

MDE (Cohen's d) Meaning for continuous outcomes Meaning for binary (base=20%) 0.05 ~0.05 SD shift in embedding similarity ~1.7 pp shift 0.10 ~0.10 SD shift ~3.3 pp shift 0.20 ~0.20 SD shift (small effect) ~6.3 pp shift 0.50 ~0.50 SD shift (medium effect) ~14.6 pp shift

The hypothesized effects (scope inflation, archetype shift) are expected to be moderate (d = 0.20-0.50), so all primary comparisons are adequately powered.

docs.title: 6.1 Metro Coverage
docs.text:
Of the 26-metro study frame, metros with SWE postings in both arshkon and scraped:

Threshold Metros meeting threshold Both >= 50 SWE 18 Both >= 100 SWE 8 Both >= 200 SWE 4 (SF Bay, DC, Seattle, Dallas)

docs.title: 6.2 Top Metros for Cross-Period Analysis
docs.text:
Metro Arshkon Scraped MDE (d) Verdict San Francisco Bay Area 403 3,000 0.149 well-powered Washington DC Metro 298 1,606 0.177 well-powered Dallas-Fort Worth 272 1,200 0.188 well-powered Seattle 189 1,433 0.217 adequate Chicago 176 708 0.236 adequate Atlanta 148 694 0.254 adequate Boston 140 969 0.253 adequate Los Angeles 130 938 0.262 adequate

Note: New York City Metro is unusable for arshkon comparison (n=13 arshkon). This is a significant gap — NYC is the 2nd largest US tech market but has minimal representation in arshkon. For NYC-specific analyses, asaniczka (n=89) provides some data but is still thin.

docs.title: 6.3 Recommendation
docs.text:
• Tier 1 metros (well-powered, d < 0.20): SF Bay Area, Washington DC, Dallas-Fort Worth — use for metro-specific effect estimation.
  • Tier 2 metros (adequate, d = 0.20-0.30): Seattle, Chicago, Atlanta, Boston, Los Angeles — use for metro-level robustness checks.
  • Metro-pooled analysis with metro fixed effects is feasible across all 18 metros with >= 50 SWE per period.

docs.title: 7.1 Overlap Statistics
docs.text: Metric Value Unique companies with SWE postings (arshkon) 2,298 Unique companies with SWE postings (scraped) 5,100 Overlapping companies 813 (35.4% of arshkon, 15.9% of scraped) Companies with >= 3 SWE in BOTH periods 202 Total postings in panel (arshkon side) 1,875 Total postings in panel (scraped side) 7,073 Avg postings per company (arshkon) 9.3 Avg postings per company (scraped) 35.0

docs.title: 7.2 Top Panel Companies
docs.text: Company Arshkon SWE Scraped SWE Capital One 115 176 DataAnnotation 163 38 Apex Systems 52 86 Motion Recruitment 42 100 Deloitte 22 345 Amazon 32 205 Google 15 520 Microsoft 19 138 JPMorgan Chase 12 479 Wells Fargo 16 162

docs.title: 7.3 Panel Power Analysis
docs.text:
• Aggregate panel (1,875 vs 7,073 postings): MDE d = 0.073, well-powered. This is T16's primary design — comparing posting content within the same company across periods.
  • Individual company (~9 vs ~35 postings): MDE d = 1.05, underpowered. Within-company analyses at the single-company level are not feasible for most companies.
  • Recommendation: Use the company panel for within-company fixed-effects analysis at the aggregate level. Company-specific trajectories should be limited to the ~10 largest panel companies (Capital One, Deloitte, Amazon, Google, etc.).

docs.title: 7.4 Panel Composition Concerns
docs.text:
• The panel is dominated by large employers (FAANG, banks, defense contractors, staffing agencies). Small/medium companies rarely have >= 3 SWE postings in both periods.
  • Staffing agencies (Motion Recruitment, Apex Systems, Actalent) contribute significantly. These may reflect labor-market intermediary behavior rather than direct employer restructuring.
  • DataAnnotation's high arshkon count (163) but low scraped count (38) likely reflects a specific recruitment campaign, not a structural trend.

docs.title: 8. Comprehensive Feasibility Table
docs.text:
The full feasibility table is saved at exploration/tables/T07/feasibility_table.csv.

Summary by verdict:

Verdict Count Examples Well-powered (MDE d <= 0.20) 12 All primary cross-period, SF/DC/Dallas metros, aggregate company panel, seniority share Adequate (MDE d = 0.20-0.35) 7 Entry arshkon-asaniczka, mid-level, Seattle/Chicago/Atlanta/Boston/LA/Austin metros Marginal (MDE d = 0.35-0.50) 0 — Underpowered (MDE d > 0.50) 2 NYC metro (arshkon n=13), individual company panel

Figure: exploration/figures/T07/power_analysis.png

docs.title: 9.1 Analyses That Should Proceed
docs.text:
1. RQ1 junior scope inflation (entry-level arshkon vs scraped): Well-powered, proceed as planned. The n=830 arshkon entry-level sample is the binding constraint but sufficient for d=0.11 detection.
  2. RQ1 senior archetype shift (senior arshkon vs scraped): Well-powered (d=0.055). Even subtle shifts in requirement language should be detectable.
  3. All-SWE aggregate comparisons: Extremely well-powered. Any planned descriptive analyses at the all-SWE level will have more than sufficient statistical resolution.
  4. Company-panel within-firm analysis (T16): Well-powered in aggregate. Proceed with 202-company panel using company fixed effects.
  5. Metro-level heterogeneity: Feasible for 18 metros (>= 50 per period). Use SF Bay Area, DC, and Dallas as anchor metros.

docs.title: 9.2 Analyses Requiring Caution
docs.text:
1. Mid-level cross-period comparison (n=228 arshkon mid): Adequate power only for moderate effects (d >= 0.22). Consider pooling mid + senior into a "non-junior" category for better power.
  2. Arshkon-asaniczka entry-level comparison (n=130 asaniczka entry): Asaniczka entry-level labels are rule-imputed and small (n=130). Use only as directional cross-check, not primary evidence.
  3. Metro-level entry-level analysis: Not assessed here, but entry-level counts per metro are likely very small. Expect most metro-level entry analyses to be underpowered.

docs.title: 9.3 Analyses That Are Not Feasible
docs.text:
1. NYC-specific arshkon vs scraped (n=13 arshkon): Not viable. NYC comparisons require asaniczka (n=89) or should be dropped.
  2. Individual company trajectories: Only ~10 companies have enough postings for company-specific analysis.
  3. State-level analysis using scraped data for small states: The scraper covers only 31 states; 20 states are missing entirely.

docs.title: 10. Data Quality Notes
docs.text:
• BLS OES data: May 2023 estimates for SOC 15-1252 used as the benchmark. These are survey-based estimates with their own sampling variability, but are the best available external anchor for SWE employment by state.
  • JOLTS data: Retrieved via BLS public API (Series JTS510000000000000JOL). Seasonally adjusted information sector openings in thousands.
  • Industry mapping: The BLS-to-LinkedIn industry mapping is approximate. LinkedIn industry labels are self-reported by companies and do not map cleanly to NAICS sectors. The industry comparison should be treated as directional, not precise.
  • Seniority counts: The "3,255 scraped entry-level" includes both native_backfill (2,592) and title_keyword (661) sources. These are substantially larger than the arshkon entry-level count because the scraper specifically targeted entry-level search queries.
  • Sample size note: The task brief referenced n=574 scraped entry and n=3,003/3,459 for mid-senior. The current stage8_final.parquet yields n=3,255 scraped entry and n=18,592 scraped senior (seniority_3level). This difference is due to data growth from continued scraping. The higher counts only improve power.

docs.title: T08: Distribution Profiling & Anomaly Detection
docs.text: Status: Complete Date: 2026-04-05 Agent: E Data: data/unified.parquet (SWE, LinkedIn, English, date_flag=ok) Script: exploration/scripts/T08_distribution_profiling.py

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
Comprehensive distribution profiling across all key variables reveals three headline findings:

  1. The YOE paradox is RESOLVED: it reflects genuine slot elimination, not a measurement artifact. Entry-level median YOE dropped from 3.0 (arshkon 2024) to 2.0 (scraped 2026), driven by the near-elimination of high-YOE "entry-level" postings (5+ YOE entry share fell from 22.8% to 2.4%). This holds within titles, within seniority provenance, after company capping, and with aggregators excluded. The interpretation: companies that formerly labeled 3-5+ YOE roles as "entry-level" have either eliminated those slots or reclassified them upward, leaving only genuinely junior (0-2 YOE) positions in the entry tier.

  2. AI/ML technology adoption shows massive, calibration-validated increases. Agent frameworks (0.1% to 10.8%), LLMs (1.8% to 14.4%), and Claude/Copilot mentions all exploded. These changes dwarf within-2024 instrument noise by 5-17x, confirming genuine market shifts.

  3. Description length grew 57% (core: 52%) for mid-senior roles, the single largest structural effect (Cohen's d = 0.77). This length inflation means ALL text-derived metrics must be length-normalized.

docs.title: 1.1 Seniority Distributions
docs.text:
seniority_final by period (all SWE):

Period entry associate mid-senior director unknown 2024-01 (asaniczka) 0.6% 6.2% 92.8% 0.4% 0.0% 2024-04 (arshkon) 16.5% 4.5% 59.5% 0.4% 19.0% 2026-03 (scraped) 13.5% 2.6% 76.2% 0.9% 6.7%

Note: Asaniczka's 0.6% entry rate is a known artifact (zero native entry labels; all imputed). Exclude from seniority-stratified analyses.

seniority_3level known-seniority share (arshkon vs scraped):

Period junior mid senior 2024-04 20.4% 5.6% 74.0% 2026-03 14.5% 2.8% 82.7%

Junior share declined -5.9pp (absolute) and -29% (relative). This is consistent with Wave 1 T03 findings.

Using seniority_native directly (no imputation): entry dropped from 22.3% to 14.0% (-8.3pp), also consistent.

docs.title: 1.2 Description Length
docs.text:
Description lengths grew substantially across all seniority tiers:

Period Seniority Median desc_len Median core_len Growth (core) 2024-04 junior 3,678 2,858 baseline 2026-03 junior 4,346 3,466 +21% 2024-04 senior 3,156 2,742 baseline 2026-03 senior 5,289 4,249 +55%

Senior descriptions grew more than junior ones (55% vs 21% at median). Core length (post-boilerplate) grew proportionally, suggesting this is genuine content expansion, not just boilerplate inflation.

Anomaly flags: Moderate positive skew in core_length for asaniczka (skew=1.15) and in YOE across all periods (skew>1.0). No bimodality detected in any univariate distribution.

See: fig1_description_length_by_period.png

docs.title: 1.3 Years of Experience (YOE)
docs.text:
YOE summary by seniority and period:

Period Seniority n (has YOE) Coverage Median Mean 2024-04 junior 476 57.3% 3.0 4.0 2026-03 junior 1,066 32.7% 2.0 2.2 2024-04 mid 129 56.6% 3.0 3.0 2026-03 mid 423 68.0% 3.0 3.7 2024-04 senior 1,989 66.1% 5.0 5.6 2026-03 senior 13,826 74.4% 5.0 5.3

Senior and mid-level YOE are stable. The dramatic change is isolated to entry-level.

YOE extraction coverage dropped for entry-level (57.3% to 32.7%). Investigated in Step 3 -- this is because more 2026 entry posts lack explicit YOE requirements, consistent with genuinely junior roles.

See: fig2_yoe_by_period_seniority.png

docs.title: 1.4 Aggregator Rates
docs.text:
Period Junior agg rate Senior agg rate Overall agg rate 2024-01 9.2% 29.2% 27.3% 2024-04 23.1% 10.3% 11.8% 2026-03 28.2% 13.2% 14.6%

Junior aggregator rates rose substantially (23.1% to 28.2%). Aggregators disproportionately post "entry-level" roles with inflated YOE, but this effect is now smaller because high-YOE entry posts disappeared.

docs.title: 1.5 Metro Areas
docs.text: Top 5 metros are stable across periods: San Francisco, Washington DC, Seattle, Dallas-Fort Worth, and Boston consistently dominate. NYC appears in scraped (ranked 4th) but was not a top-5 in either Kaggle source, likely reflecting scraper search configuration.

docs.title: 1.6 Company Industry
docs.text: Available for arshkon and scraped only (asaniczka = 0%). Compound labels in scraped (e.g., "Accounting, IT Services and IT Consulting, and Business Consulting and Services") need normalization before cross-period comparison. Top categories are stable: Software Development, IT Services/Consulting, Financial Services.

docs.title: 1.7 SWE Classification Tier
docs.text:
Period regex title_lookup_llm embedding_high 2024-01 87.9% 10.4% 1.7% 2024-04 89.2% 9.3% 1.5% 2026-03 94.2% 4.5% 1.3%

The scraped period has a higher regex-tier share. This likely reflects title normalization improvements in scraping, not a real change in role composition. Sensitivity analysis by tier shows the junior share decline holds across all tiers (regex: 20.0% to 14.5%, title_lookup_llm: 25.1% to 17.1%).

docs.title: Flagged Distributions
docs.text:
Variable Period Flag Detail core_length 2024-01 MODERATE_SKEW skew=1.15, kurtosis=1.99 (long right tail) yoe_extracted 2024-04 MODERATE_SKEW skew=1.03, kurtosis=1.60 yoe_extracted 2026-03 MODERATE_SKEW skew=1.21, kurtosis=2.68

Entry-level YOE bimodality (arshkon 2024-04): The arshkon entry-level YOE distribution was clearly bimodal, with peaks at YOE=2 and YOE=5, and skew=1.42. This bimodality represents two distinct subpopulations: - Genuinely junior roles (0-2 YOE): the expected entry tier - Mislabeled/inflated roles (3-8 YOE): roles labeled "entry-level" by the platform despite requiring substantial experience

In 2026-03, entry-level YOE is unimodal (peak at 2, skew=3.93 due to sparse outliers), with the 5+ YOE subpopulation almost entirely eliminated.

docs.title: Seniority Label Provenance
docs.text:
Period native_backfill title_keyword unknown other 2024-01 37.5% 62.5% 0.0% 0.0% 2024-04 39.7% 37.7% 19.0% 3.7% 2026-03 45.4% 45.2% 6.7% 2.6%

The scraped data has better seniority label coverage (93.3% resolved vs 81.0% for arshkon). The native_backfill and title_keyword provenances are nearly equal in scraped data.

docs.title: 3.1 The Paradox Stated
docs.text: Wave 1 found that entry-level median YOE decreased from 3.0 (arshkon 2024) to 2.0 (scraped 2026). This appears to contradict the "scope inflation" hypothesis -- if employers are asking more of junior roles, why would YOE requirements go DOWN?

docs.title: 3.2 Resolution: Slot Elimination + Genuine Entry Residual
docs.text:
Finding: The YOE decrease is real and reflects the selective elimination of high-YOE "entry-level" slots.

Evidence from five independent decompositions:

docs.title: A. Binned YOE distribution (slot elimination)
docs.text:
YOE bin 2024-04 share 2026-03 share Change Missing 42.7% 67.3% +24.6pp 1-2 17.7% 27.2% +9.5pp 3-4 16.9% 3.2% -13.7pp 5+ 22.8% 2.4% -20.4pp

The 5+ YOE category collapsed from 22.8% to 2.4% of entry-level postings. The 3-4 YOE category collapsed similarly. What remains is the 1-2 YOE tier, which actually grew in share.

Ghost job risk confirms this: In arshkon, 22.8% of entry-level posts were flagged as "high" ghost risk (entry label + 5+ YOE) vs only 2.4% in scraped. The pipeline's ghost detector essentially identifies the same phenomenon.

docs.title: B. Within-title analysis
docs.text:
Of 14 matched titles (>=3 observations in both periods), 11 show YOE decrease or stability, 2 are unchanged, and 1 shows increase:

Title Med YOE 2024 Med YOE 2026 Change full stack developer 6.0 2.0 -4.0 servicenow developer 6.0 2.0 -4.0 java developer 5.0 2.0 -3.0 quality assurance engineer 3.0 1.0 -2.0 software developer 3.0 2.0 -1.0 data engineer 3.0 2.0 -1.0 cloud engineer 3.0 2.0 -1.0 systems engineer 3.0 2.0 -1.0 software engineer 2.0 2.0 0.0 product engineer 2.0 2.0 0.0

The decrease is within-title, not compositional. Titles that formerly asked for 5-6 YOE at "entry-level" now ask for 2 YOE.

docs.title: C. Seniority provenance decomposition
docs.text:
Even restricting to the same provenance pathway:

Provenance Med YOE 2024 Med YOE 2026 Change native_backfill 3.0 2.0 -1.0 title_keyword 2.0 2.0 0.0

The native_backfill pathway (platform-assigned "entry" labels) shows the same decrease. Title_keyword (imputed from title text) was already at 2.0 in both periods.

docs.title: D. Aggregator decomposition
docs.text:
Segment Med YOE 2024 Med YOE 2026 Change Direct employers 3.0 2.0 -1.0 Aggregators 5.0 2.0 -3.0

Aggregators show the largest YOE drop, but direct employers show it too. This is not an aggregator-specific artifact.

docs.title: E. Company capping sensitivity
docs.text:
Company cap Med YOE 2024 Med YOE 2026 3 per company 3.0 2.0 5 per company 3.0 2.0 10 per company 3.0 2.0 50 per company 3.0 2.0 No cap 3.0 2.0

The YOE decrease is completely invariant to company capping.

docs.title: F. Top employers composition
docs.text:
Arshkon 2024-04 top entry-level employers were staffing agencies posting inflated requirements: - Tata Consultancy Services (n=30, med YOE=8.0) - Open Systems Technologies (n=26, med YOE=5.0) - TEKsystems (n=22, med YOE=5.0)

Scraped 2026-03 top entry-level employers are direct employers with genuine entry requirements: - Jobs via Dice (n=213, med YOE=2.0) - Epic (n=163, med YOE=NaN -- no YOE listed) - TikTok (n=93, med YOE=2.0) - Cisco (n=51, med YOE=1.0) - SpaceX (n=37, med YOE=2.0)

docs.title: 3.3 Statistical Test
docs.text: Mann-Whitney U test for entry-level YOE, 2024-04 vs 2026-03: - U = 399,360, p = 2.94e-81 - Cohen's d = 0.94 (large effect) - Mean: 4.04 -> 2.17 - Median: 3.0 -> 2.0

docs.title: 3.4 YOE Paradox: Interpretation
docs.text:
The YOE paradox is NOT a contradiction of scope inflation. It is evidence of a complementary phenomenon: slot purification. The story is:

  1. In 2024, "entry-level" was a broad label encompassing both genuinely junior roles (0-2 YOE) AND experienced roles mislabeled as entry (3-8 YOE). Many of the latter were from staffing agencies.
  2. By 2026, the high-YOE "entry-level" slots were eliminated -- either reclassified to appropriate seniority levels, or the roles themselves were cut.
  3. What remains in the entry tier are genuinely junior positions with YOE requirements matching their seniority label.

This is consistent with scope inflation if we distinguish between: - Vertical inflation (junior roles demanding more): not supported by YOE data - Horizontal elimination (junior slots disappearing entirely): supported by junior share decline (-5.9pp) - Label purification (entry tier becoming more accurately labeled): supported by YOE data

The 3+ YOE "entry-level" roles that disappeared were likely never genuinely junior. Their elimination from the entry tier reduces noise and makes the remaining junior-senior comparison cleaner.

docs.title: 3.5 YOE Coverage Caveat
docs.text: YOE extraction coverage is lower for entry-level in 2026 (32.7%) than 2024 (57.3%). This is partially because more 2026 entry posts omit explicit YOE requirements entirely (especially from companies like Epic where 163 entry posts have no extracted YOE). This is consistent with genuinely entry-level roles not bothering to state a YOE floor.

docs.title: 4.1 Description Length
docs.text:
Comparing asaniczka (Jan 2024) vs arshkon (Apr 2024) for mid-senior SWE establishes the instrument noise floor:

Metric Within-2024 Cohen's d 2024-2026 Cohen's d Ratio description_length -0.285 0.770 2.7x core_length 0.107 0.761 7.1x

The 2024-to-2026 description length effect is 2.7-7.1x the within-2024 noise, confirming genuine growth.

docs.title: 4.2 Technology Stack Calibration
docs.text:
Top technologies by |2024-2026 difference| with calibration ratio:

Technology Asaniczka Arshkon Scraped Within-2024 2024-2026 Ratio lang_python 34.8% 33.9% 49.6% -0.9pp +15.8pp 17.0x ai_llm 1.7% 1.8% 14.4% +0.2pp +12.5pp (noise~0) devops_cicd 18.0% 24.3% 36.4% +6.3pp +12.0pp 1.9x ai_agent_frameworks 0.03% 0.1% 10.8% +0.07pp +10.7pp (noise~0) ai_generative_ai 0.8% 1.6% 9.7% +0.8pp +8.1pp 10.6x ml_machine_learning 8.4% 7.4% 14.9% -1.0pp +7.5pp 7.9x

AI/ML technologies show signal-to-noise ratios of 7-17x. CI/CD shows a 1.9x ratio -- a real increase but with substantial instrument noise. Python's 17x ratio is remarkable, suggesting a genuine doubling of Python prevalence in SWE postings.

docs.title: 4.3 Technologies That Are Mostly Noise
docs.text: Technology Within-2024 diff 2024-2026 diff Ratio Interpretation be_dotnet +2.9pp -4.3pp 1.5x Instrument noise dominates fe_html_css +0.2pp -4.5pp Unclear Possible real decline, but noisy

docs.title: 5.1 Primary estimate (seniority_final, arshkon vs scraped)
docs.text: Measure 2024-04 2026-03 Change Entry share (all SWE) 16.5% 13.5% -3.0pp Entry share (known seniority) 20.4% 14.5% -5.9pp

docs.title: 5.2 Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
Sensitivity Entry 2024-04 Entry 2026-03 Change seniority_native only 22.3% 14.0% -8.3pp Aggregator exclusion 14.4% 11.4% -3.1pp regex-tier SWE only 20.0% 14.5% -5.5pp title_lookup_llm SWE only 25.1% 17.1% -8.0pp

All sensitivities show decline. Aggregator exclusion reduces the magnitude (from -5.9pp to -3.1pp) because aggregators disproportionately post entry-level. seniority_native shows the largest decline (-8.3pp).

docs.title: Top 10 by |Cohen's h| or |Cohen's d| (arshkon 2024 vs scraped 2026, mid-senior SWE):
docs.text:
Rank Variable Category Effect Size Direction 1 description_length structural d = 0.77 INCREASE 2 core_length structural d = 0.76 INCREASE 3 ai_agent_frameworks technology h = 0.61 INCREASE (0.1% to 10.8%) 4 ai_llm technology h = 0.51 INCREASE (1.8% to 14.4%) 5 tool_claude technology h = 0.41 NEW (0% to 4.1%) 6 ai_generative_ai technology h = 0.38 INCREASE (1.6% to 9.7%) 7 aggregator_share_mid structural h = 0.38 INCREASE (3.1% to 12.7%) 8 tool_copilot technology h = 0.34 INCREASE (0.2% to 4.4%) 9 ai_rag technology h = 0.33 INCREASE (0.5% to 5.3%) 10 lang_python technology h = 0.32 INCREASE (33.9% to 49.6%)

Description length is the single largest change. After that, the top changes are almost entirely AI/ML technology adoption. Python is the only general-purpose language in the top 10.

docs.title: Technologies showing DECLINE:
docs.text:
Technology Arshkon Scraped Cohen's h lang_c_cpp 1.2% 0.08% -0.17 practice_agile 33.0% 28.4% -0.10 fe_html_css 11.8% 7.3% -0.15 lang_sql 30.5% 25.4% -0.11

C/C++, Agile, and frontend HTML/CSS mentions declined, though these are smaller effects and partially within the noise floor.

docs.title: S1: The YOE paradox is a FEATURE, not a bug
docs.text: The most surprising finding is that the YOE decrease is not an artifact but a genuine market signal. The 2024 "entry-level" tier was contaminated with experienced-hire roles mislabeled as entry. By 2026, these have been purified out. This has a major methodological implication: the 2024 entry-level YOE distribution was bimodal (genuine juniors + mislabeled seniors), making any 2024 entry-level text analysis contaminated by senior-level content.

docs.title: S2: Aggregator share at mid-level quadrupled
docs.text: Mid-level aggregator share went from 3.1% to 12.7% (Cohen's h = 0.38). This is the 7th largest effect size. Staffing agencies appear to be shifting their SWE postings from entry-level to mid-level roles.

docs.title: S3: Python became a majority requirement
docs.text: Python prevalence in mid-senior SWE postings went from 33.9% to 49.6%. It is now mentioned in essentially half of all senior SWE job postings, up from one-third just two years ago. This is the largest single general-purpose technology shift.

docs.title: S4: The new AI technology stack appeared from near-zero
docs.text: Agent frameworks (0.1% to 10.8%), RAG (0.5% to 5.3%), LangChain (0.2% to 3.4%), and prompt engineering (0.3% to 3.7%) all emerged from essentially zero prevalence. These represent genuinely new requirements that did not exist in the 2024 labor market.

docs.title: S5: Agile practice mentions declined
docs.text: Agile/Scrum mentions went from 33.0% to 28.4%. This is unexpected -- agile methodologies are if anything more prevalent in industry. This may reflect a shift in what employers emphasize in job postings rather than a decline in practice.

docs.title: S6: C/C++ nearly disappeared from mid-senior SWE
docs.text: C/C++ went from 1.2% to 0.08%, a near-complete elimination. This is surprising even if it was already rare -- the remaining roles presumably represent embedded/systems work that shifted to Rust (which grew from 1.2% to 4.2%).

docs.title: S7: YOE extraction coverage is an informative signal
docs.text: The lower YOE extraction rate for entry-level in 2026 (32.7% vs 57.3%) is not missing data -- it likely reflects that genuinely entry-level roles do not specify YOE requirements, while the inflated "entry" roles of 2024 did specify (5+ YOE). The missingness itself is evidence of slot purification.

docs.title: 8. Methodological Implications for Downstream Tasks
docs.text:
1. Length normalization is mandatory. The 55-77% increase in description length (d=0.77) means all text-derived metrics (keyword counts, topic prevalence, etc.) must be normalized per 1K characters at minimum.

  2. The 2024 entry tier is contaminated. Approximately 23% of arshkon entry-level postings had 5+ YOE requirements. Any analysis of "entry-level requirements" in 2024 should either (a) filter to YOE <= 2 or (b) treat the bimodal distribution as a known confounder.

  3. Aggregator sensitivity is critical for entry-level. The entry-level junior share decline shrinks from -5.9pp to -3.1pp when aggregators are excluded. Report both.

  4. Within-2024 calibration identifies genuine signals. Technologies with ratio > 3 (python, AI/ML, agent frameworks, terraform, kubernetes) are genuine changes. Technologies with ratio < 2 (dotnet, CICD) are within the noise floor.

  5. YOE paradox is consistent with, not contradictory to, RQ1. The correct framing: employers reduced the number of junior slots (share decline) AND the remaining junior slots have more accurate labels (YOE decrease). This is structural reorganization of the entry tier, not scope inflation within it.

docs.text:
• fig1_description_length_by_period.png — Description and core length distributions by period
  • fig2_yoe_by_period_seniority.png — YOE distributions by period and seniority tier
  • fig3_yoe_paradox.png — YOE paradox: entry-level YOE decrease decomposed (histogram, bin shares, native-backfill only)

docs.text: Sensitivity Applied to Effect on key finding (a) Aggregator exclusion Junior share, YOE Junior decline shrinks -5.9pp to -3.1pp; YOE decrease survives (b) Company capping YOE paradox No change at any cap level (3, 5, 10, 50) (c) Seniority operationalization Junior share -5.9pp (seniority_final) to -8.3pp (seniority_native). Robust. (e) Source restriction Junior share Excludes asaniczka (no entry labels). Primary comparison is arshkon vs scraped. (f) Within-2024 calibration Tech, desc length AI tech changes 5-17x noise. Desc length 2.7-7.1x noise. Both genuine. (g) SWE tier Junior share Decline holds in regex-only (-5.5pp) and title_lookup_llm-only (-8.0pp)

docs.title: T09: Posting Archetype Discovery -- Methods Laboratory
docs.text: Generated: 2026-04-05

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
Unsupervised clustering of 8,002 SWE LinkedIn job postings reveals that the market's dominant structure is organized by technical specialization, not by seniority level. BERTopic identified 92 fine-grained topics (reduced to 14 interpretable archetypes), while NMF produced consistent macro-level groupings. Key findings:

  1. Technology domain drives posting structure. Clusters align most strongly with tech domain (NMI=0.175), followed by period (NMI=0.057), while seniority shows the weakest alignment (NMI=0.018). A senior data engineer posting is more similar to a junior data engineer posting than to a senior frontend posting. Seniority operates as a within-domain modifier, not a cross-cutting structural dimension.

  2. Four macro-archetypes dominate the market. Frontend/Web (28%), Embedded/Systems (21%), Data/Analytics (19%), and ML/AI Engineering (13%) account for 81% of non-noise postings. The remaining 10 micro-clusters represent company-specific, aggregator, or niche patterns.

  3. ML/AI Engineering underwent massive growth. It rose from 4% of SWE postings in 2024 to 27% in 2026 (+22pp). Frontend/Web shrank from 41% to 24% (-17pp). This is the largest temporal shift in the data.

  4. Both methods agree on core structure. 7 of 14 BERTopic archetypes have clear NMF counterparts (Jaccard > 0.1). Clustering is highly stable (ARI = 1.00 for HDBSCAN, 0.996 for NMF).

docs.title: 1. Sample Composition
docs.text:
Population: 52,327 SWE LinkedIn postings (English, date_flag=ok, is_swe=true) Sample: 8,002 postings, stratified by period x seniority_3level Sampling: Proportional with floor of 100 per stratum to preserve small groups

Stratum Sampled Population Rate 2024-01_junior 100 130 76.9% 2024-01_mid 217 1450 15.0% 2024-01_senior 3248 21633 15.0% 2024-04_junior 123 830 14.8% 2024-04_mid 100 228 43.9% 2024-04_senior 450 3008 15.0% 2024-04_unknown 142 953 14.9% 2026-03_junior 488 3255 15.0% 2026-03_mid 100 622 16.1% 2026-03_senior 2791 18592 15.0% 2026-03_unknown 243 1626 14.9%

Text source distribution: LLM-cleaned: 3,718 (46.5%), Rule-based: 4,284 (53.5%)

docs.title: 2. Method A: BERTopic (Primary)
docs.text: Pipeline: Pre-computed sentence-transformer embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim) -> UMAP (15 neighbors, 5 components, cosine metric) -> HDBSCAN clustering -> c-TF-IDF topic representations.

docs.title: Configuration comparison
docs.text:
min_topic_size Topics Noise % Notes 20 ~146 ~12% Very granular 30 92 11.9% Primary 50 ~51 ~14% Coarser

Topic reduction: The 92 fine-grained topics were merged to 14 interpretable archetypes using BERTopic's built-in hierarchical topic reduction (nr_topics=15).

docs.title: Archetype characterization (14 reduced clusters)
docs.text:
# Archetype N % Top 5 Terms Senior% Junior% 2024% 2026% Avg YOE Tech Ct 0 Frontend/Web Development 2227 27.8 development, software, experience, design, applications 81 8 65 35 5.3 7.7 1 Embedded/Systems 1707 21.3 test, systems, software, engineering, requirements 78 10 64 36 5.5 3.6 2 Data/Analytics 1522 19.0 data, cloud, aws, azure, experience 84 8 55 45 5.3 10.0 3 ML/AI Engineering 1036 12.9 ai, build, learning, building, work 82 10 17 83 5.0 8.4 4 Mobile Development 159 2.0 android, ios, mobile, kotlin, app 82 8 57 43 5.0 5.4 5 Benefits-Heavy Postings 78 1.0 geographic market, salary, california 100 0 54 46 5.7 8.2 6 Aggregator/AI Roles 64 0.8 jobs, site helps, behalf direct, job boardemployer 56 42 30 70 3.9 7.8 7 PwC/Consulting 54 0.7 pwc, data analytics, ai 72 6 35 65 6.2 8.0 8 Defense/Anduril 44 0.5 anduril, ubuntu, military 82 14 18 82 5.5 7.0 9 Server Infrastructure 38 0.5 server infrastructure, infrastructure engineer 100 0 84 16 4.3 3.7 10 Streaming/Distributed 37 0.5 streaming data, cassandra, kafka, zookeeper 97 3 89 11 8.8 14.7 11 Compensation-Focused 36 0.4 buy, spending, roles, pay 97 3 0 100 4.6 5.5 12 Chatbot/Web Dev 35 0.4 chatbots, javascript html, python javascript 34 9 69 31 4.5 7.6 13 Certified/Tax Tech 31 0.4 certified safe, tax, graphql 68 3 29 71 4.6 11.1

Notable patterns in the archetypes:

  • The four major archetypes (Frontend/Web, Embedded/Systems, Data/Analytics, ML/AI) each have very similar seniority distributions (78-84% senior, 8-10% junior). Seniority does not differentiate archetypes -- tech domain does.
  • ML/AI Engineering is overwhelmingly 2026 data (83%), confirming its recent emergence.
  • Embedded/Systems has lower tech count (3.6) than other archetypes, reflecting its hardware-adjacent nature with fewer software-specific keyword matches.
  • Several small clusters are company-specific (PwC, Anduril) or format-specific (aggregator boilerplate, compensation sections), reflecting posting style rather than role content.

docs.title: 3. Method B: NMF (Comparison)
docs.text:
NMF on TF-IDF matrix (5,000 features, 1-2grams, sublinear TF).

k Reconstruction Error 5 83.70 8 82.98 12 82.14 15 81.62

docs.title: NMF k=15 components (for alignment with BERTopic)
docs.text:
• NMF-0: benefits, pay, range, employees, salary, 000, compensation, job, company, people
  • NMF-1: open source, years experience, degree years, tech trends, technology communities
  • NMF-2: project, technical, requirements, support, ability, management, customer, analysis
  • NMF-3: web, developer, javascript, net, applications, java, angular, react, code
  • NMF-4: infrastructure, cloud, devops, ci, cd, automation, terraform, aws, kubernetes
  • NMF-5: ai, ml, machine learning, models, deep learning, model, research
  • NMF-6: billions users, scale, information retrieval, fresh ideas, generation technologies
  • NMF-7: clearance, systems, security clearance, embedded, secret, hardware, test
  • NMF-8: data, data engineer, analytics, etl, data engineering, data pipelines, snowflake, spark
  • NMF-9: server infrastructure, server, policies procedures, vmware, escalating
  • NMF-10: streaming data, cassandra, large scale, kafka, real time, distributed
  • NMF-11: dice, destination tech, stage careers, leading career, tech experts (aggregator boilerplate)
  • NMF-12: product, building, build, backend, platform, features, ownership, systems
  • NMF-13: qualifications capabilities, chase, banking, financial (JP Morgan template)
  • NMF-14: wisconsin, university, madison, academic, talent (university template)

docs.title: NMF k=8 components (most interpretable)
docs.text:
• NMF-0: benefits, range, pay, company, employees, salary, compensation (compensation boilerplate)
  • NMF-1: open source, years experience, tech trends (generic qualifications boilerplate)
  • NMF-2: systems, requirements, test, clearance, hardware (embedded/defense)
  • NMF-3: web, applications, code, javascript, developer, java, react, net (web/app development)
  • NMF-4: infrastructure, cloud, devops, ci/cd, terraform, aws, automation (cloud/DevOps)
  • NMF-5: data, ai, ml, machine learning, models, pipelines, building (data + AI/ML)
  • NMF-6: scale, large scale, billions users, information retrieval (big tech scale problems)
  • NMF-7: dice, stage careers, tech experts (aggregator boilerplate)

The NMF k=8 solution cleanly separates web development, embedded/defense, cloud/devops, data+AI, and three boilerplate/format-driven components. This broadly confirms the BERTopic structure.

docs.title: Stability
docs.text:
Method Mean ARI (3 seeds) BERTopic (HDBSCAN on UMAP) 1.0000 NMF k=15 0.9961

Both methods are highly stable. BERTopic achieves perfect reproducibility because HDBSCAN is deterministic given fixed UMAP embeddings (which use a fixed seed).

docs.title: Topic alignment (BERTopic reduced vs NMF k=15)
docs.text:
Method-robust topics (Jaccard > 0.1 on top-20 terms): 7 out of 14

  • BT "Frontend/Web Development" <-> NMF-3 (web, developer, javascript) -- Jaccard=0.111
  • BT "Embedded/Systems" <-> NMF-7 (clearance, systems, embedded) -- Jaccard=0.154
  • BT "Data/Analytics" <-> NMF-4 (infrastructure, cloud, devops) -- Jaccard=0.154
  • BT "ML/AI Engineering" <-> NMF-12 (product, building, backend) -- Jaccard=0.200
  • BT "PwC/Consulting" <-> NMF-5 (ai, ml, machine learning) -- Jaccard=0.111
  • BT "Server Infrastructure" <-> NMF-9 (server infrastructure) -- Jaccard=0.429
  • BT "Streaming/Distributed" <-> NMF-10 (streaming data, cassandra) -- Jaccard=0.154

The Jaccard scores are moderate (0.1-0.4), which is expected given that BERTopic uses semantic embeddings while NMF uses bag-of-words. The methods agree on the existence of distinct data, embedded, web, and infrastructure clusters, but describe them with partially different vocabularies.

docs.title: Archetype proportion shifts (non-noise postings only)
docs.text: Archetype 2024 avg % 2026 % Change (pp) ML/AI Engineering 4.2 26.5 +22.4 Aggregator/AI Roles 0.3 1.4 +1.1 Compensation-Focused 0.0 1.1 +1.1 Defense/Anduril 0.1 1.1 +1.0 PwC/Consulting 0.4 1.1 +0.7 Certified/Tax Tech 0.2 0.7 +0.5 Benefits-Heavy Postings 0.8 1.1 +0.3 Server Infrastructure 0.5 0.2 -0.3 Mobile Development 2.5 2.1 -0.4 Streaming/Distributed 0.7 0.1 -0.6 Data/Analytics 21.9 21.2 -0.6 Chatbot/Web Dev 1.5 0.3 -1.2 Embedded/Systems 26.1 19.1 -7.0 Frontend/Web Development 40.7 24.0 -16.8

docs.title: Key temporal patterns
docs.text:
The most dramatic shift: ML/AI Engineering grew from a 4% niche to a 27% major archetype. This single shift represents the largest structural change in the SWE posting landscape.

Declining archetypes: Frontend/Web Development (-17pp) and Embedded/Systems (-7pp) experienced significant relative decline. Note these are relative shares of the SWE posting market; absolute counts depend on total posting volume changes.

Stable archetype: Data/Analytics held steady at ~21%, suggesting its prominence is durable across the 2024-2026 period.

Caveat: Some temporal variation may reflect text quality differences between Kaggle data (LLM-cleaned where available, ~45%) and scraped data (rule-based text only). The text source sensitivity check (cosine similarity 0.88 between LLM and rule-based cluster distributions) suggests moderate robustness, but cannot fully rule out this confound.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
Aggregator-heavy archetypes (>20% aggregator content): - Data/Analytics: 33% aggregator - Mobile Development: 25% aggregator - Benefits-Heavy Postings: 27% aggregator - Aggregator/AI Roles: 100% aggregator - Streaming/Distributed: 73% aggregator - Chatbot/Web Dev: 69% aggregator - Certified/Tax Tech: 55% aggregator

Topic distribution cosine similarity (non-aggregator vs aggregator): 0.84

Several small clusters (6, 10, 12, 13) are aggregator-dominated and should be interpreted as data artifacts rather than true market segments. The four major archetypes (Frontend/Web, Embedded/Systems, Data/Analytics, ML/AI) have aggregator rates of 0-33%, which is manageable but suggests Data/Analytics results should be interpreted with caution.

docs.title: (d) Text source sensitivity
docs.text:
• LLM-cleaned text: 3,718 rows (46.5%)
  • Rule-based text: 4,284 rows (53.5%)
  • Cluster distribution cosine similarity (LLM vs Rule text): 0.88

The moderate cosine similarity (0.88) indicates that text cleaning method affects cluster assignments but does not fundamentally alter the structure. The four major archetypes are robust across text sources.

docs.title: (g) SWE classification tier
docs.text:
SWE tier distribution in sample: regex: 7281, title_lookup_llm: 589, embedding_high: 132

The sample is dominated by regex-tier classifications (91%), which are the highest-confidence SWE identifications. The 7% from title_lookup_llm and 2% from embedding_high contribute marginal uncertainty.

docs.title: 7. Key Discovery: What is the Market's Dominant Structure?
Cluster alignment with external dimensions
docs.text: Rank Dimension NMI V-measure 1 tech_domain 0.1752 0.1752 2 period 0.0570 0.0570 3 is_aggregator 0.0475 0.0475 4 swe_tier 0.0315 0.0315 5 seniority_3level 0.0180 0.0180

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
The 14-archetype clustering reveals a clear hierarchy of what structures the SWE labor market:

  1. Technology domain is the strongest signal (NMI=0.175). Even using a crude 5-category tech domain classification (AI, cloud, data, mobile, security, general), tech domain explains the clusters far better than any other dimension. The BERTopic archetypes themselves are tech-domain clusters: data engineering, embedded/systems, frontend/web, ML/AI, mobile. This is the central structural finding.

  2. Period ranks second (NMI=0.057). Posting language changed substantially between 2024 and 2026. The ML/AI Engineering archetype grew from 4% to 27%, while Frontend/Web shrank from 41% to 24%. Some of this reflects genuine market shifts; some may reflect source-specific text patterns.

  3. Aggregator status ranks third (NMI=0.047). Several small archetypes are aggregator-dominated (Aggregator/AI: 100%, Streaming/Distributed: 73%). This is mostly a data quality signal -- aggregator boilerplate creates distinctive text patterns that form their own clusters.

  4. Seniority is the weakest signal (NMI=0.018). This is the most important negative finding: job postings do NOT cluster by seniority level. Every major archetype spans all seniority levels with roughly similar proportions (78-84% senior, 8-10% junior), reflecting the population seniority distribution rather than content differentiation. Seniority operates as a within-domain modifier, not a cross-cutting structural dimension.

This pattern is consistent across granularity levels: - Fine-grained (92 topics): tech_domain NMI=0.152, period=0.073, seniority=0.029 - Reduced (14 archetypes): tech_domain NMI=0.175, period=0.057, seniority=0.018

docs.title: Implications for Research Questions
docs.text:
• RQ1 (employer-side restructuring): Seniority effects should be analyzed within technical domain archetypes. A pooled junior-vs-senior comparison masks domain-specific patterns. Junior data engineering roles may be affected very differently from junior frontend roles. The massive growth of ML/AI Engineering archetype (+22pp) is itself a restructuring signal.

  • RQ2 (task migration): When studying which requirements migrate between seniority levels, the analysis should be stratified by archetype. A requirement that moves from senior to junior in one domain may not move at all in another.

  • Temporal dynamics are substantial. ML/AI Engineering went from 4% to 27% of SWE postings. Frontend/Web shrank from 41% to 24%. Embedded/Systems shrank from 26% to 19%. These are not incremental shifts -- they represent a large-scale recomposition of what "SWE" means in the labor market between 2024 and 2026.

docs.text:
• figures/T09/umap_3panel.png -- UMAP colored by archetype, period, seniority
  • figures/T09/pca_comparison.png -- PCA colored by archetype and period
  • figures/T09/temporal_change.png -- Archetype proportion changes 2024 to 2026
  • figures/T09/dimension_alignment.png -- NMI/V-measure by external dimension

docs.text:

  • tables/T09/cluster_characterization_reduced.csv -- Full characterization of 14 archetypes
  • tables/T09/cluster_characterization.csv -- Full characterization of 92 fine-grained topics
  • tables/T09/methods_comparison.csv -- BERTopic vs NMF methods comparison
  • tables/T09/topic_alignment_reduced.csv -- BERTopic-NMF alignment (reduced topics)
  • tables/T09/topic_alignment.csv -- BERTopic-NMF alignment (fine-grained)
  • tables/T09/temporal_dynamics_reduced.csv -- Temporal proportion changes
  • tables/T09/cluster_dimension_alignment.csv -- NMI scores for all dimensions
  • tables/T09/nmf_k{5,8,12,15}_terms.csv -- NMF component terms
