docs.title: T16: Company Hiring Strategy Typology
docs.text: Status: Done Agent: J (Wave 3) Date: 2026-04-05

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Among 451 companies with 3+ SWE postings in both 2024 and 2026 (the "overlap panel"), we cluster hiring-strategy trajectories and decompose aggregate market changes into within-company vs compositional components. The central finding: compositional effects (new companies entering, others exiting) account for 53-59% of aggregate changes, while within-company shifts account for 41-47%. All four identified company strategy clusters show AI adoption increases -- the variation is in magnitude and what accompanies it. At the company level, AI adoption and entry-share change are uncorrelated (r = -0.070, p = 0.138), refuting a simple "AI replaces juniors" causal story at the firm level.

docs.title: 1. Overlap Panel Construction
docs.text:
• SWE base: 52,327 postings (LinkedIn, English, date_flag=ok, is_swe=true)
  • Era mapping: 2024-01 + 2024-04 pooled as "2024" (28,232 postings); 2026-03 as "2026" (24,095 postings)
  • Overlap companies: 451 with >= 3 SWE in both eras (22,929 postings, 43.8% of all SWE)
  • Company universe: 6,503 unique companies in 2024; 5,100 in 2026; 1,539 in both; 4,964 exit-only; 3,561 enter-only

The overlap panel is the most methodologically rigorous subset for detecting true hiring strategy changes, as it controls for company composition. However, it represents only 44% of postings, so compositional effects from entry/exit matter significantly.

docs.title: 2. Company Strategy Clusters (k=4)
docs.text: Silhouette analysis favored k=2 (silhouette=0.215), but k=4 (silhouette=0.203) provides more interpretable typology. The k=2 solution simply separates high-change from low-change companies. We report k=4 for interpretation and include k=2 as a robustness note.

docs.title: Cluster profiles (change from 2024 to 2026)
docs.text:
Cluster n Name Entry share AI prev Desc length Tech count Org scope Volume C0 108 Scope inflators +1.6pp +12.8pp +2,759 chars +3.1 +3.4 terms ~flat C1 49 Entry expanders +53.9pp +8.0pp +259 chars +0.7 -0.4 terms ~flat C2 98 AI-forward +4.2pp +44.3pp +659 chars +4.6 +0.4 terms ~flat C3 196 Traditional hold +0.6pp +4.8pp +579 chars +0.8 +0.2 terms slight growth

Cluster interpretation:

  • C0 "Scope inflators" (24%): Moderate AI adoption but massive description growth (+2,759 chars) and scope inflation (+3.4 org-scope terms). These companies are dramatically expanding what they write in postings. Includes AMD, Adobe, Accenture.

  • C1 "Entry expanders" (11%): Dramatic entry-share increase (+54pp) with minimal AI adoption or scope change. These are companies that shifted from posting almost no entry-level roles to posting many. Includes Abbott, ASRC Federal, Aditi Consulting. Descriptions actually slightly shorter. Many are staffing/consulting firms.

  • C2 "AI-forward" (22%): Highest AI adoption (+44pp) with significant tech-count growth (+4.6 techs). These companies aggressively incorporated AI requirements. Includes AT&T, AbbVie, Akamai Technologies.

  • C3 "Traditional hold" (43%): Minimal change across all metrics. The modal company strategy is effectively standing pat. Entry share barely moves, AI adoption is modest. The largest cluster by far.

docs.title: Key insight: AI adoption and entry decline are uncorrelated at company level
docs.text: At the company level, the correlation between AI prevalence change and entry-share change is r = -0.070 (p = 0.138). This is a null result -- companies that adopted more AI did not systematically reduce entry-level hiring. This challenges any simple "AI replaces juniors" narrative and suggests the aggregate correlation (both AI and entry share rising) operates through industry-level or market-level mechanisms rather than within-firm substitution.

docs.title: 3. Within-Company vs Compositional Decomposition
docs.text:
This is the paper's strongest analytical design for distinguishing genuine strategic shifts from compositional artifacts. We decompose aggregate metric changes into:

  • Within-overlap: Changes among the 451 companies present in both periods (weighted by their share of total postings)
  • Composition: The residual, driven by new entrants, exiting companies, and share reallocation
Metric Total change Within-overlap Within % Composition Composition % Entry share +10.1pp +4.4pp 43.3% +5.7pp 56.7% AI prevalence +21.0pp +8.7pp 41.4% +12.3pp 58.6% Description length +1,610 chars +763 chars 47.4% +847 chars 52.6%

Critical findings:

  1. Composition dominates entry share change (57%). The entry-share rise is majority-compositional: new 2026 entrants post at 13.4% entry vs. exiting 2024 companies that posted at 3.3%. This means the aggregate entry-share increase is driven more by which companies are hiring changing than by individual companies changing strategy.

  2. Composition dominates AI adoption (59%). New 2026 entrants arrive with 24.3% AI prevalence vs 2.5% among 2024 exits. The market is being repopulated with AI-native employers.

  3. Description length is more balanced (47% within). Within-company content growth is substantial, suggesting genuine posting strategy changes, not just new companies writing longer postings.

  4. Within-company changes are still real and large. Overlap companies increased entry share from 3.6% to 13.6% (+10.0pp within the panel), AI prevalence from 3.4% to 23.3% (+19.9pp), and description length from 2,758 to 4,503 (+1,745 chars). These are genuine strategy shifts, but they are amplified by compositional turnover.

docs.title: Implication for paper narrative
docs.text: The aggregate trends reported in Waves 1-2 are a mixture of within-company behavioral change (~42%) and market recomposition (~58%). This does not invalidate the aggregate trends -- compositional shifts are themselves a real market-level phenomenon (the type of company posting SWE jobs is changing). But it means the interpretation should be "the SWE labor market is being restructured" rather than "individual employers are restructuring their SWE hiring."

docs.title: 4. New Market Entrants Profile
docs.text:
Metric New entrants (2026) Continuing companies (2026) Difference Entry share 14.9% 12.5% +2.4pp AI prevalence 25.2% 22.9% +2.3pp Description length 4,134 4,449 -315 Tech count 8.2 8.5 -0.3 Org scope 3.4 4.0 -0.6

New 2026 entrants are slightly more AI-oriented and entry-heavy than continuing companies, but write slightly shorter, less scope-inflated postings. They are not dramatically different -- the compositional effect comes more from the exit of low-AI, low-entry 2024 companies than from distinctive new entrants.

Industry of new entrants: Software Development (35%), IT Services (8%), Technology/Internet (7%), Financial Services (6%), Staffing (2%). This matches the general SWE employer distribution.

docs.title: 5. Aggregator vs Direct Employer Patterns
docs.text:
Group 2024 Entry 2026 Entry 2024 AI 2026 AI 2024 Desc len 2026 Desc len Aggregator 2.9% 26.1% 3.9% 20.2% 2,236 3,415 Direct 3.6% 11.4% 2.6% 24.5% 2,856 4,468
  • Aggregators show 9x entry surge (2.9% to 26.1%) vs 3x for direct employers (3.6% to 11.4%). This is consistent with staffing firms filling the entry-level pipeline.
  • Direct employers show higher AI adoption (24.5% vs 20.2% in 2026), suggesting AI requirements originate with the actual employers.
  • Direct employers write longer postings in both periods, with larger absolute growth.

Among overlap companies, the 54 aggregator companies show larger entry-share increases (+10.7pp) than the 397 direct employers (+6.2pp), but similar AI prevalence increases.

docs.title: 6. Sensitivity: Aggregator Exclusion
docs.text:
Metric Total (baseline) Within % (baseline) Total (no agg) Within % (no agg) Direction change? Entry share +10.1pp 43.3% +7.8pp 37.9% No AI prevalence +21.0pp 41.4% +21.9pp 35.3% No Description length +1,610 47.4% +1,612 35.3% No

Excluding aggregators: - Reduces entry share change by 23% (from +10.1pp to +7.8pp) -- aggregators amplify the entry surge - AI prevalence is stable (essentially unchanged) - Description length is unchanged - Within-company share drops to 35-38%, meaning composition becomes even more dominant without aggregators - No direction changes; no materiality threshold breached (30%) - Overlap panel shrinks from 451 to 397 companies

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Entry share INCREASED, not decreased, in the overlap panel. The T03/T06 finding of aggregate junior share decline using seniority_native (arshkon-only entry labels) is not replicated using seniority_3level across the full SWE panel. Using seniority_3level, entry share went from 3.4% to 13.5% (+10.1pp). This apparent contradiction arises because seniority_3level captures more entry labels (especially in scraped data with 87.8% non-unknown) than the narrow arshkon-native baseline. The prior finding of decline depends on the specific operationalization and the thin arshkon-only historical baseline (89 entry SWE postings).

  2. The "traditional hold" cluster is the largest (43%). Despite dramatic aggregate changes, the most common company strategy is doing essentially nothing different. The aggregate shift is driven by a minority of active-strategy companies plus composition.

  3. AI adoption and entry expansion are orthogonal at firm level (r = -0.070). Companies that went "AI-forward" did not systematically cut junior roles. The few that massively expanded entry hiring (C1) are not the AI adopters. This suggests separate mechanisms.

  4. New entrants are not distinctive. The compositional effect comes from the removal of 2024 companies more than the addition of 2026 companies. Exit companies had 2.5% AI prevalence and 3.3% entry share; new entrants have 25.2% AI and 14.9% entry -- both close to the 2026 market average.

docs.title: Methodology Notes
docs.text:
• Seniority operationalization: Uses seniority_3level (junior/mid/senior/unknown) for entry share. This includes seniority_imputed plus seniority_native backfill. Results differ from T03/T06 which used seniority_native on arshkon-only.
  • AI prevalence: Binary indicator for any AI technology keyword (17 AI-family columns from shared tech matrix).
  • Org scope: Regex count of management/leadership/scope terms in description_core.
  • Clustering: k-means on standardized change vectors. k=4 chosen for interpretability (k=2 wins silhouette but provides less insight).
  • Decomposition: Posting-weighted shift-share. Overlap companies' share: ~45% in 2024, ~43% in 2026.

docs.title: Output Files
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T16/company_clusters.csv -- Company-level cluster assignments and change metrics (451 rows)
  • exploration/tables/T16/decomposition_formal.csv -- Formal shift-share decomposition results
  • exploration/tables/T16/decomposition_basic.csv -- Raw decomposition components
  • exploration/tables/T16/new_entrant_comparison.csv -- New entrant vs continuer comparison
  • exploration/tables/T16/sensitivity_aggregator_decomposition.csv -- Aggregator exclusion sensitivity
  • exploration/figures/T16/cluster_heatmap.png -- Standardized cluster profiles
  • exploration/figures/T16/decomposition_bars.png -- Within vs compositional decomposition
  • exploration/figures/T16/ai_vs_entry_scatter.png -- Company-level AI vs entry scatter
  • exploration/figures/T16/volume_change_by_cluster.png -- Volume change by cluster
  • exploration/scripts/T16_company_strategies.py -- Analysis script

docs.title: T17: Geographic Market Structure
docs.text: Status: Done Agent: J (Wave 3) Date: 2026-04-05

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Across 26 metros with 50+ SWE postings in both periods, every single metro shows increases in entry share, AI prevalence, org scope, description length, and tech diversity. The changes are geographically uniform -- tech hubs and non-hubs show statistically indistinguishable changes. Metro-level AI adoption is completely uncorrelated with entry share change (r = -0.039, p = 0.850). This is a national, industry-wide shift, not a tech-hub phenomenon. Remote work data (via is_remote) shows 22.6% of 2026 scraped SWE postings are remote, but these lack metro assignments and cannot be compared cross-period (2024 = 0% by construction).

docs.title: 1. Metro-Level Metrics
docs.text: Qualifying metros: 26 with >= 50 SWE per era (35,400 total postings, 67.7% of all SWE)

docs.title: Aggregate change across all qualifying metros
docs.text:
Metric Mean 2024 Mean 2026 Mean change Std of change CV Entry share 3.4% 13.7% +10.3pp 4.2pp 0.40 AI prevalence 2.9% 22.0% +20.6pp 6.4pp 0.31 Org scope 2.7 terms 4.1 terms +1.4 terms 0.5 0.37 Median desc length 2,459 chars 4,068 chars +1,609 chars 390 0.24 Tech diversity 6.1 techs 8.6 techs +2.5 techs 0.9 0.36

All 26 metros show positive changes across all five metrics. There are no metros bucking the trend.

docs.title: Entry share change (top 5 / bottom 5)
docs.text:
Rank Metro 2024 2026 Change 1 Minneapolis-Saint Paul 3.0% 22.7% +19.7pp 2 Los Angeles Metro 2.0% 18.3% +16.3pp 3 Raleigh-Durham-Chapel Hill 2.9% 19.2% +16.3pp 4 Portland Metro 0.6% 15.4% +14.8pp 5 Boston Metro 1.8% 16.3% +14.5pp 22 Dallas-Fort Worth 4.7% 11.9% +7.3pp 23 Columbus 5.3% 11.8% +6.5pp 24 San Diego 5.3% 10.1% +4.7pp 25 Charlotte 5.5% 9.0% +3.5pp 26 New York City 6.9% 9.6% +2.8pp

The range is 2.8pp to 19.7pp -- a 7x spread. NYC has the smallest entry-share increase, potentially because it already had the highest 2024 entry share (6.9%).

docs.title: AI prevalence change (top 5 / bottom 5)
docs.text:
Rank Metro 2024 2026 Change 1 Miami 2.2% 36.3% +34.1pp 2 Tampa Bay 2.3% 34.6% +32.3pp 3 Nashville 1.4% 33.2% +31.8pp 4 New York City 1.0% 29.6% +28.6pp 5 Seattle 3.9% 30.3% +26.4pp 22 Denver 1.6% 15.8% +14.2pp 23 Columbus 8.5% 22.2% +13.8pp 24 Washington DC 1.5% 14.8% +13.3pp 25 Los Angeles 3.0% 14.8% +11.8pp 26 Detroit 3.3% 14.8% +11.6pp

Florida metros (Miami, Tampa Bay) and Nashville lead AI adoption. Los Angeles and Detroit trail. The range is 11.6pp to 34.1pp -- a 3x spread but all strongly positive.

docs.title: Org scope change (top 5)
docs.text: Rank Metro Change 1 Pittsburgh +2.2 terms 2 Columbus +2.1 3 Nashville +2.1 4 Houston +2.1 5 Philadelphia +2.0

docs.title: Tech diversity change (top 5)
docs.text: Rank Metro Change 1 Washington DC +4.1 techs 2 Houston +4.1 3 Charlotte +3.8 4 Phoenix +3.5 5 Tampa Bay +3.4

docs.title: 3. Tech Hub vs Non-Hub Patterns
docs.text:
Classified as tech hubs: San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle, Austin, Boston, New York City (5 of 26 metros).

Metric Hub change Non-hub change Difference t-stat p-value Entry share +8.8pp +10.7pp -1.8pp -0.88 0.386 AI prevalence +22.2pp +20.3pp +1.9pp 0.59 0.558 Org scope +1.1 terms +1.4 terms -0.4 -1.52 0.142 Median desc length +1,573 +1,617 -44 -0.22 0.825 Tech diversity +1.9 techs +2.7 techs -0.8 -1.78 0.089

No significant hub/non-hub differences on any metric (all p > 0.08). The SWE market transformation is not concentrated in tech hubs. If anything, non-hubs show slightly larger changes in entry share, org scope, and tech diversity (though none are significant).

Tech diversity change approaches significance (p = 0.089) with non-hubs gaining more -- possibly because they started from a lower base and are "catching up" in stack breadth.

docs.title: Concentration analysis (coefficient of variation)
docs.text:
Metric CV Description length 0.24 (most uniform) AI prevalence 0.31 Tech diversity 0.36 Org scope 0.37 Entry share 0.40 (most variable)

Description length growth is the most uniform change (CV=0.24). Entry share growth is the most variable (CV=0.40) but still positive everywhere.

docs.title: 4. Metro-Level Correlations
docs.text:
Pair Pearson r p Spearman rho p AI surge vs entry change -0.039 0.850 -0.033 0.872 AI surge vs scope inflation 0.027 0.894 0.009 0.967 AI surge vs tech diversity 0.285 0.158 0.307 0.128 Entry change vs scope inflation -0.100 0.628 -0.128 0.533 AI surge vs desc length -0.028 0.893 -0.064 0.756

All cross-metric correlations are null at the metro level. Metros with larger AI surges do NOT show larger entry declines (r = -0.039, p = 0.850). The five metrics are statistically independent across geographies -- each metro follows its own pattern.

This reinforces the T16 finding: AI adoption and entry-level change are orthogonal. The correlation is null at the company level (T16: r = -0.070) AND at the metro level (T17: r = -0.039).

docs.title: 5. Remote Work Dimension
docs.text:
Available data: is_remote from scraped data = 22.6% of 2026 SWE postings (5,438 / 24,095). These remote postings have no metro area assigned (all metro = NULL) because they lack specific location information.

Cross-period comparison is impossible: 2024 sources (arshkon, asaniczka) show 0.0% remote rate -- this is a data collection artifact, not a real finding. The is_remote_inferred field is 0% everywhere (not functioning for this data).

Within-metro remote rates (2026): All 0% because remote postings are not assigned to metros. The 22.6% remote share exists in the non-metro-assigned pool.

Interpretation: Remote SWE postings constitute at least 22.6% of the 2026 market but are invisible to metro-level analysis. Our metro-level findings describe the non-remote segment of the market.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
Metric Base mean change No-agg mean change % difference Entry share +10.3pp +8.0pp -22.4% AI prevalence +20.6pp +21.6pp +4.9% Org scope +1.4 +1.6 +12.9% Median desc length +1,609 +1,730 +7.6% Tech diversity +2.5 +2.9 +15.4%

Entry share change drops by 22% without aggregators (below the 30% materiality threshold). All other metrics change by less than 16%. No direction changes. Aggregators amplify entry-share trends but do not create them.

docs.title: (b) Company capping (max 10 per company per metro per era)
docs.text:
Capping reduces the dataset from 35,400 to 28,193 postings (20% reduction).

Metric Base mean Capped mean % difference Rank rho Entry share +10.3pp +10.7pp 3.7% 0.936 AI prevalence +20.6pp +21.2pp 2.5% 0.962 Org scope +1.4 +1.2 11.5% 0.901 Median desc length +1,609 +1,430 11.1% 0.848 Tech diversity +2.5 +2.6 4.3% 0.848

All changes less than 12%. Rank order stability is high (rho 0.85-0.96, all p < 0.001). Company capping does not materially alter any finding. The geographic patterns are not driven by any single large employer dominating a metro.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Every metro goes the same direction. All 26 metros show increases in entry share, AI prevalence, org scope, description length, and tech diversity. There is not a single counter-trend metro. This is remarkable uniformity for a 26-metro panel.

  2. Null metro-level correlation between AI and entry change. Even in the aggregate cross-section where you might expect ecological correlation, AI adoption and entry-share change are completely orthogonal (r = -0.039). This is consistent with the T16 company-level null result.

  3. NYC stands out as the least-changed metro for entry share (+2.8pp), despite being the largest SWE market. It had the highest 2024 entry share (6.9%) and shows a ceiling effect.

  4. Florida metros lead AI adoption. Miami (+34.1pp) and Tampa Bay (+32.3pp) have the highest AI surge, suggesting these growing tech markets adopted AI requirements most aggressively -- possibly because they are newer SWE markets less anchored to pre-AI patterns.

  5. Non-hub metros change as much as hubs. The hypothesis that "tech hub dynamics would drive national patterns" is refuted. If anything, second-tier metros show larger scope and tech-diversity increases (non-significant). The transformation is truly national.

  6. Remote SWE is a large blind spot. 22.6% of 2026 SWE postings are remote-tagged but invisible to metro analysis. This is a meaningful limitation: if remote postings have systematically different characteristics (which is plausible), our metro-level findings describe only the 77% of non-remote postings.

docs.title: Methodology Notes
docs.text:
• Metro assignment: Uses metro_area field (Stage 6, 26-metro study frame). 73.9% of SWE postings have metro assignments.
  • Minimum threshold: 50 SWE per metro per era (all 26 study metros qualify).
  • AI prevalence: Binary indicator for any of 17 AI technology keywords (from shared tech matrix).
  • Org scope: Regex count of management/leadership/scope terms in description_core.
  • Tech diversity: Mean count of technologies detected per posting (146-tech regex dictionary).
  • Remote work: is_remote from source data. Cross-period comparison invalid (2024 = 0% by construction). is_remote_inferred is not functioning.

docs.title: Output Files
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T17/metro_metrics_by_era.csv -- Full metro-era metrics (26 metros x 2 eras)
  • exploration/tables/T17/metro_change_ranked.csv -- Change metrics ranked by metro
  • exploration/tables/T17/metro_correlations.csv -- Pairwise metro-level correlations
  • exploration/tables/T17/remote_by_metro_2026.csv -- Remote work share by metro (2026)
  • exploration/tables/T17/sensitivity_comparison.csv -- Sensitivity analysis results
  • exploration/figures/T17/metro_heatmap.png -- Metro x metric change heatmap (z-scored)
  • exploration/figures/T17/ai_vs_entry_metro.png -- AI adoption vs entry change scatter (hub-colored)
  • exploration/figures/T17/metro_bar_charts.png -- Ranked bar charts for entry and AI change
  • exploration/figures/T17/remote_by_metro.png -- Remote share by metro (all 0% due to data structure)
  • exploration/scripts/T17_geographic_structure.py -- Analysis script

docs.title: T18: Cross-Occupation Boundary Analysis
docs.text:
Task: Determine which observed changes in SWE postings (2024 to 2026) are SWE-specific vs field-wide labor market trends.

Method: Compare parallel trends across three occupation groups -- SWE (n=52K), SWE-adjacent (n=20K), and Control (accountants, nurses, engineers -- n=142K) -- using difference-in-differences. TF-IDF boundary analysis for occupational convergence. Keyword searches use description (full text) to avoid boilerplate-removal recall loss.

Bottom line: AI adoption and tech stack expansion are overwhelmingly SWE-specific. Scope inflation (collaboration, mentoring, strategy language) is partly field-wide but has a meaningful SWE-excess component. Description length growth is largely field-wide.

docs.title: 1.1 AI/ML Keyword Prevalence
docs.text:
Period SWE SWE-adjacent Control Jan 2024 14.8% 10.9% 0.4% Apr 2024 12.3% 14.3% 0.4% Mar 2026 39.1% 38.3% 2.8%

SWE AI prevalence nearly tripled from ~12-15% to 39%. Control occupations went from 0.4% to 2.8% -- a 7x relative increase but still negligible in absolute terms. The SWE-Control gap widened from ~12pp to ~36pp.

GenAI-specific terms (LLM, GPT, generative AI, copilot, langchain, agentic, etc.): - SWE: 2.7% -> 3.1% -> 23.3% (8x increase in absolute terms) - SWE-adjacent: 1.5% -> 3.6% -> 20.4% - Control: 0.0% -> 0.0% -> 0.8%

AI tool usage language (ai-powered, ai-assisted, leverage AI, ai-driven): - SWE: 1.8% -> 2.5% -> 21.3% (8.5x) - Control: 0.0% -> 0.1% -> 1.5%

AI adoption in job postings is overwhelmingly concentrated in SWE and SWE-adjacent roles, with minimal spillover to control occupations.

docs.title: 1.2 Description Length
docs.text:
Period SWE (median core) SWE-adj Control Jan 2024 2,419 2,624 2,280 Apr 2024 2,504 2,764 1,928 Mar 2026 4,171 4,471 3,160

All three groups show substantial description length growth. SWE grew +1,667 chars (Apr 2024 to Mar 2026), Control grew +1,232 chars. The DiD is +435 chars -- meaningful but modest relative to the overall growth. Description length expansion is predominantly a field-wide phenomenon, with a mild SWE excess.

docs.title: 1.3 Scope / Organizational Language
docs.text:
Metric SWE change (04->26) Control change (04->26) DiD (SWE - Control) Cross-functional/stakeholder +16.0pp +10.7pp +5.3pp Mentoring +11.6pp +10.6pp +1.0pp Leadership +9.7pp +9.9pp -0.2pp Soft skills -0.6pp +4.5pp -5.1pp Strategy/roadmap +9.4pp +2.4pp +7.0pp

Mentoring and leadership language growth is entirely field-wide -- the DiD is near zero. Cross-functional/stakeholder language has a modest SWE-specific excess (+5.3pp). Strategy/roadmap language growth is partly SWE-specific (+7.0pp). Soft skills actually grew less in SWE than in control occupations.

docs.title: 1.4 Tech Mention Count (30 tracked technologies)
docs.text:
Period SWE SWE-adjacent Control Jan 2024 4.74 2.18 0.31 Apr 2024 4.79 2.61 0.33 Mar 2026 5.67 3.64 0.43

SWE change: +0.88. Control change: +0.10. DiD: +0.78. Tech stack expansion is strongly SWE-specific. Control occupations barely mention these technologies at all.

docs.title: 1.5 Seniority Distribution
docs.text:
Period SWE junior Control junior SWE senior Control senior Jan 2024 0.6%* 0.4%* 93.2% 94.7% Apr 2024 16.5% 19.0% 59.9% 58.8% Mar 2026 13.5% 40.9% 77.2% 50.9%

*Jan 2024 (asaniczka) has no native entry-level labels -- values represent imputed entries only.

Critical finding: Control occupations have a higher junior share (40.9%) than SWE (13.5%) in March 2026. From 2024-04 to 2026-03, SWE junior share declined 3.0pp while control junior share increased 21.9pp. The DiD is -24.9pp. This is strongly SWE-specific -- the junior role compression is not a general labor market trend; it is the opposite of what happened in control occupations.

docs.title: 2. Difference-in-Differences Summary
docs.text:
Metric SWE change (04->26) Control change DiD (SWE-Ctrl) SWE-specific? AI prevalence (broad) +26.7pp +2.4pp +24.4pp YES, strongly GenAI prevalence +20.2pp +0.8pp +19.4pp YES, strongly AI tool language +18.8pp +1.5pp +17.4pp YES, strongly Tech mention count +0.88 +0.10 +0.78 YES Strategy/roadmap +9.4pp +2.4pp +7.0pp YES, moderate Cross-functional +16.0pp +10.7pp +5.3pp Partly Description length +1,667 chars +1,232 chars +435 chars Partly Mentoring +11.6pp +10.6pp +1.0pp NO, field-wide Leadership +9.7pp +9.9pp -0.2pp NO, field-wide Soft skills -0.6pp +4.5pp -5.1pp NO, inverse

SWE vs SWE-adjacent DiD: The DiD between SWE and SWE-adjacent is near zero for most metrics (AI: +2.7pp, tech: -0.15, collab: -4.9pp). SWE and SWE-adjacent move in lockstep. The distinguishing boundary is between technical roles (SWE + adjacent) vs non-technical control occupations.

docs.title: 3.1 TF-IDF Cosine Similarity (500 samples per group)
docs.text:
Period SWE <-> SWE-adj SWE <-> Control Adj <-> Control Jan 2024 0.853 0.409 0.449 Apr 2024 0.871 0.434 0.470 Mar 2026 0.875 0.507 0.522

SWE and SWE-adjacent remain highly similar (0.85-0.88) and this is stable or slightly increasing over time. SWE-Control and Adj-Control similarity increased modestly (0.41->0.51 and 0.45->0.52), suggesting all job descriptions are becoming more generically similar -- likely an artifact of increasing description length adding shared boilerplate language.

docs.title: 3.2 Term Migration
docs.text:
Shared terms gaining importance (2024 -> 2026): "ai" became a top shared term between SWE and SWE-adjacent by 2026 (SWE=0.055, Adj=0.043) that was not prominent in 2024.

Persistent SWE-distinctive terms: "software", "code", "development", "java", "backend", "software engineer", "stack", "developer" -- core SWE vocabulary remains stable.

Persistent SWE-adj distinctive terms: "security", "network", "data", "analytics", "data science", "database", "business" -- domain-specific vocabulary is preserved.

The boundary between SWE and SWE-adjacent is stable, not blurring. The groups share more AI vocabulary in 2026 but retain their distinct domain identities.

docs.title: 4.1 Data Scientist
docs.text:
• AI prevalence: 76% -> 82% -> 84% (already high, modest further increase)
  • GenAI: 12% -> 19% -> 34% (meaningful GenAI-specific growth)
  • Cross-functional: 49% -> 59% -> 72% (strong scope expansion)
  • Description length (med core): 2,854 -> 3,352 -> 4,712 (+1,360)

docs.title: 4.2 Network Engineer
docs.text:
• AI prevalence: 2.0% -> 4.0% -> 10.4% (low base, meaningful 5x growth)
  • GenAI: 0% -> 0% -> 3.2% (emerging GenAI presence)
  • Cross-functional: 10% -> 21% -> 36% (strong scope expansion)
  • Description length: 2,308 -> 2,814 -> 4,076 (+1,262)

docs.title: 4.3 Security Engineer
docs.text:
• AI prevalence: 4.6% -> 6.4% -> 17.1% (tripled)
  • GenAI: 0.8% -> 1.3% -> 11.3% (major GenAI surge)
  • Cross-functional: 23% -> 28% -> 47% (strong scope expansion)
  • Description length: 3,049 -> 3,518 -> 4,968 (+1,450)

docs.title: 4.4 Quality Engineer
docs.text:
• AI prevalence: 0.8% -> 0.7% -> 5.4% (low but growing)
  • GenAI: 0% -> 0% -> 0.9% (minimal)
  • Cross-functional: 33% -> 37% -> 48% (scope expansion)

Pattern: Security and data science roles show meaningful AI adoption, while network and quality engineering show much less. Scope expansion (cross-functional language) is universal across all adjacent roles.

Caveat on data engineers: Most "data engineer" titles are classified as SWE (n=1,260 in Jan 2024), not SWE-adjacent (n=110). The small SWE-adjacent data engineer sample has elevated AI prevalence (69%) because the few postings that failed the SWE classifier tend to be ML-heavy outliers. Data engineer trends should be interpreted from the SWE sample, not the adjacent sample.

docs.title: 5. AI Adoption Gradient
docs.text:
The SWE-Control AI gap is widening:

Period SWE-Ctrl gap SWE-Adj gap Jan 2024 14.4pp 3.8pp Apr 2024 11.9pp -2.0pp Mar 2026 36.3pp 0.7pp

The SWE-to-SWE-adjacent gap is near zero -- these groups track each other closely. The meaningful divide is between technical roles (SWE + adjacent, ~39% AI prevalence) and non-technical control roles (~3% AI prevalence). The gradient is widening dramatically.

docs.title: 6a. Aggregator exclusion
docs.text: After removing aggregator postings: - SWE AI prevalence: 13.1% -> 40.9% (slightly higher without aggregators) - Pattern is robust. Aggregator-filtered SWE AI prevalence is actually slightly higher, suggesting aggregator-sourced postings slightly dilute AI content.

docs.title: 6g. SWE classification tier (regex-only)
docs.text: Restricting to regex-tier SWE (highest confidence classification): - AI prevalence: 12.7% -> 39.9% (essentially identical to full sample) - All trends fully replicate at highest-confidence classification tier.

docs.title: What is SWE-specific (strong DiD)
docs.text:
1. AI/ML adoption -- 24pp excess over control. This is the defining SWE-specific change.
  2. GenAI adoption -- 19pp excess. GenAI terms barely exist in control occupations.
  3. AI tool language -- 17pp excess. "AI-powered", "AI-assisted" etc. are SWE phenomena.
  4. Tech stack expansion -- +0.78 more technologies listed than control's increase.
  5. Junior role compression -- SWE junior share declined while control's increased.
  6. Strategy/roadmap language -- 7pp SWE excess.

docs.title: What is partly SWE-specific (moderate DiD)
docs.text:
1. Cross-functional/stakeholder language -- 5pp excess. Some SWE-specific component.
  2. Description length growth -- Both grew, SWE grew slightly more.

docs.title: What is field-wide (near-zero DiD)
docs.text:
1. Mentoring language -- identical growth across occupations.
  2. Leadership language -- identical growth.
  3. Soft skills -- actually grew less in SWE than control.

docs.title: Implications for the paper
docs.text: The "AI-driven SWE restructuring" story is strongly supported. The core findings -- AI adoption, tech stack growth, junior share decline -- are overwhelmingly SWE-specific, not general labor market trends. However, some aspects of "scope inflation" (mentoring, leadership language) are field-wide phenomena that should not be attributed solely to AI. The paper should distinguish between: - SWE-specific restructuring: AI adoption, tech stack complexity, junior compression, strategic scope - Field-wide posting evolution: Description length growth, mentoring/leadership language, general scope expansion

docs.text:

  • exploration/figures/T18/ai_adoption_gradient.png -- AI category breakdown
  • exploration/figures/T18/boundary_similarity.png -- TF-IDF cosine similarity
  • exploration/figures/T18/adjacent_role_trends.png -- Adjacent role metrics

docs.text:

  • exploration/tables/T18/ai_prevalence_by_group_period.csv
  • exploration/tables/T18/description_length_by_group_period.csv

docs.title: T19: Temporal Patterns & Rate-of-Change Estimation
docs.text:
Task: Characterize temporal structure across three discrete snapshots and estimate rates of change, including acceleration.

Snapshots: Asaniczka (Jan 2024), Arshkon (Apr 2024), Scraped (Mar 2026).

Bottom line: GenAI prevalence accelerated dramatically (8x), description length grew at 2.5x the within-2024 rate, while entry-level share dynamics are dominated by cross-source measurement differences. Within-March metrics are stable (CV < 10% for most measures), supporting the scraped data as a reliable snapshot. Scraper yield shows moderate first-day backlog (1.6x) with clear weekend dips.

docs.title: 1.1 Snapshot Values
docs.text: Metric Jan 2024 Apr 2024 Mar 2026 Entry share (known seniority) 0.56% 20.4% 14.5% AI prevalence (broad) 14.8% 12.3% 39.1% GenAI prevalence 2.5% 2.8% 23.2% Description length (median core) 2,419 2,504 4,171 Tech mention count (mean) 4.62 4.68 5.50 Scope density (collab/stakeholder) 27.3% 29.1% 45.1%

docs.title: 1.2 Annualized Rates and Acceleration
docs.text:
Metric Within-2024 (annualized) Cross-period (annualized) Acceleration ratio Entry share +79.4pp/yr* -3.0pp/yr -0.04 AI prevalence -9.7pp/yr +13.4pp/yr -1.37** GenAI prevalence +1.2pp/yr +10.2pp/yr 8.25 Description length +340 chars/yr +834 chars/yr 2.45 Tech mention count +0.27/yr +0.41/yr 1.53 Scope density +7.5pp/yr +8.0pp/yr 1.06

* Entry share within-2024 rate is meaningless -- the Jan 2024 value (0.56%) reflects asaniczka's lack of native entry-level labels, not actual entry-level prevalence.

** AI prevalence shows a negative within-2024 rate because asaniczka (14.8%) > arshkon (12.3%). This is likely a source composition artifact rather than a real decrease.

docs.title: 1.3 Interpretation
docs.text:
Genuine acceleration signals: 1. GenAI prevalence: 8.25x acceleration. Within-2024, GenAI was growing at +1.2pp/year. Cross-period, it grew at +10.2pp/year. This is the single strongest acceleration signal and aligns with the release of GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, o1, and subsequent models in the intervening period.

  1. Description length: 2.45x acceleration. Descriptions grew faster in the 2024-2026 period than within 2024. This could reflect genuine content expansion or evolving platform norms.

  2. Tech mention count: 1.53x acceleration. Moderate acceleration in the number of technologies mentioned per posting.

Stable rate (no acceleration): - Scope density: 1.06x. Cross-functional/stakeholder language grew at essentially the same rate within 2024 and cross-period. This suggests scope inflation was already underway in early 2024.

Unreliable rates (cross-source artifacts): - Entry share -- confounded by asaniczka's missing entry-level labels. - Broad AI prevalence -- the within-2024 dip is likely a source artifact.

docs.title: 2. Within-Arshkon Stability
docs.text:
Arshkon data spans approximately 3 weeks (April 1-15, 2024) with date_posted available.

Post week n AI share Med core len Tech count Entry share Apr 1 396 13.4% 2,647 4.88 22.9% Apr 8 912 12.6% 2,570 4.72 22.3% Apr 15 3,711 12.2% 2,472 4.65 19.6%

Coefficient of variation:

Metric CV AI share 0.049 Description length 0.034 Tech count 0.024 Entry share 0.080

All metrics show very low week-to-week variation (CV < 10%). Entry share shows the highest variation at 8%, driven by the first week's smaller sample. The arshkon data represents a stable snapshot rather than a rapidly evolving period.

docs.title: 3.1 Daily Volumes (12 days, Mar 20-31 2026)
docs.text:
Date All rows SWE SWE-adj Control Mar 20 16,974 3,047 798 2,527 Mar 21 9,639 1,415 429 1,571 Mar 22 9,088 1,215 367 1,606 Mar 23 (Sun) 6,001 865 238 818 Mar 24 15,072 2,679 628 2,167 Mar 25 16,098 2,805 735 2,412 Mar 26 15,467 2,597 818 2,248 Mar 27 14,886 2,707 732 1,908 Mar 28 14,550 2,314 702 2,148 Mar 29 (Sat) 7,925 1,086 303 1,100 Mar 30 (Sun) 6,320 927 255 873 Mar 31 13,497 2,438 662 1,913

First-day backlog: 1.6x the steady-state average (3,047 vs ~1,913 SWE/day). Moderate backlog effect -- not extreme enough to bias the snapshot.

Weekend dips: Saturday and Sunday show approximately 40-55% of weekday volume. This reflects LinkedIn posting patterns, not scraper limitations.

Steady-state yield: Approximately 2,500 SWE postings/day on weekdays, 1,000/day on weekends.

docs.title: 4.1 Key Metrics by Day (SWE postings)
docs.text:
Metric Mean Std CV Interpretation AI share 39.2% 1.9pp 0.047 Very stable GenAI share 23.1% 2.0pp 0.088 Stable Desc length (median core) 4,242 290 0.068 Stable Tech count (mean) 5.44 0.28 0.051 Very stable Entry share 14.9% 2.5pp 0.167 Moderate variation Collab share 45.0% 2.0pp 0.045 Very stable

Most metrics have CV below 10%, indicating the March snapshot is temporally stable. Entry share shows the highest variation (CV 16.7%), which is expected given smaller cell sizes for the junior seniority category.

docs.title: 4.2 Day-of-Week Effects
docs.text:
Day Mean n AI share Med core len Monday 896 39.6% 4,466 Tuesday 2,558 37.9% 3,900 Wednesday 2,805 39.6% 4,034 Thursday 2,597 37.9% 4,130 Friday 2,877 41.0% 4,191 Saturday 1,864 37.4% 4,213 Sunday 1,150 40.1% 4,599

No meaningful day-of-week effects on metric values. Volume varies (weekday ~2,500 vs weekend ~1,000-1,500), but conditional on being posted, metrics are stable across days. This is reassuring for the validity of the snapshot.

docs.title: 5. Timeline Contextualization
docs.text:
Jan 2024: ASANICZKA SNAPSHOT
  Context: GPT-4 (Mar 2023), Claude 2 (Jul 2023)
  --- 3 months ---
Apr 2024: ARSHKON SNAPSHOT
  Context: GPT-4o imminent, Gemini 1.5 Pro (Feb 2024)
  --- AI release cascade ---
May 2024: GPT-4o
Jun 2024: Claude 3.5 Sonnet
Sep 2024: o1
Dec 2024: DeepSeek V3
  --- continued acceleration ---
May 2025: Claude 4 Opus
Sep 2025: Claude 4 Sonnet
Jan 2026: Claude Opus 4.5
  --- 2 months ---
Mar 2026: SCRAPED SNAPSHOT
  Context: Claude Opus 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, DeepSeek R2

Between the 2024 snapshots and the 2026 snapshot, at least 8 major AI model releases occurred. The GenAI acceleration (8.25x) is consistent with the hypothesis that this wave of releases drove rapid adoption of AI/ML requirements in job postings.

The within-2024 period (Jan to Apr) spans before the major 2024 release wave (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, o1). The low within-2024 GenAI growth rate (+1.2pp/yr) vs the high cross-period rate (+10.2pp/yr) is temporally consistent with the model release timeline.

docs.title: 6. Key Caveats
docs.text:
1. Three-point estimation is fragile. With only three snapshots, within-2024 rates are estimated from a single 3-month difference (Jan-Apr 2024). Small source composition differences can dominate.

  2. Cross-source confounds for entry share. Asaniczka (Jan 2024) has zero native entry-level labels. Entry share comparisons between asaniczka and arshkon are meaningless. Only arshkon-to-scraped is valid for entry share trends.

  3. Within-2024 AI prevalence dip. Asaniczka shows higher AI prevalence (14.8%) than arshkon (12.3%). This is likely a source composition effect rather than a genuine 3-month decline. Different search queries, sample frames, and LinkedIn API access could explain this.

  4. Acceleration ratios with small denominators. When the within-2024 rate is near zero or negative, the acceleration ratio is undefined or misleading. Interpret with caution.

  5. First-day scraper backlog. The first scrape day (Mar 20) has 1.6x normal volume. This is mild and unlikely to bias metrics, but worth noting for volume-based analyses.

docs.title: T20: Seniority Boundary Clarity
docs.text:
Task: Measure how sharp the boundaries between seniority levels are and whether they blurred between 2024 and 2026.

Sample: SWE, LinkedIn-only, seniority_final != unknown. 2024: arshkon + asaniczka combined (n=27,279). 2026: scraped (n=22,469). Total: 49,748 rows.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text: Seniority boundaries are ASYMMETRICALLY EVOLVING. Lower boundaries (entry/associate, associate/mid-senior) SHARPENED from 2024 to 2026, while the top boundary (mid-senior/director) BLURRED dramatically. The features that separate levels also shifted: in 2024, YOE and tech count dominated separation; in 2026, scope density, management language, and AI mentions became the primary discriminators at lower boundaries. Meanwhile, associate roles moved CLOSER to entry in feature space -- not disappearing, but collapsing downward.

docs.title: Boundary Discriminability (AUC)
docs.text:
Boundary AUC 2024 AUC 2026 Change Interpretation Entry vs Associate 0.561 0.592 +0.031 SHARPENED Associate vs Mid-Senior 0.675 0.703 +0.028 SHARPENED Mid-Senior vs Director 0.750 0.639 -0.111 BLURRED

Method: L2 logistic regression, 5-fold stratified CV, 7 features (YOE, tech count, AI mention, scope density, management density, description length log, education ordinal).

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
1. Entry/Associate boundary sharpened slightly (0.561 to 0.592). This is still a very low AUC -- barely above random (0.5) -- meaning entry and associate roles remain nearly indistinguishable by structured features. The small improvement suggests 2026 entry-level postings have become marginally MORE distinctive from associate.

  2. Associate/Mid-Senior boundary sharpened (0.675 to 0.703). The moderate discriminability reflects a genuine step function in requirements at this boundary, and it became slightly more pronounced.

  3. Mid-Senior/Director boundary BLURRED substantially (-0.111 AUC). This is the most striking finding. In 2024, director roles were clearly distinguishable from mid-senior (AUC=0.75) by their higher YOE, longer descriptions, more scope language, and more management language. In 2026, this distinction weakened considerably (AUC=0.639). This suggests mid-senior roles absorbed characteristics previously distinctive to directors -- OR that director postings became less distinctive in their language.

docs.title: What Drives Separation? Feature Importance Shifts
Entry vs Associate
docs.text:
Rank 2024 2026 1 Tech Count Scope Density 2 Description Length Management Density 3 Management Density AI Mention 4 AI Mention Tech Count 5 Education Description Length

In 2024, the entry/associate boundary was primarily about technical breadth (tech count, description length). In 2026, scope language, management expectations, and AI mentions became the top discriminators. This aligns with T11's finding that entry-level roles absorbed scope/management language disproportionately.

docs.title: Associate vs Mid-Senior
docs.text:
Rank 2024 2026 1 YOE YOE 2 Tech Count Description Length 3 Scope Density AI Mention 4 Education Education 5 Management Density Tech Count

YOE remains the strongest separator at this boundary in both periods. However, AI mention rose significantly as a discriminator in 2026, consistent with mid-senior roles absorbing more AI requirements than associate roles.

docs.title: Mid-Senior vs Director
docs.text:
Rank 2024 2026 1 YOE Tech Count 2 Description Length Scope Density 3 Scope Density Description Length 4 Management Density AI Mention 5 Education YOE

The most dramatic shift. In 2024, YOE was the primary director discriminator; in 2026, it dropped to rank 5. Tech count and scope density rose to the top. This suggests the "director" designation in 2026 is less about accumulated experience and more about technical breadth and organizational scope -- but these features are increasingly shared with mid-senior roles, explaining the blurring.

docs.title: The "Missing Middle": Associate Role Positioning
docs.text:
Associate roles are collapsing TOWARD entry, not mid-senior.

Metric Period Associate-to-Entry Distance Associate-to-MidSenior Distance Relative Position (0=entry, 1=midsenior) Feature space (Euclidean) 2024 0.913 2.153 0.298 Feature space (Euclidean) 2026 0.335 1.822 0.155

In 2024, associate sat at position 0.30 between entry and mid-senior (closer to entry). In 2026, it moved to 0.16 -- significantly closer to entry. The associate-to-entry distance collapsed from 0.913 to 0.335, while the associate-to-mid-senior distance only decreased from 2.153 to 1.822.

docs.title: Feature-by-feature comparison
docs.text:
Feature Entry 2024 Assoc 2024 Entry 2026 Assoc 2026 Entry-Assoc gap 2024 Entry-Assoc gap 2026 YOE (imputed) 4.39 4.39 4.07 4.09 0.00 0.02 Tech Count 5.43 4.53 6.91 7.17 -0.90 0.26 AI Mention 2.9% 2.5% 19.9% 17.7% -0.4pp -2.2pp Scope Density 0.37 0.39 0.32 0.45 0.02 0.13 Mgmt Density 0.94 1.05 0.90 1.05 0.11 0.15 Desc Length (log) 7.25 7.32 7.86 7.84 0.07 -0.02 Education 0.77 0.81 0.82 0.80 0.04 -0.02

The critical observation: In 2024, entry and associate had near-identical YOE, similar AI rates, and the gap was mostly in tech count (associate had LESS tech breadth than entry -- counterintuitive but reflects asaniczka associate labeling). In 2026, both levels converged further: nearly identical YOE, description length, education, and AI rates. The remaining gap is in scope density and management density, where associate maintains a slight premium.

Interpretation: Associate is not "disappearing" in the sense of being eliminated, but its feature profile is becoming a near-duplicate of entry. If entry-level roles absorbed scope/management/AI requirements that previously distinguished associate, then the associate category loses its structural meaning. This is consistent with a "flattening" of the bottom two tiers.

docs.title: Full Feature Profile Heatmap
docs.text:
Z-scored feature values by seniority x period (see exploration/figures/T20/feature_profile_heatmap.png):

Key patterns visible in the heatmap: - YOE shows the expected monotonic increase with seniority, but the step from director 2024 to director 2026 is notable (7.72 to 5.78 -- YOE expectations for directors dropped substantially) - AI Mention was near-zero everywhere in 2024 (all z < 0) and elevated everywhere in 2026, with the largest increase at entry and mid-senior - Management Density shows the largest director-2024 value (1.80) but in 2026 this compressed (director=1.38, mid-senior=1.29), while entry INCREASED (0.94 to 1.10) - Scope Density is the clearest hierarchical signal: monotonically increases with seniority in both periods

docs.title: (a) Aggregator Exclusion
docs.text:
Boundary Period AUC (all) AUC (no aggregator) Diff Entry vs Associate 2024 0.561 0.556 -0.005 Entry vs Associate 2026 0.592 0.600 +0.008 Associate vs Mid-Senior 2024 0.675 0.661 -0.014 Associate vs Mid-Senior 2026 0.703 0.700 -0.003 Mid-Senior vs Director 2024 0.750 0.689 -0.061 Mid-Senior vs Director 2026 0.639 0.641 +0.002

The blurring/sharpening pattern is robust to aggregator exclusion. One notable detail: the mid-senior/director boundary in 2024 drops from 0.750 to 0.689 without aggregators, suggesting aggregators contributed some spurious director discriminability in 2024. The 2026 director blurring is essentially unchanged.

docs.title: (c) Seniority Operationalization (3-level)
docs.text:
Boundary (3-level) AUC 2024 AUC 2026 Change Junior vs Mid 0.561 0.592 +0.031 Mid vs Senior 0.675 0.703 +0.028

Using 3-level seniority (junior/mid/senior) produces identical AUCs for the lower boundaries (because the 3-level mapping is entry+associate -> junior/mid, mid-senior+director -> senior). This confirms the findings are not an artifact of 5-level classification granularity.

docs.title: Sample Sizes
docs.text:
Period Entry Associate Mid-Senior Director 2024 960 1,678 24,528 113 2026 3,255 622 18,370 222

Note: The 2024 director sample (n=113) is small but grew to 222 in 2026. Both entry and director samples are adequate for the logistic regression but less precise than the large mid-senior sample.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
1. The seniority system is restructuring from the top down. Director roles are becoming less distinguishable from mid-senior roles, while the entry/associate boundary sharpened slightly. This is consistent with a model where AI tools flatten the top of the hierarchy (orchestration and scope language diffusing to mid-senior) while scope inflation at the bottom makes entry roles more distinctive from a "new normal" associate.

  2. Associate is collapsing toward entry. The feature-space distance between associate and entry halved from 2024 to 2026, while the gap to mid-senior barely changed. This suggests a de facto 3-tier system: entry/associate (junior), mid-senior (fully independent), director (barely distinct from mid-senior). The traditional 5-level hierarchy is compressing to ~3 meaningful levels.

  3. The features that define boundaries changed. YOE and tech count mattered most in 2024. In 2026, scope density, management language, and AI mentions became the primary boundary markers. This means seniority is increasingly defined by WHAT you do (orchestrate, manage, scope) rather than HOW LONG you have done it.

  4. Director YOE expectations fell substantially (7.72 to 5.78 mean). Combined with the boundary blurring, this suggests a redefinition of director away from "senior person with long tenure" toward "technically broad person with scope."

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
• Figures: exploration/figures/T20/ (4 PNGs)
  • auc_comparison.png -- AUC by boundary and period
  • feature_importance_comparison.png -- coefficient comparison by boundary
  • feature_profile_heatmap.png -- z-scored feature profiles (seniority x period)
  • missing_middle.png -- feature trajectories across seniority levels
  • Tables: exploration/tables/T20/ (5 CSVs)
  • auc_comparison.csv -- AUC comparison data
  • feature_importance.csv -- full feature importance data
  • seniority_profiles.csv -- mean feature profiles by seniority x period
  • sensitivity_aggregator.csv -- aggregator exclusion sensitivity
  • sensitivity_3level.csv -- 3-level seniority sensitivity
  • Script: exploration/scripts/T20_seniority_boundaries.py

docs.title: T21: Senior Role Evolution Deep Dive
docs.text:
Task: Deep analysis of how senior SWE roles evolved. Compare management language in senior postings to the entry-level surge from T11. Test whether management language migrated downward or expanded everywhere.

Sample: SWE, LinkedIn-only, seniority_final != unknown. Senior focus: mid-senior + director (n=43,233). Full cross-seniority: all 4 levels (n=49,748).

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
Management language EXPANDED EVERYWHERE -- it did NOT migrate downward. The T11 finding that entry-level management indicators jumped from 9.4% to 40.8% is real, but senior roles saw a parallel increase (23.1% to 49.9% at mid-senior, using T11-compatible narrow metric). The increase was largest in absolute terms at entry (+31pp) and smallest at director (+7pp), creating a convergence pattern: all seniority levels are moving toward a common management language baseline. This is NOT redistribution from senior to junior -- it is universal expansion, likely driven by AI-era posting templates that embed management/mentoring/coaching language as default expectations.

The senior role itself restructured along a different axis: technical orchestration surged (+16%) while management density shifted modestly. AI-mentioning senior postings show a distinct profile: HIGHER orchestration language, LOWER management language. A "technical orchestrator" archetype is emerging within the senior cohort.

docs.title: Three-Dimensional Language Profiles
docs.text: Each posting measured on three density dimensions (mentions per 1K characters):

docs.title: Mid-Senior Roles
docs.text: Dimension 2024 (n=24,528) 2026 (n=18,370) Change People Management 1.18 1.29 +9.1% Technical Orchestration 0.97 1.12 +15.8% Strategic Scope 0.38 0.37 -1.0%

docs.title: Director Roles
docs.text:
Dimension 2024 (n=113) 2026 (n=222) Change People Management 1.80 1.38 -23.2% Technical Orchestration 0.76 1.10 +45.6% Strategic Scope 0.62 0.56 -10.8%

Key observation: Directors are pivoting AWAY from people management and TOWARD technical orchestration. Mid-senior roles are absorbing orchestration language while maintaining management language. This suggests the "director as people manager" archetype is being partly replaced by "director as technical/AI orchestrator."

docs.title: Using T11-compatible narrow management patterns
docs.text:
These are the specific T11 patterns (team management, direct reports, hiring, performance reviews, mentoring juniors/engineers, coaching teams, recruiting, staffing, budget management):

Seniority 2024 2026 Change (pp) Entry 9.5% 40.8% +31.3 Associate 9.2% 33.8% +24.6 Mid-Senior 23.1% 49.9% +26.8 Director 47.8% 54.5% +6.7

docs.title: Using broad management patterns (any mention of manage/mentor/coach/etc.)
Statistical significance (broad management density, Mann-Whitney U)
docs.text: Seniority Direction p-value Effect r Sig Entry INCREASED 1.0e-14 0.164 *** Associate INCREASED 3.0e-4 0.097 *** Mid-Senior INCREASED 1.3e-110 0.126 *** Director DECREASED 0.026 -0.149 *

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
The migration hypothesis is REJECTED. If management language had migrated from senior to junior, we would expect senior management language to DECREASE while junior increased. Instead:

  • Entry, associate, and mid-senior management language ALL increased significantly (p < 0.001)
  • Director management DENSITY decreased (p = 0.026), but director BINARY presence still increased (85% to 96.4%)
  • The director density decrease reflects a pivot away from HEAVY management language, not an absence of it

The correct interpretation is UNIVERSAL EXPANSION with CONVERGENCE. All levels are converging toward a common baseline where management/mentoring/coaching language is nearly universal. The entry-level "surge" is the most visible because entry started from the lowest base (9.5% narrow, 65.3% broad), but it is part of a market-wide pattern.

The one exception is director-level density. Directors in 2026 use LESS management language per character than in 2024 (1.80 to 1.38 density), even though nearly all (96.4%) mention it at least once. The text space freed by reducing management language appears to have been filled by technical orchestration language (+45.6%).

docs.title: Senior Sub-Archetypes
docs.text:
K-means clustering on [management density, orchestration density, strategic scope density] across all senior roles. Best k=4 (silhouette=0.364).

Cluster Name n Mgmt Orch Strat AI% 2024 Share 2026 Share Shift 0 Low-touch Generalist 25,305 0.73 0.76 0.18 11.8% 58.8% 58.2% -0.5pp 1 People Manager 9,079 2.74 0.86 0.25 11.3% 19.9% 22.4% +2.5pp 2 Technical Orchestrator 2,187 0.98 5.66 0.27 18.0% 4.5% 5.8% +1.3pp 3 Strategic Leader 6,662 1.17 0.81 1.33 12.8% 16.8% 13.5% -3.3pp

docs.title: Archetype evolution
docs.text:
• Low-touch Generalist (59% of senior roles): Stable across periods. These are standard senior SWE postings with moderate levels of all three language dimensions. Neither growing nor shrinking.

  • People Manager archetype GREW (+2.5pp): Postings emphasizing team management, mentoring, hiring. This grew slightly, suggesting the "engineering manager who codes" role is expanding.

  • Technical Orchestrator GREW (+1.3pp): This is the most AI-concentrated cluster (18% AI mention rate vs 11-13% for others). Dominant in code review, system design, pipeline, automation, quality gates language. Small but growing -- this is the nascent "AI orchestrator" role.

  • Strategic Leader SHRANK (-3.3pp): Postings emphasizing stakeholder engagement, business impact, roadmap, budgeting. This is the traditional "director-profile" cluster, and it declined. Consistent with the director density decreases seen above.

docs.title: The "New Senior" Question
docs.text:
There is no wholly new archetype that was absent in 2024. However, the Technical Orchestrator cluster (Cluster 2) shows the strongest growth signal relative to its base:

  • 2024: 4.5% of senior roles (1,110 postings)
  • 2026: 5.8% of senior roles (1,077 postings)
  • AI mention rate: 18.0% (highest of any cluster)
  • Orchestration density: 5.66 (7x the next cluster)

The Technical Orchestrator existed in 2024 but is gaining share. Its AI rate is 50% higher than the market average for senior roles. This cluster most closely matches the hypothesized "AI orchestrator" -- a senior role focused on designing systems, reviewing code, managing technical quality, and integrating AI workflows, with relatively less people management.

docs.title: AI Interaction in Senior Roles
docs.text:
Among senior postings (mid-senior + director):

Period Group n Mgmt Density Orch Density Strat Density 2024 Non-AI 23,919 1.18 0.96 0.38 2024 AI-mentioning 722 1.19 1.10 0.44 2026 Non-AI 14,047 1.32 1.01 0.36 2026 AI-mentioning 4,545 1.21 1.47 0.42

AI-mentioning senior roles have a distinctive profile: HIGHER orchestration language (1.47 vs 1.01 in 2026), HIGHER strategic scope (0.42 vs 0.36), but LOWER management density (1.21 vs 1.32). This supports the hypothesis that AI-era senior roles emphasize technical orchestration over people management.

The gap between AI-mentioning and non-AI senior roles WIDENED from 2024 to 2026: - Orchestration gap: 0.14 (2024) to 0.46 (2026) -- 3x wider - Management gap: -0.01 (2024) to -0.11 (2026) -- reversed to negative

In 2024, AI-mentioning senior postings looked much like non-AI ones. In 2026, they are becoming a distinct subtype: more technically orchestrative, less people-management focused.

docs.title: Director Deep Dive
docs.text:
Metric Director 2024 (n=113) Director 2026 (n=222) Mid-Senior 2024 (n=24,528) Mid-Senior 2026 (n=18,370) Mgmt Density 1.80 1.38 1.18 1.29 Orch Density 0.76 1.10 0.97 1.12 Strat Density 0.62 0.56 0.38 0.37 AI Rate 1.8% 21.2% 2.9% 24.5% Has Mgmt 85.0% 96.4% 75.0% 91.2% Has Orch 55.8% 85.6% 61.7% 82.7% Has Strat 51.3%* 72.5%* 36.8%* 49.3%*

(*Strategic presence rates estimated from the density/count data)

docs.title: How directors changed
docs.text:
1. Management language DECREASED in intensity but became near-universal. Density fell 23%, but binary presence rose to 96.4%. Directors mention management but dedicate less text to it.

  2. Technical orchestration SURGED. Orchestration density rose 46%, binary presence rose from 55.8% to 85.6%. Directors are now expected to engage with technical systems, code review, architecture decisions, and automation at much higher rates.

  3. AI rate jumped 12x (1.8% to 21.2%). This tracks the market-wide AI adoption but is notable because directors historically were not expected to engage with specific technologies.

  4. Strategic scope barely changed. Despite the narrative that directors are "more strategic," the data shows strategic language was already high and remained relatively stable.

docs.title: How directors differ from mid-senior in 2026
docs.text:
The gap between director and mid-senior NARROWED on nearly every dimension: - Management density gap: 0.62 (2024) to 0.09 (2026) - Orchestration density gap: -0.21 (2024) to -0.02 (2026) - Strategic density gap: 0.24 (2024) to 0.19 (2026)

The only dimension where directors maintain a clear premium is strategic scope (0.56 vs 0.37). This is consistent with the T20 finding that the mid-senior/director boundary blurred (AUC dropped from 0.75 to 0.64): directors are becoming less linguistically distinct from mid-senior roles.

docs.title: Sensitivity Check: Aggregator Exclusion
docs.text:
Period Seniority Mgmt Density (all) Mgmt Density (no agg) Diff 2024 Entry 0.949 0.960 +0.011 2024 Mid-Senior 1.182 1.170 -0.012 2024 Director 1.796 1.458 -0.338 2026 Entry 1.098 1.107 +0.009 2026 Mid-Senior 1.290 1.308 +0.018 2026 Director 1.380 1.374 -0.006

All findings robust to aggregator exclusion. The only notable difference is 2024 director density dropping when excluding aggregators (1.80 to 1.46), but the 2024 director sample is small (n=113, of which 39 are aggregator postings). The 2026 patterns are essentially unchanged.

docs.title: Surprises and Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Director management density FELL while everyone else's rose. This is the clearest signal that the director role is being redefined. Directors are pivoting from "senior people manager" to "technical and strategic leader who happens to manage."

  2. The universal management expansion was NOT anticipated. The T11 framing implied management language was something anomalously appearing in entry-level postings. It is actually appearing everywhere -- entry-level just had the most room to grow.

  3. Technical Orchestrator archetype is small (5.8%) but has the highest AI rate (18%). This may be the "AI orchestrator" role at its earliest stage. Watch for this archetype to grow in future scrapes.

  4. AI-mentioning senior postings show LOWER management density. This suggests AI adoption and people management are somewhat inversely correlated at the senior level -- AI-focused senior roles are more about technical systems than team leadership.

  5. Director AI rate (21.2%) is LOWER than mid-senior (24.5%). Directors are less likely to have AI-specific technical requirements, even in 2026. The director role retains its strategic/scope focus rather than becoming technical.

docs.title: Implications for Paper Framing
docs.text:
1. Revise the "management migration" narrative. T11's headline of +31pp management at entry level is accurate, but the framing should note this is the most visible manifestation of a UNIVERSAL expansion. The paper should say: "Management language expanded at all seniority levels, but the relative increase was largest at entry (+31pp) and smallest at director (+7pp), creating convergence toward a common baseline."

  2. Senior role redefinition operates through orchestration, not management. The senior role evolution story is NOT about losing management -- it is about GAINING orchestration. Technical orchestration density increased 16% at mid-senior and 46% at director, while management density was essentially flat (mid-senior) or decreased (director).

  3. The AI-orchestrator archetype supports RQ1. The emergence of a distinct senior subtype that emphasizes technical orchestration over people management, with the highest AI rate of any cluster, is evidence that AI is restructuring what "senior" means.

  4. The blurring of mid-senior/director (T20) aligns with the density convergence here. The two-task combination tells a coherent story: director roles are becoming less distinct because their traditional differentiators (high management, low orchestration, high scope) are converging with mid-senior profiles.

docs.text:
• Figures: exploration/figures/T21/ (4 PNGs)
  • mgmt_vs_orch_scatter.png -- 2D scatter of management vs orchestration density by period
  • three_dimension_scatter.png -- management vs strategic and orchestration vs strategic panels
  • entry_vs_senior_language.png -- THE KEY CHART: all three language dimensions across all seniority levels
  • binary_indicators_by_seniority.png -- presence rates by seniority and period
  • Tables: exploration/tables/T21/ (7 CSVs)
  • senior_language_profiles.csv -- density profiles for mid-senior and director
  • senior_archetypes.csv -- cluster characterization
  • senior_ai_interaction.csv -- AI vs non-AI senior profiles
  • director_comparison.csv -- director vs mid-senior detail
  • entry_vs_senior_mgmt.csv -- cross-seniority management comparison

docs.title: T22: Ghost & Aspirational Requirements Forensics
docs.text: Status: Complete Purpose: Stress-test the paper's headline finding that entry-level management indicators jumped from ~9.4% to ~40.8% (previous wave estimate). Determine whether scope inflation is real hiring-bar expansion or measurement artifact.

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
The headline management indicator finding is substantially inflated by measurement error. The broad pattern set used in earlier waves captures generic language ("a leading company", "cross-functional collaboration") rather than actual management responsibilities. When cleaned:

Pattern set 2024 junior 2026 junior Change Interpretation Broad (earlier waves) 39.2-39.4% 61.7% +22pp INFLATED Strict (validated) 24.6-30.1% 34.3% +4-10pp Moderate, real Moderate (careful) 26.2-31.2% 35.3% +4-9pp Moderate, real

The corrected finding: Management/scope indicators in entry-level SWE postings increased by approximately 4-10 percentage points (not 31pp), representing moderate scope inflation that is real but far less dramatic than initially reported.

AI requirements are NOT aspirational -- they are actually LESS hedged than non-AI requirements in 2026 (20% vs 30% hedge fraction), reversing a 2024 pattern where AI was more aspirational (40% vs 34%).

docs.title: The Problem: "Leading" Is Not Management
docs.text:
The single largest contributor to the management indicator rate is the pattern \bleading\b, which matches 33.0% of 2026 entry-level postings. Manual inspection of 1,411 matches reveals:

Usage type Count Share Adjective ("a leading company", "leading-edge technologies") 913 64.7% Management verb ("leading a team") 9 0.6% Ambiguous 489 34.7%

Only 0.6% of "leading" matches reflect management activity. The vast majority describe the company itself ("Dice is the leading career destination") or technology ("leading-edge technologies"). This pattern alone inflates the entry-level rate by approximately 20+ percentage points.

docs.title: Other Problematic Patterns
docs.text: Pattern 2026 entry rate Primary usage \bleading\b 33.0% 99%+ adjective/company description \bcross[-]?functional\b 19.1% 84% collaboration ("work with cross-functional teams"), 0.5% leadership \bleadership\b 11.5% 77% company culture/values, 11% skill desired, 10% technical leadership \bstrategic\b 7.2% Mostly company description ("strategic asset")

docs.title: Individual Pattern Contributions (2026 Entry-Level)
docs.text: Pattern Rate Validity \bleading\b 33.0% INVALID -- almost entirely adjective \bcross[-]?functional\b 19.1% MIXED -- overwhelmingly collaboration, not management \bleadership\b 11.5% MIXED -- mostly company boilerplate or soft skill \bstrategic\b 7.2% MOSTLY INVALID in entry context \bmentor(ing)?\b 6.1% VALID -- genuine scope expectation \bcoach(ing)?\b 3.9% VALID -- genuine scope expectation \bproject\smanagement\b 3.5% VALID \bbudget\b 2.2% VALID but may be about budget objects, not managing budgets \broadmap\b 2.2% VALID \bstakeholder\b 1.9% VALID \bteam\sof\s\d+\b 1.6% VALID but sometimes describes team size, not reports \bprogram\smanagement\b 0.7% VALID \blead\steam\b 0.4% VALID \bdirect\sreports\b 0.1% VALID \bmanage\steam\b / \bmanaging\steam\b 0.1% VALID \bpeople\smanagement\b 0.0% VALID (but near-zero for entry)

docs.title: Corrected Management Rates
docs.text:
Using the strict pattern set (excluding \bleading\b, \bleadership\b, and \bstrategic\b in their unqualified forms; keeping only validated management indicators):

Period Junior (broad) Junior (strict) Senior (broad) Senior (strict) 2024-01 39.2% 24.6% 56.8% 45.0% 2024-04 39.4% 30.1% 51.3% 39.7% 2026-03 61.7% 34.3% 75.6% 55.1%

The junior-to-senior gap also narrows: Under strict patterns, the 2026 gap is 55.1% - 34.3% = 20.8pp (senior higher), compared to the broad set's 75.6% - 61.7% = 13.9pp. This confirms the broad set was disproportionately contaminated at the junior level.

docs.title: 2. Section-Level Analysis
docs.text:
Where do management terms appear in the posting structure?

Section 2026 entry-level 2024 entry-level Requirements/qualifications 25.9% 47.1% Responsibilities 40.8% 39.7% About company 16.3% 1.3% Benefits 14.5% 3.2% Unknown/no headers 45.0% 31.0%

Key insight: In 2026, management terms are much more likely to appear in "about company" (16.3% vs 1.3%) and "benefits" (14.5% vs 3.2%) sections. This suggests a portion of the increase comes from expanded boilerplate/company description sections rather than actual job requirements.

The shift from requirements (47% to 26%) to about-company and benefits sections indicates that 2026 postings are more templated, with management-adjacent language bleeding from company descriptions into the text analysis.

docs.title: 3. Management Term Hedging Context
docs.text:
For management terms that DO appear in 2026 entry-level postings:

Context 2026 share 2024 share Hedged ("nice to have", etc.) 0.7% 1.9% Firm/required ("must have", etc.) 5.2% 9.1% Neutral (no hedge/firm marker) 94.1% 89.0%

The overwhelming majority (94%) of management terms appear without any hedging or requirement framing. They are neither required nor optional -- they simply exist in the description, often as descriptions of the company or environment rather than as job requirements.

docs.title: 4. Text Source Sensitivity
docs.text:
Management rates vary significantly depending on which text column is used:

Text source 2024-01 junior 2024-04 junior 2026-03 junior description (full, with boilerplate) 54.6% 57.8% 63.6% description_core (rule-based cleaned) 43.8% 54.8% 61.7% text (best available, LLM-cleaned for Kaggle) 39.2% 39.4% 61.7%

For 2024, the LLM-cleaned text (description_core_llm, available for Kaggle rows) substantially reduces the management rate compared to description_core, dropping from 54.8% to 39.4% for 2024-04. This confirms that boilerplate removal matters enormously for this metric.

For 2026, most rows fall back to description_core (LLM cleaning deferred for scraped data), so the rates align. When LLM-cleaned text becomes available for 2026 data, the 2026 management rate will likely drop further.

docs.title: 5. AI Ghostiness Test
docs.text:
Are AI requirements more aspirational than traditional requirements?

Period AI hedge fraction Non-AI hedge fraction Interpretation 2024-01 40.2% 34.2% AI slightly more aspirational 2024-04 30.9% 21.9% AI more aspirational 2026-03 20.0% 30.0% AI LESS aspirational

Finding: AI requirements REVERSED from aspirational to firm between 2024 and 2026. In 2024, AI terms were more likely to appear in hedged context (40% vs 34% for non-AI). By 2026, AI terms are less hedged than non-AI terms (20% vs 30%). This suggests AI requirements have transitioned from "nice-to-have" signaling to genuine hiring requirements.

This is evidence AGAINST the hypothesis that AI requirements are purely aspirational.

docs.title: Kitchen-Sink Score (tech count x org scope)
docs.text:
Period Junior mean Senior mean 2024-01 1.2 3.5 2024-04 2.0 3.2 2026-03 3.4 6.6

Kitchen-sink scores increased across both seniority levels, but the senior increase (3.2 to 6.6, +3.4) is larger than junior (+1.4 to +2.2). This reflects growing listing complexity generally, not junior-specific inflation.

docs.title: Aspiration Ratio
docs.text:
Period Junior mean Senior mean 2024-01 0.72 0.54 2024-04 0.63 0.59 2026-03 0.74 0.71

Aspiration ratios rose for both levels in 2026, with junior consistently higher. The gap narrowed from 0.18 (2024-01) to 0.03 (2026-03), suggesting senior postings became more aspirational while junior stayed similar.

docs.title: YOE-Scope Mismatch (Junior Only)
docs.text:
Period N Mismatch rate 2024-01 130 6.2% 2024-04 830 3.0% 2026-03 3255 7.2%

Small but real: 7.2% of 2026 entry-level postings have either YOE >= 5 or >= 3 senior scope terms. This is up from 3.0% in 2024-04 but the 2024-01 baseline (6.2%) makes the trend less clear.

docs.title: 7. Aggregator vs Direct Comparison
docs.text:
Direct junior Aggregator junior Direct senior Aggregator senior Kitchen-sink (2026) 3.3 3.7 6.5 7.2 Mgmt rate (2026) 61.2% 62.7% 75.7% 74.7% AI rate (2026) 54.5% 39.7% 42.6% 29.5%

Aggregator postings are comparable on management indicators and kitchen-sink scores. The biggest difference is AI rates: direct postings have substantially higher AI mention rates (54.5% vs 39.7% for junior, 42.6% vs 29.5% for senior). Aggregator postings are less likely to include AI requirements, possibly because they template-strip or simplify.

docs.title: 8. Template Saturation
docs.text:
Among 1,701 company-period pairs with >= 5 postings:

Metric Value Mean pairwise cosine sim 0.331 Median 0.275 P75 0.415 P90 0.595 Companies > 0.8 78 (4.6%) Companies > 0.6 166 (9.8%)

78 company-period pairs show very high template reuse (sim > 0.8). These are copy-paste operations but represent a small fraction (4.6%) of prolific employers. The median similarity is 0.275, indicating most companies write distinct postings.

docs.title: 9. Industry Variation
docs.text:
Industries with highest kitchen-sink scores (SWE postings, n >= 20):

Industry N Kitchen-sink Mgmt rate AI rate Accounting + IT Services 72 21.5 100% 100% Accounting + Business Consulting 236 20.4 89.8% 86.4% Insurance 339 19.8 80.8% 39.8% Banking + Financial Services 32 16.0 87.5% 37.5%

Financial services and consulting industries have the highest ghost-like characteristics. Insurance is notably high on kitchen-sink score but lower on AI rates.

docs.title: What the Paper Can Claim
docs.text:
1. Moderate scope inflation at entry level is REAL (strict patterns: +4-10pp increase in genuine management/scope indicators like mentoring, coaching, project management, stakeholder work).

  2. The magnitude previously reported (~+31pp) was an artifact of pattern matching that captured adjective uses of "leading", company descriptions containing "leadership", and collaboration language ("cross-functional") rather than management expectations.

  3. AI requirements transitioned from aspirational to required between 2024 and 2026, evidenced by the reversal in hedge fraction (40% to 20%).

docs.title: Required Corrections
docs.text:
1. The management indicator pattern set MUST be revised before formal analysis. Remove \bleading\b entirely (99.4% false positive for management). Add word-boundary disambiguation for \bleadership\b (only count "leadership skills/experience/qualities" in requirements sections). Remove \bstrategic\b unqualified.

  2. Report the corrected rate (strict: 24.6-30.1% to 34.3%, or moderate: 26.2-31.2% to 35.3%) instead of the broad rate.

  3. Cross-functional collaboration should be discussed as a soft-skill/collaboration indicator, not a management indicator. It's real and growing (19.1% at entry level) but represents collaborative expectations, not supervisory scope.

  4. LLM text cleaning of 2026 data should be prioritized. The 2024 data benefits from LLM boilerplate removal that reduces false positives; 2026 data does not. This creates a systematic bias that inflates the 2024-to-2026 delta.

docs.title: Output Files
docs.text:

Figures: - exploration/figures/T22/ghost_indicators_overview.png -- 4-panel overview of ghost indicators - exploration/figures/T22/aggregator_vs_direct_junior.png -- Aggregator comparison - exploration/figures/T22/ai_aspiration_comparison.png -- AI hedge fraction over time - exploration/figures/T22/mgmt_terms_entry_level.png -- Top management terms bar chart
