docs.title: T23: Employer-Requirement / Worker-Usage Divergence
docs.text: Status: Complete Purpose: Compare posting-side AI requirements against worker-side AI usage benchmarks for RQ3.

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
Posting AI requirements lag developer usage rates, but the gap is closing fast. In 2024, only ~12% of SWE postings mentioned any AI requirement while ~57% of developers reported using AI tools -- a ~45pp gap. By 2026, posting requirements surged to ~41% while usage estimates reached ~75% -- a ~34pp gap.

The direction of the gap is posting-BELOW-usage, not above. Employers are catching up to reality, not inflating beyond it. However, there is a critical asymmetry: posting requirements grew 29pp (250% relative) versus usage growing 18pp (32% relative). Requirements are converging toward usage at a rate that, if sustained, would reach parity by late 2027.

One exception: AI-as-domain (ML, DL, NLP) requirements now slightly exceed domain-specialist usage rates (22.7% posting vs ~20% usage), suggesting some overshoot in ML/AI-specific requirements.

docs.title: 1. AI Requirement Rates in SWE Postings
By Category and Period
docs.text:
Category 2024-01 2024-04 2026-03 Change (pp) AI-as-tool (Copilot, LLM, prompt eng.) 1.7% 1.7% 23.3% +21.6 AI-as-domain (ML, DL, NLP, CV) 11.1% 8.5% 22.7% +12.1 AI-general (AI, artificial intelligence) 2.8% 2.7% 17.5% +14.7 Any AI 12.0% 9.7% 40.6% +29.0

The most striking shift is AI-as-tool: from virtually zero (1.7%) to 23.3%. This category barely existed in posting requirements in 2024 and now appears in nearly one-quarter of SWE postings.

docs.title: By Seniority (2026)
docs.text:
Seniority AI-any AI-tool AI-domain Junior 38.2% 18.7% 25.4% Mid 30.2% 20.7% 14.8% Senior 40.3% 24.3% 21.2%

Junior AI-domain rates are notably high (25.4%), higher than senior (21.2%). This is somewhat surprising and may reflect the inclusion of ML Engineer intern/new-grad roles in the entry-level bucket, or it may indicate employers seeking AI/ML capabilities in junior hires who grew up with the technology.

Senior roles have the highest AI-tool rates (24.3%), consistent with the expectation that experienced engineers are being asked to integrate AI coding tools.

docs.title: Specific Tool Mention Rates (%)
docs.text:
Tool 2024-01 2024-04 2026-03 LLM 0.45 1.00 9.87 Copilot 0.04 0.14 3.88 Claude 0.01 0.02 3.89 Prompt engineering 0.19 0.28 3.81 Cursor 0.00 0.00 2.35 ChatGPT 0.33 0.02 0.64 GPT-N 0.06 0.04 0.50

LLM is the dominant AI-tool term at nearly 10% of all SWE postings. Copilot and Claude are nearly tied at ~3.9%, with Cursor close behind at 2.4%. ChatGPT, which was the most-mentioned specific tool in early 2024, has been overtaken by developer-focused tools. This mirrors the shift from consumer AI chatbots to purpose-built developer tools.

docs.title: 2. External Usage Benchmarks
docs.text:
Source Metric Value Year StackOverflow Dev Survey 2024 Currently using AI tools 62% 2024 JetBrains Dev Ecosystem 2024 AI assistant usage 52% 2024 GitHub (Oct 2024) Copilot adoption 35% 2024 Anthropic Economic Index (Feb 2025) SWE tasks with AI augmentation 37% 2025 Industry consensus Developer AI tool adoption ~75% 2026 (est.)

Key nuance: The Anthropic Economic Index measures task-level augmentation (37% of SWE tasks), not person-level adoption. The StackOverflow and JetBrains numbers measure whether a developer uses AI at all (52-62%). GitHub's number measures paid Copilot subscribers specifically.

The 2026 estimate of 75% is an extrapolation based on ~10pp annual growth from the 52-62% 2024 baseline, consistent with industry trajectory.

docs.title: Posting Requirements vs Developer Usage
docs.text:
Year Category Posting rate Usage rate (est.) Gap (pp) Ratio 2024 AI-any 11.6% 57.0% -45.4 0.20 2024 AI-tool 1.7% 35.0% -33.3 0.05 2024 AI-domain 10.6% 15.0% -4.4 0.71 2026 AI-any 40.6% 75.0% -34.4 0.54 2026 AI-tool 23.3% 55.0% -31.7 0.42 2026 AI-domain 22.7% 20.0% +2.7 1.14

Key findings:

  1. AI-any: The gap narrowed from -45pp to -34pp. Posting requirements are closing the gap but still trail usage by a wide margin. Posts express AI requirements at 54% the rate developers actually use AI (up from 20%).

  2. AI-tool: Massive catch-up from near-zero. Posting AI-tool rates were 0.05x usage in 2024 (essentially no postings mentioned coding AI tools despite 35% usage). By 2026, the ratio improved to 0.42x. Still a large gap, but the trajectory is steep.

  3. AI-domain: Near parity or slight overshoot. ML/DL/NLP requirements (22.7%) now slightly exceed the estimated specialist usage rate (~20%). This may indicate some aspirational ML hiring, or it may reflect a genuine expansion of ML-adjacent work in SWE roles.

docs.title: Temporal Divergence
docs.text:
Metric Growth 2024->2026 (pp) Relative growth Posting AI-any rate +29.0 +250% Developer AI usage (est.) +18.0 +32% Ratio (posting/usage growth) 1.6x pp 7.9x relative

Posting requirements are growing approximately 1.6x faster in percentage points and 7.9x faster in relative terms than developer usage. This convergence rate suggests the gap will close to near-parity within 1-2 years if trends continue.

docs.title: 4. Divergence by Specificity
docs.text:
Period Specific tool mentions Generic AI mentions 2024-01 2.9% 12.9% 2024-04 2.7% 14.8% 2026-03 13.9% 53.4%

Generic AI mentions (AI, ML) are far more common than specific tool mentions (Copilot, Cursor, ChatGPT). However, specific mentions grew faster in relative terms (2.9% to 13.9%, 4.8x) than generic (12.9% to 53.4%, 4.1x). This suggests employers are moving from vague AI signaling to concrete tool requirements, though generic language still dominates.

docs.title: 5. Aggregator Sensitivity
docs.text:
Direct postings Aggregator postings 2024 AI-any 9.0-10.3% 5.4-20.3% 2026 AI-any 42.5% 29.5% 2026 AI-tool 24.2% 18.2% 2026 AI-domain 24.3% 13.4%

Direct postings consistently show higher AI requirement rates than aggregator postings. The gap is 13pp for AI-any in 2026 (42.5% vs 29.5%). This makes sense: aggregators strip or simplify job descriptions, losing AI-specific language.

When excluding aggregators, the overall 2026 AI rate rises from 40.6% to 42.5%. The core divergence finding is not aggregator-driven.

The 2024-01 aggregator anomaly (20.3% AI-any) comes from a smaller, potentially unrepresentative aggregator subsample.

docs.title: What RQ3 Can Claim
docs.text:
1. Posting AI requirements dramatically lag actual developer AI usage. In 2026, ~41% of SWE postings mention AI while an estimated ~75% of developers use AI tools. The gap is narrowing but still substantial.

  2. The catch-up is primarily in AI-as-tool. Requirements for coding-specific AI tools (Copilot, LLM, prompt engineering) went from near-zero to 23%. This is the fastest-growing category and most directly comparable to the usage benchmarks.

  3. AI-as-domain requirements may have overshot actual specialist rates. At 22.7% of SWE postings requiring ML/DL/NLP, the posting rate exceeds the estimated ~20% of developers who are actual ML specialists. This may reflect genuine expansion of ML-adjacent work or some aspirational hiring.

  4. The shift from aspirational to firm AI language (from T22) reinforces that posting AI requirements are becoming operational, not performative.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Usage benchmarks come from surveys with different populations and definitions. Direct comparison requires careful framing.
  • The 2026 usage estimate (75%) is extrapolated, not directly observed.
  • Posting rates measure whether AI is mentioned at all, not the weight or centrality of the AI requirement.
  • The "AI-any" rate of 53.4% for generic mentions includes \bai\b which matches many false positives (abbreviations, etc.). The structured categories (tool: 23.3%, domain: 22.7%) are more reliable.

docs.title: Output Files
docs.text:
Tables: - exploration/tables/T23/ai_requirement_rates.csv -- AI rates by period x seniority - exploration/tables/T23/specific_tool_rates.csv -- Individual tool mention rates - exploration/tables/T23/external_benchmarks.csv -- External usage benchmarks - exploration/tables/T23/requirement_usage_divergence.csv -- Divergence computation - exploration/tables/T23/ai_rates_aggregator_sensitivity.csv -- Aggregator sensitivity - exploration/tables/T23/ai_specificity_rates.csv -- Specific vs generic rates

Figures: - exploration/figures/T23/ai_requirement_rates.png -- 3-panel AI rates by seniority - exploration/figures/T23/requirement_vs_usage_divergence.png -- Posting vs usage comparison - exploration/figures/T23/temporal_growth_comparison.png -- Growth rate comparison - exploration/figures/T23/specific_tool_rates.png -- Individual tool trends

Scripts: - exploration/scripts/T23_divergence.py

docs.title: T24: Hypothesis Generation from Exploration Findings
docs.text: Status: Complete Agent: N (Wave 4) Date: 2026-04-05

docs.title: 1. Confirmation Inventory: RQ1-RQ4 Hypotheses vs Evidence
RQ1: Employer-side restructuring
docs.text: Hypothesis Status Evidence Caveats Junior share declined Confirmed 5.9-8.3pp decline (T03); within-company -11.8pp (T06); SWE-specific DiD = -24.9pp (T18) Direction depends on seniority operationalization in overlap panel (T16 surprise); asaniczka cannot participate Scope inflation of remaining junior roles Partially confirmed, partially corrected AI requirements +24pp at entry (T11), tech count +26% (T11), strategy/roadmap +7pp SWE-specific DiD (T18) Management indicator corrected from +31pp to +4-10pp (T22); management expansion is field-wide (T18 DiD ~0); soft skills grew LESS in SWE than control (DiD -5.1pp) Senior roles redefined toward orchestration Confirmed Technical orchestration density +16% mid-senior, +46% director (T21); management density -23% at director; AI-orchestrator sub-archetype emerging Small director sample (n=113 in 2024); not yet tested via cross-occupation DiD AI caused junior elimination Contradicted at firm level AI adoption and entry-share change are orthogonal: r=-0.07 firm (T16), r=-0.04 metro (T17) Aggregate co-occurrence remains; within-firm null may reflect power constraints or time lag

docs.title: RQ2: Task and requirement migration
docs.text: Hypothesis Status Evidence Caveats Management migrated from senior to junior Rejected Management expanded at ALL levels (T21); entry +31pp, mid-senior +27pp, director +7pp (narrow metric) Corrected management indicator reduces magnitude; but the universal expansion pattern is robust Requirements moved between seniority levels Partially confirmed Strategy/roadmap language is SWE-specific (DiD +7pp); AI requirements entered entry-level Domain recomposition (T09) is 10x more structurally important than seniority-level migration Junior-senior semantic convergence Fails calibration T15: within-2024 variation exceeds cross-period change; 7/9 findings repr-robust but convergence claim not

docs.title: RQ3: Employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence
docs.text: Hypothesis Status Evidence Caveats Posting requirements outpace developer usage Inverted Requirements LAG usage: ~41% posting vs ~75% developer usage (T23); gap narrowed from -45pp to -34pp Usage benchmarks come from surveys with self-selection bias; 75% is extrapolated AI requirements are aspirational/signaling Rejected AI terms are LESS hedged than traditional requirements in 2026 (20% vs 30% hedge fraction; T22); reversed from 2024 where AI was more aspirational AI-as-domain (22.7%) may slightly overshoot specialist usage (~20%)

docs.title: RQ4: Mechanisms (qualitative)
docs.text: Not tested in exploration. The orthogonality finding, the requirements-lag-usage inversion, and the corrected management indicator are the three most important interview prompts.

docs.title: Tier 1: Strongly evidenced surprises that reshape the paper
docs.text: # Surprise Evidence strength Implication S1 AI-entry orthogonality (r ~ 0) Strong (T16 firm, T17 metro) Cannot frame as "AI replaces juniors" within firms; parallel market trends S2 Management indicator was measurement error Strong (T22 pattern decomposition) Paper must use corrected +4-10pp, not +31pp S3 Requirements LAG usage, not lead Strong (T23 divergence) Inverts RQ3 direction; employers are catching up, not inflating beyond reality S4 Control juniors INCREASED while SWE juniors decreased Strong (T18 DiD) Junior compression is SWE-specific, not a general labor market trend S5 57% of aggregate change is compositional Strong (T16 decomposition) Most of what we observe is different companies posting, not same companies changing

docs.title: Tier 2: Moderate surprises with important implications
docs.text: # Surprise Evidence strength Implication S6 Domain recomposition is 10x more important than seniority Strong (T09 NMI) Paper should lead with domain axis, not seniority axis S7 YOE slot purification (5+ YOE entry: 22.8% to 2.4%) Strong (T08, 5 decompositions) 2024 entry-level was contaminated; "entry-level" means something different now S8 GenAI accelerated 8.3x between within-2024 and cross-period rates Moderate (T19; 3-point estimation) Consistent with model release cascade; temporal alignment is suggestive S9 Director management density FELL 23% Moderate (T21; small n=113 in 2024) Director role pivoting from people management to technical orchestration S10 Agile/Scrum language declined 5pp Moderate (T12, T14) Methodology era may be ending; unexpected given industry practice

docs.title: Tier 3: Tentative surprises needing further investigation
docs.text: # Surprise Evidence strength Implication S11 Associate roles collapsing toward entry (relative position 0.30 to 0.16) Moderate (T20) Effective 5-level hierarchy compressing to ~3 tiers S12 Architect titles collapsed from 1.8% to 0.25% Moderate (T10) Role may be absorbed into staff/principal IC track S13 Florida metros lead AI adoption Moderate (T17) Newer tech markets may adopt AI requirements faster (less legacy inertia) S14 Entry-level readability IMPROVED (FK grade 16.5 to 15.3) Moderate (T13) Counter to "more complex roles" prediction; suggests clearer writing

docs.title: 3. New Hypothesis Generation
docs.text: The following hypotheses are testable, NOT part of the original research design, and account for the orthogonality finding (they do not assume AI caused junior elimination).

docs.title: H1: Domain recomposition as the primary restructuring mechanism
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The decline in junior SWE share is primarily driven by the growth of ML/AI engineering (which has a structurally lower entry-level posting rate due to higher skill floors) rather than by within-domain junior elimination.

Test: Decompose the junior share decline into within-domain (holding archetype constant) and between-domain (archetype composition shift) components. If ML/AI has lower entry share AND grew from 4% to 27%, the compositional effect alone could account for a substantial portion of the 5.9pp aggregate decline.

Data needed: Cross-tabulation of seniority by T09 archetype by period. Available in existing data.

Why it matters: If confirmed, the "junior decline" story becomes "the market shifted toward domains with inherently higher skill floors" rather than "employers stopped hiring juniors." This is a more nuanced and defensible claim.

docs.title: H2: AI requirements as labor market signaling (independent of actual job content)
docs.text:
Hypothesis: Firms add AI requirements to postings not because the role involves AI work, but to signal organizational modernity and attract candidates who self-select as technologically current. Evidence: AI adoption at the firm level is uncorrelated with junior hiring changes, but firms in competitive labor markets (where signaling matters more) show higher AI requirement rates.

Test: Compare AI requirement rates controlling for firm characteristics (size, industry, prior AI adoption). Test whether the signaling interpretation survives when controlling for actual AI technology use (e.g., companies with AI products vs companies adding AI to non-AI postings).

Data needed: Company-level AI product indicators (partially derivable from industry labels); firm-size data (limited to arshkon). Could proxy via posting characteristics.

docs.title: H3: Template modernization as the driver of field-wide management language expansion
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The universal expansion of management/mentoring/coaching/leadership language across all seniority levels and occupations (including control) is driven by changes in HR technology platforms (e.g., AI-powered job posting generators, compliance templates) rather than by actual changes in job scope.

Test: Examine template saturation (T22: 4.6% of company-periods have pairwise cosine similarity >0.8). If the management language increase is concentrated in template-heavy companies, this supports the template hypothesis. If management language grew even in companies with low template reuse, the hypothesis weakens.

Data needed: Company-level template saturation scores crossed with management language rates. Available in existing data.

docs.title: H4: The "two-speed market" -- AI-native firms vs legacy transformation
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The SWE labor market is bifurcating into (a) AI-native companies that were founded during the AI era and build AI into roles from inception, and (b) legacy companies transforming existing roles to incorporate AI. These two populations drive different components of the aggregate change: AI-natives drive compositional effects (57% of change); legacy companies drive within-firm effects (43% of change).

Test: Classify companies in the T16 panel as "AI-native" (high AI prevalence in both periods or new-entrant with high AI) vs "AI-transforming" (low-to-high AI trajectory) vs "traditional" (low AI in both). Compare entry-share changes across these types.

Data needed: Company-level AI prevalence trajectory from T16 panel. Available in existing data.

docs.title: H5: Slot purification reflects credentialism retreat, not AI-driven restructuring
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The elimination of high-YOE "entry-level" postings (22.8% to 2.4%) reflects a broader retreat from experience-based credentialism in SWE hiring, driven by: (a) LinkedIn platform changes making seniority labels more accurate, (b) legal/compliance pressure against misleading job level labels, or (c) skill-based hiring trends. This would be unrelated to AI adoption.

Test: Check whether YOE slot purification also occurred in control occupations and SWE-adjacent roles. If yes, it is field-wide and likely platform-driven. If SWE-specific, the AI link is more plausible.

Data needed: Entry-level YOE distributions for control occupations across periods. Partially available (control has limited entry-level seniority labels).

docs.title: H6: The AI-entry orthogonality resolves through a time-lag mechanism
docs.text:
Hypothesis: AI adoption and junior hiring reduction ARE causally linked, but the effect operates with a multi-quarter lag that our two-snapshot design cannot capture. Firms first add AI requirements to existing postings (observed), then restructure their seniority mix in subsequent hiring cycles (not yet observable in cross-sectional snapshots).

Test: Not directly testable with current data (two snapshots, no longitudinal tracking). However, could look for leading indicators: among T16 panel firms that adopted AI between periods, do those with the HIGHEST AI adoption in 2026 show slightly lower entry share, even if the correlation is not significant? A dose-response pattern (even weak) would be suggestive.

Data needed: Existing T16 panel data with finer binning of AI adoption intensity.

docs.title: H7: GenAI adoption as a domain-boundary dissolving force
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The growth of GenAI requirements is blurring the boundaries between T09 archetypes -- postings in traditionally separate domains (frontend, embedded, data) increasingly share GenAI vocabulary, creating convergence in posting language at the domain level even as domain remains the primary structural axis.

Test: Compare the technology co-occurrence structure (T14 networks) between AI-mentioning and non-AI-mentioning postings within each domain. If AI-mentioning postings show higher cross-domain similarity, GenAI is acting as a convergence force.

Data needed: Embeddings stratified by AI-mention and domain. Available in existing data.

docs.title: H8: "Staff engineer" growth reflects AI-driven organizational flattening
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The near-tripling of "staff" title markers (2.4% to 6.6%, T10) reflects organizational flattening where firms remove mid-management layers and create high-seniority IC tracks. AI tools that enable senior ICs to manage larger scope drive this. The architect-to-staff title migration (-1.6pp architect, +4.9pp staff) is the same phenomenon.

Test: Check whether companies that increased "staff" titles also decreased "architect" titles. Cross-reference with AI adoption levels.

Data needed: Company-level title analysis crossed with AI adoption. Partially available.

docs.title: RQ1: SWE labor demand restructuring
docs.text: Method Suitability Rationale Repeated cross-section DiD (SWE vs control) High Already validated in T18; strong identification Within-firm panel (451 overlap companies) High for aggregate; Low for firm-level Powers aggregate effects; individual firm analysis underpowered Shift-share decomposition High T16 demonstrates feasibility; 57% compositional finding is central Domain-stratified analysis High (recommended) T09 shows domain is 10x more important than seniority; must stratify Interrupted time series Low Only 3 time points; no within-period variation for Kaggle data Causal mediation (AI -> entry) Not supported Orthogonality finding (r ~ 0) eliminates simple mediation

docs.title: RQ2: Technology ecosystem evolution
docs.text: Method Suitability Rationale Technology co-occurrence network analysis High T14 demonstrates feasibility; Louvain community detection works NLP-based requirement extraction Moderate Rule-based (description_core) ~44% accuracy; LLM-cleaned only for Kaggle Skill taxonomy alignment Low No cross-period structured skills data

docs.title: RQ3: Employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence
docs.text: Method Suitability Rationale Benchmark comparison (posting vs survey) Moderate External benchmarks have different sampling frames Within-posting aspiration analysis High T22's hedge-fraction method is validated Specificity gradient (generic vs tool-specific) High T23 demonstrates feasibility

docs.title: RQ4: Mechanisms (qualitative)
docs.text: Method Suitability Rationale Semi-structured interviews with hiring managers High Orthogonality puzzle is a strong elicitation stimulus JD-stimulated recall High T25 will produce paired JD artifacts Reflexive thematic analysis High Standard qualitative method; suited to mechanism questions

docs.title: 5. Key Tensions: The Five Most Important Puzzles
Tension 1: The seniority operationalization discrepancy
docs.text:
The problem: The direction of the entry-level trend depends on which seniority column is used. Using seniority_native (arshkon-only as 2024 baseline), entry share declined from ~20-22% to ~14% (T03). Using seniority_3level across the full T16 overlap panel, entry share appeared to increase. T16 noted this surprise but did not fully resolve it.

Why it matters: If the direction of the headline finding depends on the column choice, the claim is fragile. The planned seniority_llm labels are the resolution mechanism -- they will provide a consistent, high-quality seniority classification across all sources.

Resolution path: (a) Clearly document the ablation framework in all analysis: seniority_llm (when available) > seniority_native > seniority_final > seniority_imputed. (b) Run all entry-level analyses with seniority_native (cleanest, arshkon-only baseline) as primary, and seniority_final as robustness. (c) Flag explicitly that seniority_llm will resolve the discrepancy.

docs.title: Tension 2: The orthogonality puzzle
docs.text:
The problem: At the aggregate level, AI requirements surged AND junior share declined -- the co-occurrence is striking and tempting to present causally. But at the firm level (r=-0.07) and metro level (r=-0.04), the correlation is null. How can two trends co-occur at the market level but be orthogonal within units?

Candidate explanations: (a) Both trends are driven by a common third factor (e.g., post-pandemic labor market normalization, macro hiring slowdown). (b) The relationship operates through market-level equilibrium effects (AI adoption changes what skills are valued, which shifts the type of companies that hire, which changes the composition). (c) The within-firm null reflects insufficient time for the causal mechanism to operate (lag hypothesis). (d) The trends are genuinely independent -- AI adoption is about what companies ask for, junior decline is about how many they ask for, and these are separate decisions.

Why it matters: The paper's credibility depends on NOT overclaiming causation. The orthogonality finding is one of the most important results and must be prominently presented.

docs.title: Tension 3: Compositional vs behavioral change
docs.text:
The problem: 57% of aggregate change is compositional (different companies posting). This means the "restructuring" story is more about market-level turnover than firm-level strategic decisions.

Why it matters: "Companies are restructuring SWE hiring" implies behavioral change. "Different companies are hiring" is a compositional story. The paper should present both components clearly.

Resolution path: Frame the contribution as documenting BOTH within-firm behavioral changes (~43%) AND market-level compositional shifts (~57%). The fact that 43% is genuine within-firm change is still substantial and meaningful.

docs.title: Tension 4: Description quality asymmetry
docs.text:
The problem: 2024 Kaggle data benefits from LLM-cleaned text (description_core_llm, ~80-99% coverage for SWE). 2026 scraped data has 0% LLM-cleaned coverage. All text-based indicators operate on lower-quality rule-based cleaned text for 2026. T22 showed this matters enormously for management indicators (LLM-cleaned 2024 rates are ~15pp lower than rule-based rates).

Why it matters: Every text-based 2024-to-2026 comparison has a systematic quality bias. The 2024 rates are measured on cleaner text; the 2026 rates on noisier text. This asymmetry inflates apparent 2024-to-2026 changes for boilerplate-sensitive indicators.

Resolution path: (a) Use binary keyword presence on raw description for prevalence measures (less sensitive to cleaning quality). (b) When LLM-cleaned 2026 text becomes available, re-run all text-based analyses. (c) Explicitly flag this asymmetry as a limitation.

docs.title: Tension 5: What "SWE-specific" means when SWE and SWE-adjacent move in lockstep
docs.text:
The problem: The DiD analysis (T18) shows SWE and SWE-adjacent move together (DiD between them ~0 for most metrics). The real boundary is technical roles (SWE + adjacent) vs non-technical control. So "SWE-specific" actually means "technical-role-specific."

Why it matters: The paper's framing is about SWE. But the data shows the changes apply broadly to technical roles. This is actually a stronger finding (larger affected population) but requires honest framing.

docs.title: What we would need to answer the most interesting unanswered questions
docs.text: Question Data gap Feasibility Is the AI-entry relationship truly null or delayed? Longitudinal firm-level data (multiple time points) Low -- would need quarterly scrapes over 1-2 years Is slot purification platform-driven or employer-driven? LinkedIn platform policy changes re: seniority labels Low -- would need LinkedIn internal data Are AI requirements real hiring bars or signaling? Interview data matching postings to actual hires Medium -- planned for RQ4 interviews Does domain recomposition explain the junior decline? Domain-stratified entry share (within-archetype) High -- available now with existing data How do practitioners experience the orthogonality? Semi-structured interviews Medium -- planned for RQ4 What does the 2026 management indicator look like with LLM-cleaned text? LLM cleaning of scraped data High -- planned pipeline improvement Does seniority_llm resolve the operationalization discrepancy? Stage 10 LLM budget allocation High -- planned pipeline improvement Are AI-mentioning postings from firms with AI products? Firm-level product data Low -- no such data in our pipeline What happened between April 2024 and March 2026? Monthly or quarterly posting data Low -- would need continuous scraping from 2024 Do posting changes predict actual hiring outcomes? Matched posting-to-hire data Very low -- not available

docs.title: Priority data improvements
docs.text:
1. seniority_llm labels (Stage 10 budget): Resolves seniority operationalization discrepancy. Highest priority.
  2. description_core_llm for scraped data (Stage 9 budget): Resolves text quality asymmetry. Reduces all text-based indicator biases.
  3. Domain-stratified entry share decomposition: Testable immediately with existing data. Should be first analysis task.

docs.title: 7. Summary: The Strongest Story the Data Supports
docs.text:
The exploration produced a richer and more honest story than the initial hypotheses anticipated. The strongest narrative, accounting for all corrections:

The SWE labor market between 2024 and 2026 restructured along three axes:

  1. AI skill requirements surged -- genuinely, not aspirationally -- and this is the defining SWE-specific change (DiD +24pp vs control). Requirements still lag developer usage but are converging fast.

  2. Junior SWE hiring contracted in a SWE-specific manner (control junior share increased). But this contraction is NOT caused by AI adoption within firms (r ~ 0). It co-occurs with AI adoption as parallel market-level trends, mediated primarily by compositional shifts (57% of aggregate change reflects different companies hiring).

  3. The SWE domain landscape recomposed -- ML/AI engineering grew from niche (4%) to major category (27%), frontend contracted, and AI skills became additive to existing technology stacks rather than replacing them.

What the paper should NOT claim: - That AI caused junior elimination (firm-level evidence contradicts) - That management scope inflation is dramatic or SWE-specific (corrected to +4-10pp, field-wide) - That junior and senior postings are converging semantically (fails calibration) - That requirements outpace developer usage (they lag)

docs.title: Output Files
docs.text: • exploration/reports/T24.md -- This report

docs.title: T25: Interview Elicitation Artifacts
docs.text: Status: Complete Agent: N (Wave 4) Date: 2026-04-05

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Produced six interview artifacts for RQ4 semi-structured interviews, all using corrected findings from the full exploration pipeline (T01-T23). The artifacts are designed to elicit practitioner perspectives on three core puzzles: (1) the AI-entry orthogonality finding, (2) whether AI requirements are real hiring bars or signaling, and (3) how practitioners experience the domain recomposition from frontend/web toward ML/AI engineering.

docs.title: 1. Entry-Level JD Examples (Tables)
docs.text:
Files: - exploration/tables/T25/entry_2026_scope_jds.csv (20 rows) - exploration/tables/T25/entry_2024_contrast_jds.csv (20 rows)

2026 entry-level postings selected for validated scope expansion: mentor, coach, project management, or stakeholder terms present; YOE <= 3; non-aggregator. 2024 postings from arshkon for temporal contrast. Each row includes company name, title, YOE, and binary scope indicator flags.

Companies represented in 2026 scope examples: Accenture (AI Native Software Engineer, YOE 1), Bosch (Rotational Development Program, YOE 2), Transamerica (Intermediate Software Engineer, YOE 1), CGI (Data Engineer, YOE 2), Medtronic (Reliability Engineer II, YOE 2), among others.

docs.title: 2. Company Panel for Paired JD Comparisons (Table)
docs.text:
File: exploration/tables/T25/company_ai_entry_orthogonality.csv (463 companies)

Each row shows a company with >= 3 SWE postings in both 2024 and 2026, with AI prevalence change and entry-share change. Enables selection of paired JDs illustrating: - AI-forward: Oracle (0% to 100% AI), Deloitte (0% to 94%), KPMG (0% to 97%) - Traditional hold: Google (96% to 96%, already saturated), Boeing (13% to 11%), Northrop Grumman (1% to 2%) - Entry expanders: Epic (0% to 99% AI AND 0% to 100% entry -- dramatic dual shift), Cisco (40% to 100% AI, 0% to 61% entry)

docs.title: 3. Junior-Share Trend Visualization
docs.text:
File: exploration/figures/T25/junior_share_trend.png

Timeline from April 2024 to March 2026 showing entry-level share declining from 22.3% to 14.0% (seniority_native). Annotated with 6 major AI model releases. Includes prominent caveat box noting the seniority operationalization dependence and that LLM seniority labels will resolve the discrepancy.

Data points: Arshkon (n=769 entry / 3,444 known) at April 2024; Scraped (n=2,789 entry / 19,896 known) at March 2026.

docs.title: 4. Senior Archetype Chart
docs.text:
File: exploration/figures/T25/senior_archetype_chart.png

Two-panel figure: - Left: Management density by seniority level (2024 vs 2026). Shows universal expansion at entry/associate/mid-senior and -23% decrease at director. Highlights the corrected finding that management language expansion is field-wide, not SWE-specific. - Right: Senior sub-archetype shares from T21 clustering. Technical Orchestrator grew +29% relative (4.5% to 5.8%), Strategic Leader declined -20% relative (16.8% to 13.5%). People Manager grew modestly (+2.5pp). Low-touch Generalist stable at ~58%.

docs.title: 5. AI Divergence Chart
docs.text:
File: exploration/figures/T25/ai_divergence_chart.png

Grouped bar chart comparing posting AI requirement rates vs estimated developer usage rates, by category (AI-any, AI-tool, AI-domain) in 2024 and 2026. Key visual message: posting bars are consistently below usage bars. AI-as-domain (22.7% posting vs ~20% usage) is the one exception where posting may slightly overshoot.

Gap annotations: AI-any narrowed from -45pp to -34pp; AI-tool narrowed from -33pp to -32pp; AI-domain went from -4pp to +3pp (possible overshoot).

docs.title: 6. AI-Entry Orthogonality Scatter
docs.text:
File: exploration/figures/T25/ai_entry_orthogonality.png

Company-level scatter of AI prevalence change vs entry-level share change for 463 overlap companies. Shows a flat regression line with r = -0.019 (p = 0.683). Includes explanation box noting the metro-level null result (r = -0.04) and framing the key message: "Companies that adopted AI did NOT cut junior roles."

docs.title: Priority Elicitation Order
docs.text:
1. Start with the orthogonality scatter (Artifact 6). This is the most counterintuitive finding and generates the richest discussion. Frame: "We expected companies adopting AI would hire fewer juniors. They don't. What explains this?"

  2. Follow with junior-share trend (Artifact 3). Establish the macro context: junior SWE postings declined while AI models proliferated. Use the model release annotations as temporal anchors.

  3. Show the AI divergence chart (Artifact 5). Probe: "Posting requirements lag actual usage. Is this your experience? Are AI skills assumed? Or are they genuinely not required for some roles?"

  4. Present the senior archetype chart (Artifact 4). Probe director role evolution: "Directors are using less people-management language but more technical orchestration language. Is this a real change in the role, or a posting-language artifact?"

  5. Close with paired JDs (Artifacts 1-2). Ground the discussion in specific examples. Select pairs relevant to the interviewee's company/domain.

docs.title: Probes for Each Artifact
docs.text: Detailed probes are in exploration/artifacts/README.md.

docs.title: Caveats for Interview Use
docs.text:
1. Management indicator is corrected. Use +4-10pp (strict validated patterns), not +31pp from T11's uncorrected broad patterns. The broad patterns captured "a leading company" and "cross-functional collaboration" as management indicators, which they are not.

  2. Entry-level direction is operationalization-dependent. Present the decline (seniority_native: 22.3% to 14.0%) as the primary estimate but note the caveat. Do not present as settled fact until LLM seniority labels are available.

  3. Usage benchmarks are estimates. The 75% developer AI usage is extrapolated from 2024 surveys. Present as approximate, not precise.

  4. 57% of aggregate change is compositional. When discussing company-level findings, note that the aggregate trends reflect different companies posting, not just the same companies changing behavior.

docs.title: T26: Exploration Synthesis
docs.text: Status: Complete Agent: N (Wave 4) Date: 2026-04-05

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The exploration synthesis consolidates findings from 23 task reports (T01-T25), 4 gate memos, and the INDEX into a single handoff document for the analysis phase.

The full synthesis is at: exploration/reports/SYNTHESIS.md

This report briefly summarizes what the synthesis covers and its key structural decisions.

docs.title: What the Synthesis Covers
docs.text:
1. Data quality verdict per RQ -- RQ1 through RQ4, with explicit quality ratings and caveats for each analytical dimension.

  2. Recommended analytical samples -- SQL templates for primary SWE, entry-level, cross-occupation DiD, and company overlap panel samples.

  3. Seniority column recommendation -- Full ablation framework: seniority_llm (when available) > seniority_native > seniority_final. Explicit documentation of the operationalization discrepancy and why it matters.

  4. Known confounders -- 4 HIGH, 4 MODERATE, 3 LOW severity confounders with affected analyses and mitigation strategies.

  5. Discovery findings -- Organized as Confirmed (6 findings), Corrected (2 findings), Contradicted (3 hypotheses), and New (5 discoveries).

  6. Domain summaries from T09 (posting archetypes), T14 (technology evolution), T17 (geographic structure), T21 (senior archetypes), T22 (ghost/aspirational assessment).

  7. New hypotheses from T24, with H1 (domain recomposition drives junior decline) flagged as most testable with existing data.

  8. Method recommendations -- Primary and secondary methods for each RQ; recommended sensitivity framework (5 dimensions).

  9. Sensitivity requirements -- Which findings need formal robustness checks, and which specifications to run.

  10. Pending data improvements -- seniority_llm (highest priority) and description_core_llm for scraped data.

  11. Interview priorities -- Three themes: orthogonality puzzle, AI requirements as hiring bars vs signaling, domain recomposition experience.

docs.title: Key Structural Decisions in the Synthesis
1. Honest about corrections
docs.text: The synthesis prominently documents that the management indicator was corrected from +31pp to +4-10pp (T22), that soft skills expansion is NOT SWE-specific (T18), and that semantic convergence fails calibration (T15). These corrections are not buried -- they appear in Section 5 (Discovery Findings) with explicit "Corrected" and "Contradicted" labels.

docs.title: 2. Orthogonality finding is core, not caveat
docs.text: The AI-entry orthogonality (r ~ 0 at firm and metro level) is presented as a core finding, not a footnote. The synthesis explicitly states what the paper CANNOT claim (AI caused junior elimination within firms) alongside what it CAN claim (parallel market trends, SWE-specific via DiD).

docs.title: 3. Pending data improvements are promises, not excuses
docs.text: The synthesis documents that seniority_llm and description_core_llm for scraped data will change specific findings when available, with concrete predictions about direction of change.

docs.title: 4. The 57% compositional finding shapes framing
docs.text: The synthesis recommends the analysis phase frame its contribution as documenting BOTH within-firm behavioral changes (~43%) AND market-level compositional shifts (~57%), not just one or the other.
